## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Explain sp² hybridization in carbon with a suitable example.
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In sp² hybridization, **one 2s orbital** and **two 2p orbitals** (e.g., 2p_x, 2p_y) of the carbon atom mix to form **three equivalent sp² hybrid orbitals**, leaving **one unhybridized p_z orbital** perpendicular to the plane. The three sp² hybrids lie in a plane at **120°** to one another and form **three σ (sigma) bonds**. The unhybridized p_z orbital forms a **π bond** with a neighboring atom. **Example:** In **graphene**, each carbon uses three sp² orbitals to form σ bonds with three neighbors, while the p_z orbitals overlap to create a **delocalized π-electron cloud** above and below the plane, giving graphene its semi-metallic conductivity.

*(Mark split: 1 mark hybridization definition, 1 mark geometry/angle, 1 mark example with property link.)*

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Distinguish between σ (sigma) and π (pi) bonds in carbon materials.
**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | **σ Bond** | **π Bond** |
|---|---|---|
| Formation | Head-on overlap of hybrid orbitals | Lateral/sideways overlap of unhybridized p orbitals |
| Strength | Stronger | Weaker |
| Rotation | Allows free rotation around the bond axis | Restricts rotation |
| Number in diamond | 4 per C | 0 |
| Number in graphene | 3 per C (in-plane) | 1 delocalized per C |
| Number in carbyne | 1 (per internal C–C link) | 2 (per C≡C triple bond) |

*(Mark split: 1 mark definition of each, 1 mark comparative table, 1 mark carbon-material example.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module – Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a)** With neat diagrams, describe the three types of hybridization (sp³, sp², sp) in carbon. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**sp³ Hybridization (2 marks):** Carbon promotes one 2s electron to 2p_z, then mixes 2s with all three 2p orbitals to form **four equivalent sp³ hybrids** oriented tetrahedrally with **bond angle 109.5°**. All four are σ-bonding. **Example:** Diamond, where every C bonds to four neighbors in a 3D cubic network (zinc-blende structure). No π electrons → insulator.

**sp² Hybridization (2.5 marks):** One 2s + two 2p (e.g., 2p_x, 2p_y) → **three sp² hybrids** at **120°** in a plane + **one unhybridized p_z** perpendicular to the plane. The p_z orbitals overlap sideways to form a **delocalized π cloud**. **Example:** **Graphene** — a 2D honeycomb sheet where each C forms three σ bonds + contributes one electron to the π system. The π electrons make graphene a semi-metal with **zero band gap at Dirac points**.

**sp Hybridization (2.5 marks):** One 2s + one 2p (e.g., 2p_x) → **two sp hybrids** at **180°** + two unhybridized p_y, p_z perpendicular. Each sp hybrid forms a σ bond; the two p orbitals form **two π bonds** (triple bond C≡C). **Example:** **Carbyne** (α and β forms). Bond order = 3; bond length shortest (~1.20 Å); highest bond energy (~839 kJ/mol).

---

**(b)** Compare the properties of diamond, graphite, and graphene in terms of bonding, structure, and electrical behavior. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| Property | **Diamond** | **Graphite** | **Graphene** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hybridization | sp³ | sp² | sp² |
| Bonding | 4 σ per C; no π | 3 σ + 1 delocalized π per C in-plane; weak van der Waals between layers | 3 σ + 1 delocalized π per C; monoatomic layer |
| C–C Bond Length | 1.54 Å | 1.42 Å | 1.42 Å |
| Structure | 3D tetrahedral network | 2D stacked layers (3.35 Å apart) | 2D single monolayer |
| Electrical | Insulator (Eg = 5.5 eV) | Semi-metal (in-plane) | Zero-gap semi-metal (Dirac cones) |
| Hardness | 10 Mohs (hardest natural) | 1–2 Mohs (lubricating) | Strongest measured monolayer (~130 GPa) |
| Thermal Cond. | ~900 W/m·K | ~2000 W/m·K (in-plane) | ~5000 W/m·K |
| Application | Cutting, heat sinks | Electrodes, lubricants | Electronics, sensors, composites |

*(Mark split: 2 marks bonding, 2 marks structure, 2 marks electrical, 1 mark application.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a)** Derive the bond angle for sp³ hybridized carbon. Show that $\cos\theta = -\frac{1}{3}$ and hence $\theta \approx 109.5°$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Carbon has four valence orbitals (2s, 2p_x, 2p_y, 2p_z). Upon promotion, these combine to form four equivalent sp³ hybrids. By symmetry, they must point to the corners of a **regular tetrahedron**.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Place the nucleus at the origin. The four normalized direction vectors toward tetrahedral vertices are:

$$
\hat{v}_1 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(1,1,1),\quad
\hat{v}_2 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(1,-1,-1),\quad
\hat{v}_3 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(-1,1,-1),\quad
\hat{v}_4 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(-1,-1,1)
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the dot product of any two adjacent vectors:

$$
\hat{v}_1 \cdot \hat{v}_2 = \tfrac{1}{3}\left[(1)(1) + (1)(-1) + (1)(-1)\right] = \tfrac{1}{3}(-1) = -\tfrac{1}{3}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** By definition:

$$
\hat{v}_1 \cdot \hat{v}_2 = \cos\theta \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \cos\theta = -\tfrac{1}{3}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\theta = \cos^{-1}(-\tfrac{1}{3}) \approx 109.47^\circ\;}
$$

This is the **tetrahedral angle** in diamond, methane, and all sp³ carbon systems.

---

**(b)** Explain the s-character rule and calculate the % s-character for sp³, sp², and sp hybridizations. Use it to justify the observed C–C bond-length trend: sp > sp² > sp³. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**The s-Character Rule (3 marks):** A hybrid orbital of the form sp^n has s-character given by:

$$
\text{\% s-character} = \frac{1}{n+1}\times 100\%
$$

Since **2s orbitals are closer to the nucleus** (no radial node, lower energy) than 2p orbitals, a hybrid with **higher s-character holds its electrons closer to the nucleus**. This causes:
1. **Shorter bond length** (electrons pull bonding partners inward).
2. **Stronger, more directional bond** (better overlap with neighboring s-rich orbital).

**Calculations (2 marks):**

| Hybrid | n | s-character = 1/(n+1) × 100% |
|---|---|---|
| sp³ | 3 | **25.0%** |
| sp² | 2 | **33.3%** |
| sp | 1 | **50.0%** |

**Bond-Length Justification (2 marks):** As s-character increases (sp³ → sp² → sp), the hybrid orbitals become more compact and closer to the carbon nucleus. Hence, bonded atoms are pulled closer, giving:

$$
d_{sp^3} = 1.54\ \text{Å} \;>\; d_{sp^2} = 1.42\ \text{Å} \;>\; d_{sp} = 1.20\ \text{Å}
$$

This is fully consistent with **Pauling's empirical formula** $d(n) = 1.54 - 0.71\log_{10}(n)$ (derived in §3.2).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-Mark Pitfalls in KTU Board Evaluation:**
> 1. **Drawing sp² diagrams without the unhybridized p_z orbital** — loses 1 mark in Q1(a). Examiners specifically check for the **perpendicular p_z lobe** that forms the π bond.
> 2. **Confusing band gap of diamond (5.5 eV) with that of graphite (~0 eV).** Many students incorrectly write that graphite has a "large band gap" — it is in fact a **semi-metal**.
> 3. **Writing 109° instead of 109.5°** for sp³ bond angle. The exact value **109.47°** is preferred; rounding to 109° without the decimal loses 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention the **promotion energy (~4 eV)** when describing the hybridization process.** Examiners expect this in the 7-mark sub-part.
> 5. **Mixing up sigma and pi bond counts** for graphene — the correct count is **3σ + 1 delocalized π per carbon**, not "3σ + 3π."
> 6. **In derivation questions, skipping the intermediate normalization step** of $\hat{v}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(1,1,1)$ — this is the step where students most often lose 2 marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PEMET868 Module 3, "Bonding in C Materials":**

- ⬛ Carbon's ground state is **1s² 2s² 2p²**; promotion of one 2s electron to 2p_z costs **~4 eV** and enables tetravalency.
- ⬛ **sp³** → tetrahedral, **109.47°**, 4 σ bonds → **diamond**, band gap **5.5 eV**.
- ⬛ **sp²** → trigonal planar, **120°**, 3 σ + 1 delocalized π → **graphene, graphite, CNT, fullerene**.
- ⬛ **sp** → linear, **180°**, 1 σ + 2 π → **carbyne**; triple bond C≡C, length **1.20 Å**.
- ⬛ **s-character** of sp^n = **1/(n+1) × 100%** → 25%, 33.3%, 50% for sp³, sp², sp.
- ⬛ Higher s-character → **shorter bond length** and **higher bond energy**.
- ⬛ **Bond lengths:** sp³ = 1.54 Å > sp² = 1.42 Å > sp = 1.20 Å.
- ⬛ **Bond energies:** C–C (sp³) ≈ **347 kJ/mol** < C=C (sp²) ≈ **614 kJ/mol** < C≡C (sp) ≈ **839 kJ/mol**.
- ⬛ **Pauling's formula:** $d(n) = d_1 - 0.71\log_{10}(n)$ reliably predicts C–C lengths for n = 1, 2, 3.
- ⬛ The **tetrahedral angle derivation** uses the **cube-corner geometry** leading to $\cos\theta = -\tfrac{1}{3}$.
- ⬛ **Graphene** = 2D monoatomic sp² sheet; **CNT** = rolled graphene; **C₆₀** = wrapped graphene (with pentagonal defects introducing curvature/strain).
- ⬛ In **fullerenes**, curvature causes pyramidalization: some **sp² character is converted toward sp³**, lowering π-conjugation.
- ⬛ **Carbyne** exists as α (cumulene, all double bonds) and β (polyyne, alternating single/triple bonds).
- ⬛ All **sp² carbons** exhibit **delocalized π systems** responsible for electrical conductivity, optical absorption, and aromatic chemistry.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synthesis and Properties of Fullerene, Graphene, and CNT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET868 - NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Synthesis and Properties of Fullerene, Graphene, and CNT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔬 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Carbon Allotropes in Nanotechnology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Carbon, in its various nano-allotropic forms, forms the cornerstone of modern nanotechnology. The three primary nano-carbon structures studied are **Fullerene (0D)**, **Carbon Nanotubes (1D)**, and **Graphene (2D)**. Together with bulk graphite and diamond, these represent the complete dimensional spectrum of carbon.

The element **Carbon (C, atomic number 6, atomic mass 12.011 g/mol)** exhibits extraordinary versatility due to its unique electron configuration $1s^2 2s^2 2p^2$, which allows for **sp, sp², and sp³ hybridization**. This hybridization flexibility is the fundamental origin of all the diverse carbon nanostructures studied in this module.

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundational Concept:** Each carbon atom has 4 valence electrons. In nanostructures, the bonding configuration dictates the geometry: **sp² hybridization** produces planar/curved sheets (graphene, fullerene, CNT walls), while **sp³ hybridization** produces tetrahedral structures (diamond).

---

## 1.2 Fullerene (C₆₀) — The "Buckyball"

### Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **Fullerene** is a closed-cage carbon molecule consisting of exactly **n carbon atoms** arranged in pentagonal and hexagonal rings, with the general formula $C_n$ where $n \geq 20$ (even number). The most stable and abundant fullerene is **C₆₀** (Buckminsterfullerene), discovered by **Kroto, Curl, and Smalley in 1985** (Nobel Prize in Chemistry, 1996).

### Structural Description

C₆₀ is composed of **60 carbon atoms** arranged as a **truncated icosahedron**, identical in geometry to a soccer ball. The structure contains:
- **20 hexagonal faces** (6-membered rings)
- **12 pentagonal faces** (5-membered rings)
- **No two pentagons share an edge** (Isolated Pentagon Rule — IPR)

The carbon atoms are **sp² hybridized**, but due to curvature, they possess a small degree of **sp³ character (~10%)**, releasing ring strain.

### Intuitive Analogy 🏆

> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a soccer ball. The black pentagons and white hexagons correspond to the 12 pentagonal and 20 hexagonal carbon rings in C₆₀. Just as the soccer ball is a hollow, closed cage, so is C₆₀ — with a diameter of approximately **0.71 nm** and an internal cavity large enough to encapsulate small atoms (metallofullerene).

---

## 1.3 Carbon Nanotubes (CNT) — The "Cylindrical Buckyball"

### Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **Carbon Nanotube (CNT)** is a one-dimensional (1D) hollow cylindrical nanostructure formed by rolling a single (or multiple) sheet(s) of graphene into a seamless tube. Each CNT consists entirely of **sp² hybridized carbon atoms** arranged in a hexagonal lattice.

### Classification

| Type | Abbreviation | Structure | Typical Diameter |
|------|--------------|-----------|------------------|
| Single-Walled CNT | SWCNT | Single graphene cylinder | **0.4 – 2 nm** |
| Double-Walled CNT | DWCNT | Two concentric cylinders | **1 – 3.5 nm** |
| Multi-Walled CNT | MWCNT | Multiple concentric cylinders | **2 – 100 nm** |

### Intuitive Analogy 📜

> **Conceptual Analogy:** Take a single sheet of chicken wire (graphene) and roll it up like a straw. The resulting hollow tube is a **SWCNT**. Now, place one straw inside another slightly larger straw, and another inside that — you get an **MWCNT**.

### Chiral Configurations (Helicity)

The way graphene is rolled determines the chirality, defined by the chiral vector:

$$\vec{C}_h = n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2$$

where $n, m$ are integers and $\vec{a}_1, \vec{a}_2$ are graphene lattice vectors.

| Type | Condition | Geometry |
|------|-----------|----------|
| **Armchair** | $n = m$ | High conductivity, **metallic** |
| **Zigzag** | $m = 0$ (or $n = 0$) | Either metallic or **semiconducting** |
| **Chiral** | $n \neq m \neq 0$ | Either metallic or **semiconducting** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** A SWCNT is **metallic** if $(n - m)$ is a multiple of 3, otherwise it is **semiconducting**. Approximately **1/3** of all SWCNTs are metallic, and **2/3** are semiconducting.

---

## 1.4 Graphene — The "Wonder Material"

### Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** **Graphene** is a two-dimensional (2D), single-atom-thick planar sheet of **sp² hybridized carbon atoms** arranged in a hexagonal (honeycomb) lattice. It is the basic building block of all other graphitic carbon allotropes (rolled → CNT, wrapped → Fullerene, stacked → Graphite). Isolated and characterized by **Geim and Novoselov in 2004** (Nobel Prize in Physics, 2010).

### Structural Description

- **Bond length:** C–C bond length = **0.142 nm (1.42 Å)**
- **Lattice constant:** $a_0$ = **0.246 nm (2.46 Å)**
- **Layer thickness:** Single atomic layer = **0.34 nm** (including van der Waals gap in graphite)

### Intuitive Analogy 🍯

> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a single layer of atoms arranged like a honeycomb — each cell being a perfect hexagon with a carbon atom at every vertex. This is graphene — so thin (one atom thick) that it is essentially **2D material**. If graphite (pencil lead) is a stack of papers, graphene is a single sheet of that paper.

---

## 1.5 Comparative Summary Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Reference: Dimensional Classification of Carbon Nanostructures**

| Property | Fullerene (C₆₀) | CNT | Graphene |
|----------|----------------|-----|----------|
| **Dimensionality** | 0D (quantum dot-like) | 1D (wire-like) | 2D (sheet-like) |
| **Hybridization** | sp² (with sp³ character) | sp² (pure) | sp² (pure) |
| **Geometry** | Closed cage | Hollow cylinder | Planar sheet |
| **Typical Size** | Diameter ~0.71 nm | Length 1 nm–1 cm | Lateral size up to meters |
| **Discovery** | 1985 | 1991 (Iijima) | 2004 |
| **Building Block** | Wrapped graphene | Rolled graphene | Basic 2D unit |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Synthesis Methods — Comprehensive Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Fullerene Synthesis

#### Method 1: Arc Discharge Method (Krätschmer–Huffman Method, 1990)

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle:** Vaporization of graphite electrodes in an inert gas atmosphere (Helium, low pressure ~100 torr) produces carbon soot containing fullerenes.

**Step-by-Step Process:**
1. Two high-purity graphite rods (anode and cathode) are placed inside a chamber filled with **Helium gas** at low pressure.
2. A high current (~100–200 A) at low voltage (~20–25 V) is passed between the electrodes, generating an **electric arc** (temperature ~3000–4000 °C).
3. The anode is vaporized, producing a carbon plasma/plume.
4. Carbon atoms in the plasma collide and recondense on cooler surfaces, forming soot that contains C₆₀ and C₇₀.
5. Fullerenes are extracted from the soot using a **non-polar organic solvent** (typically **toluene** or **benzene**).
6. Separation is achieved via **column chromatography** (typically using activated charcoal or silica gel).

**Yield:** ~10–15% of soot mass for C₆₀ + C₇₀ mixture.

#### Method 2: Laser Ablation / Laser Vaporization

**Step-by-Step Process:**
1. A high-power pulsed laser (Nd:YAG or CO₂, ~1–10 J/cm²) is focused on a graphite target.
2. The target is placed inside a **quartz tube** heated to ~1200 °C in an inert gas atmosphere (Ar/He at ~500 torr).
3. Carbon vapor is produced and swept downstream by the carrier gas, where it condenses on a cold collector.
4. The soot produced contains fullerenes (and SWCNTs, in case of catalyst addition).

**Advantage:** Higher purity; **Disadvantage:** Low yield and expensive.

#### Method 3: Combustion Method (Industrial Scale)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Significance:** Combustion of hydrocarbons (benzene, toluene) in a low-pressure sooting flame produces fullerenes at industrial scale (~tons/year).

**Step-by-Step Process:**
1. A premixed hydrocarbon/oxygen flame is stabilized at low pressure (50–100 torr).
2. Conditions are tuned to favor fullerene formation (C/O ratio ~0.7–1.0).
3. Soot is collected and fullerenes extracted using solvents.

#### Method 4: Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD)

1. Hydrocarbon gas (e.g., CH₄, C₂H₂) is introduced into a reactor at high temperature (500–1000 °C).
2. Decomposition on a substrate produces carbon species that nucleate fullerene-like cages.
3. Less common than arc-discharge for bulk fullerene production.

---

### 2.1.2 Carbon Nanotube Synthesis

#### Method 1: Arc Discharge Method (Iijima, 1991)

**Step-by-Step Process:**
1. Two graphite electrodes are placed in an inert gas (He/Ar, ~500 torr).
2. A catalyst (Fe, Co, Ni, or Y) is mixed into the anode.
3. An arc discharge (~100 A, 20 V) vaporizes the anode.
4. Carbon condenses on the cathode, forming MWCNTs (and SWCNTs if catalyst is used).
5. **Cathode deposit** contains MWCNTs; **web-like soot on chamber walls** contains SWCNTs.

**Yield:** Up to **30–60% MWCNTs** in cathode deposit.

#### Method 2: Laser Ablation

1. Laser pulses vaporize a graphite target doped with metal catalyst (Ni/Co, ~1–2 at.%).
2. The vapor is carried by flowing inert gas at ~1200 °C.
3. SWCNTs form in the high-temperature zone.
4. **Produces high-quality SWCNTs** (~70–90% purity) with narrow diameter distribution.

#### Method 3: Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) — Most Scalable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Standard:** CVD is the **dominant industrial method** for CNT production due to scalability and cost-effectiveness.

**Step-by-Step Process:**
1. **Substrate preparation:** Si or SiO₂ wafer coated with catalyst nanoparticles (Fe, Co, Ni, ~1–100 nm) using sputtering or wet chemistry.
2. **Reactor setup:** Quartz tube inside a furnace heated to **600–900 °C**.
3. **Carbon source:** Hydrocarbon gas (C₂H₂, CH₄, C₂H₄, or CO) introduced with carrier gas (H₂/N₂/Ar).
4. **Decomposition:** Hydrocarbon decomposes on catalyst nanoparticles via the **vapor–liquid–solid (VLS) mechanism**.
5. **Nucleation and growth:** Carbon atoms dissolve into the metal nanoparticle, become supersaturated, and precipitate as a tubular structure.

**Key Equations:**
$$ \text{C}_x\text{H}_y \xrightarrow{\Delta, \text{catalyst}} x\text{C} + \frac{y}{2}\text{H}_2 $$

**Tip-growth model:** Catalyst remains at the tip of the growing CNT (weak catalyst–substrate interaction).
**Base-growth model:** Catalyst stays anchored to the substrate (strong interaction).

#### Method 4: High-Pressure Carbon Monoxide (HiPco) Process

1. CO at high pressure (30–50 atm) and temperature (900–1100 °C) flows over Fe(CO)₅ catalyst in a reactor.
2. CO disproportionates: $2CO \rightarrow C + CO_2$.
3. Carbon atoms nucleate SWCNTs on iron nanoparticles.
4. Produces **high-quality SWCNTs** continuously.

---

### 2.1.3 Graphene Synthesis

#### Method 1: Mechanical Exfoliation (Scotch Tape Method)

> [!NOTE]
> **"Scotch Tape Method"** — Used by Geim and Novoselov (2004) to isolate graphene for the first time.

**Step-by-Step Process:**
1. A piece of **highly oriented pyrolytic graphite (HOPG)** is repeatedly peeled using adhesive tape.
2. Repeated peeling thins down the graphite to single or few layers.
3. The thin flakes are transferred onto a **SiO₂/Si substrate**.
4. Single-layer graphene is identified by optical microscopy (due to interference contrast on SiO₂ of specific thickness ~300 nm).

**Advantage:** Highest-quality monolayer graphene; **Disadvantage:** Not scalable.

#### Method 2: Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD)

1. **Substrate:** Copper (Cu) or Nickel (Ni) foil.
2. **Annealing:** Substrate heated to ~1000 °C in H₂/Ar to clean and recrystallize the surface.
3. **Carbon source:** Methane (CH₄) introduced at low pressure or atmospheric pressure.
4. **Growth mechanism:**
   - On **Cu**: Surface-mediated (carbon dissolves minimally). Growth is **self-limiting** → monolayer graphene.
   - On **Ni**: Precipitation-mediated (carbon dissolves and precipitates upon cooling). Layer thickness controlled by cooling rate.
5. **Transfer:** PMMA-assisted transfer to a target substrate.

**Equation:**
$$ \text{CH}_4 \xrightarrow{\text{Cu, 1000°C}} \text{C (graphene)} + 2\text{H}_2 $$

#### Method 3: Epitaxial Growth on SiC

1. SiC substrate heated to **~1400–1600 °C in ultra-high vacuum (UHV)**.
2. Si atoms sublimate preferentially, leaving behind a carbon-rich surface.
3. The remaining carbon rearranges into epitaxial graphene layers.
4. Produces wafer-scale graphene directly on a semiconducting substrate (electronics-compatible).

#### Method 4: Reduction of Graphene Oxide (RedGO)

1. **Oxidation:** Graphite is oxidized using **Hummers' method** (KMnO₄ + H₂SO₄ + NaNO₃) to form graphite oxide.
2. **Exfoliation:** Graphite oxide is exfoliated in water via ultrasonication to form **graphene oxide (GO)**.
3. **Reduction:** GO is reduced using chemical reductants (hydrazine, NaBH₄) or thermal annealing (~1000 °C) to form **reduced graphene oxide (rGO)**.

**Equation:**
$$ \text{GO} + \text{Reductant} \rightarrow \text{rGO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Caution:** rGO contains residual oxygen functional groups and defects; properties are inferior to pristine graphene.

#### Method 5: Liquid-Phase Exfoliation

1. Graphite is dispersed in a solvent (NMP, DMF) or aqueous surfactant solution.
2. Ultrasonication exfoliates the graphite into few-layer graphene flakes.
3. Centrifugation separates thin flakes from thicker ones.

---

## 2.2 Properties of Carbon Nanostructures

### 2.2.1 Properties of Fullerene (C₆₀)

| Property | Value/Description |
|----------|------------------|
| **Molecular formula** | C₆₀ |
| **Molecular weight** | 720.66 g/mol |
| **Diameter** | ~0.71 nm |
| **Color (solution)** | Magenta/Purple (in toluene) |
| **Density** | 1.65 g/cm³ |
| **Melting point** | ~600 °C (sublimes at ~400 °C at low pressure) |
| **Band gap** | ~1.5–1.8 eV (semiconducting) |
| **Solubility** | Soluble in non-polar solvents (toluene, CS₂) |
| **Electron affinity** | ~2.65 eV (good electron acceptor) |

**Special Properties:**
- **Superconductivity:** Alkali-doped C₆₀ (e.g., K₃C₆₀) becomes superconducting; **Tc ≈ 18 K** (highest for molecular solids).
- **Electron acceptor:** Easily accepts electrons → forms C₆₀⁻, C₆₀²⁻, etc.
- **Endohedral fullerenes:** Metal atoms (La, Gd) can be trapped inside the cage (e.g., La@C₆₀).

### 2.2.2 Properties of CNT

| Property | SWCNT | MWCNT |
|----------|-------|-------|
| **Young's modulus** | ~1 TPa | ~1–1.2 TPa |
| **Tensile strength** | 50–500 GPa | 10–60 GPa |
| **Density** | ~1.3–1.4 g/cm³ | ~1.7–2.1 g/cm³ |
| **Electrical conductivity** | Metallic or semiconducting | Generally metallic |
| **Current capacity** | ~10⁹ A/cm² (1000× Cu) | Lower |
| **Thermal conductivity** | ~3500 W/m·K (axial) | ~3000 W/m·K |
| **Aspect ratio** | 100–10,000 | 100–10,000 |
| **Specific surface area** | ~400–900 m²/g | ~200–400 m²/g |

**Unique Properties:**
- **Ballistic transport:** Electrons travel without scattering over micron-length scales.
- **Field emission:** Low turn-on voltage (~1–3 V/μm).
- **EMI shielding:** Superior due to high conductivity and aspect ratio.

### 2.2.3 Properties of Graphene

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Thickness** | 0.34 nm (interlayer), single atomic layer ~0.142 nm |
| **Specific surface area** | **2630 m²/g** (highest for a monolayer material) |
| **Young's modulus** | ~1 TPa |
| **Intrinsic strength** | ~130 GPa |
| **Fracture strength** | ~42 N/m |
| **Electron mobility (suspended)** | ~200,000 cm²/V·s (room temperature) |
| **Sheet resistance** | ~31 Ω/sq (theoretical minimum) |
| **Optical transmittance** | ~97.7% per layer (absorbs only 2.3% of visible light) |
| **Thermal conductivity (suspended)** | ~1500–5300 W/m·K |
| **Density** | ~2.267 g/cm³ |
| **Fermi velocity** | $v_F \approx 1 \times 10^6$ m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anomalous Quantum Hall Effect:** Graphene exhibits the **quantum Hall effect at room temperature**, a property unique among 2D materials.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table for KTU 2024 Exam — Must Memorize**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|-----------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Chiral vector of CNT | $\vec{C}_h = n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2$ | Defines tube geometry |
| 2 | Chiral angle | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}\,m}{2n+m}\right)$ | Armchair: $\theta = 30°$, Zigzag: $\theta = 0°$ |
| 3 | CNT diameter | $d = \frac{a_0 \sqrt{n^2 + nm + m^2}}{\pi}$ | $a_0 = 0.246$ nm (graphene lattice constant) |
| 4 | Number of hexagons per unit cell | $N = \frac{2(n^2 + nm + m^2)}{d_R}$ | $d_R = \gcd(2n+m, 2m+n)$ |
| 5 | Metallicity condition | $(n - m) = 3k$ | $k \in \mathbb{Z}$ → metallic |
| 6 | C–C bond length | $a_{CC} = 0.142$ nm | Same in graphene, CNT |
| 7 | Optical absorption of graphene | $A \approx \pi\alpha \approx 2.3\%$ | $\alpha$ = fine structure constant (1/137) |
| 8 | 2D electron density (graphene) | $n = \frac{\pi}{(\hbar v_F)^2} E_F^2$ | Dirac fermions |
| 9 | Aspect ratio of CNT | $AR = \frac{L}{d}$ | L = length, d = diameter |
| 10 | Fullerene cage atom count (Cₙ) | $n = 20 + 12k$, $k = 0,1,2,\ldots$ | Euler's formula |
| 11 | Pentagonal faces in fullerene | Always **12** | For any $C_n$ obeying IPR |
| 12 | Hexagonal faces in fullerene | $\frac{n - 20}{2}$ | For $C_n$ where $n \geq 24$ |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Relevance for KTU:**

| Material | Key Applications |
|----------|------------------|
| **Fullerene** | Organic solar cells (PCBM acceptor), lubricants, MRI contrast agents (Gd@C₆₀), HIV drug delivery (research), superconductors |
| **CNT** | Structural composites (sporting goods, aerospace), supercapacitors, field-emission displays, AFM tips, hydrogen storage, interconnects |
| **Graphene** | Flexible electronics, supercapacitors, sensors (single-molecule detection), batteries (Si anodes), transparent electrodes, biomedical |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📐 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Chiral Vector, Chiral Angle & Diameter of CNT

### Given:
A graphene sheet with lattice vectors $\vec{a}_1$ and $\vec{a}_2$, where:
$$\lvert \vec{a}_1 \rvert = \lvert \vec{a}_2 \rvert = a_0 = 0.246 \text{ nm}, \quad \angle(\vec{a}_1, \vec{a}_2) = 60°$$

### Step 1: Express the chiral vector

The chiral vector is defined as:
$$\vec{C}_h = n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2$$

where $n$ and $m$ are integers.

**Logic:** When graphene is rolled to form a CNT, the chiral vector $\vec{C}_h$ becomes the **circumference** of the tube. The two ends of $\vec{C}_h$ must coincide after rolling.

### Step 2: Compute the magnitude of the chiral vector

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lvert \vec{C}_h \rvert^2 &= (n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2)\cdot(n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2) \\
&= n^2 \lvert\vec{a}_1\rvert^2 + 2nm\,\vec{a}_1\cdot\vec{a}_2 + m^2 \lvert\vec{a}_2\rvert^2 \\
&= n^2 a_0^2 + 2nm\,a_0^2\cos(60°) + m^2 a_0^2 \\
&= a_0^2 \left( n^2 + 2nm\cdot\frac{1}{2} + m^2 \right) \\
&= a_0^2 (n^2 + nm + m^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Using the dot product identity $\vec{a}_1\cdot\vec{a}_2 = a_0^2\cos(60°) = a_0^2/2$.

### Step 3: Compute the diameter

Since $\vec{C}_h$ becomes the circumference of the rolled tube:
$$L = \pi d \quad\Rightarrow\quad d = \frac{\lvert\vec{C}_h\rvert}{\pi}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{d_{CNT} = \frac{a_0\sqrt{n^2 + nm + m^2}}{\pi}}$$

### Step 4: Compute the chiral angle

The chiral angle $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{C}_h$ and $\vec{a}_1$. Using the dot product:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos\theta &= \frac{\vec{C}_h\cdot\vec{a}_1}{\lvert\vec{C}_h\rvert\,\lvert\vec{a}_1\rvert} \\
&= \frac{(n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2)\cdot\vec{a}_1}{\lvert\vec{C}_h\rvert\,a_0} \\
&= \frac{n a_0^2 + m\,a_0^2\cos(60°)}{a_0^2 \sqrt{n^2 + nm + m^2}} \\
&= \frac{n + \frac{m}{2}}{\sqrt{n^2 + nm + m^2}} \\
&= \frac{2n + m}{2\sqrt{n^2 + nm + m^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\theta = \cos^{-1}\left( \frac{2n + m}{2\sqrt{n^2 + nm + m^2}} \right)}$$

Equivalently:
$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left( \frac{\sqrt{3}\,m}{2n+m} \right)$$

### Verification with special cases:

| Case | $(n,m)$ | Predicted $\theta$ | Geometric Type |
|------|---------|--------------------|---------------|
| Armchair | $(n,n)$ | $\theta = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{3n}{2\sqrt{3}\,n}\right) = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = 30°$ | ✓ Armchair |
| Zigzag | $(n,0)$ | $\theta = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{2n}{2\sqrt{n^2}}\right) = \cos^{-1}(1) = 0°$ | ✓ Zigzag |
| Chiral | $(n,m)$, $n \neq m$ | $0° < \theta < 30°$ | ✓ Chiral |

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Metallicity Condition for SWCNT

### Quantum confinement of graphene band structure:

In graphene, the conduction and valence bands meet at the **K and K' points** of the Brillouin zone. The energy dispersion near these points is:
$$E(\vec{k}) = \pm \hbar v_F \lvert\vec{k} - \vec{K}\rvert$$

**Logic:** Graphene's charge carriers behave as massless **Dirac fermions** with linear dispersion (not parabolic like in conventional semiconductors).

### Quantization due to periodic boundary conditions:

When graphene is rolled into a CNT, the wavevector component along the circumference must satisfy:
$$\vec{k}\cdot\vec{C}_h = 2\pi q, \quad q \in \mathbb{Z}$$

This creates a set of **1D sub-bands** (cutting lines through the 2D graphene Brillouin zone). The number of sub-bands that pass through the K point determines if the tube is metallic.

### Condition for metallic tube:

The allowed k-points in the rolled structure are:
$$\vec{k}_\perp = \frac{2\pi q}{\lvert\vec{C}_h\rvert}$$

The tube is **metallic** if one of these allowed lines passes through the K point. Mathematically:
$$\vec{K}\cdot\vec{C}_h = \frac{2\pi}{3}(2n + m) = 2\pi q_{\text{integer}}$$

This requires:
$$\frac{2n+m}{3} \in \mathbb{Z} \quad\Rightarrow\quad (2n + m) = 3k$$

Equivalent forms (using symmetry):
$$\boxed{(n - m) = 3k, \quad k \in \mathbb{Z}}$$

### Probability distribution:
- If $n, m$ are chosen randomly, $(n - m) \bmod 3$ is **equally likely** to be 0, 1, or 2.
- Therefore: **P(metallic) = 1/3, P(semiconducting) = 2/3**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Number of Carbon Atoms in CNT Unit Cell

### Concept:
A unit cell of a CNT contains a finite number of hexagons, each contributing 2 carbon atoms.

### Step 1: Translational vector along the tube axis

The translational vector $\vec{T}$ is the shortest lattice vector perpendicular to $\vec{C}_h$ in the graphene lattice:
$$\vec{T} = \frac{2m + n}{d_R}\vec{a}_1 - \frac{2n + m}{d_R}\vec{a}_2$$

where $d_R = \gcd(2n+m, 2m+n)$.

### Step 2: Number of unit cells in the chiral vector

$$N_{\text{cells}} = \frac{\lvert\vec{C}_h \times \vec{T}\rvert}{\lvert\vec{a}_1 \times \vec{a}_2\rvert} = \frac{2(n^2 + nm + m^2)}{d_R}$$

### Step 3: Total carbon atoms

Each unit cell has 2 atoms, and the unit cell contains $N_{\text{cells}}$ hexagons:
$$\boxed{N_{\text{atoms}} = \frac{4(n^2 + nm + m^2)}{d_R}}$$

### Example: Armchair (n,n) — e.g., (5,5) tube
- $d_R = \gcd(15, 15) = 15$
- $N_{\text{atoms}} = \frac{4(25 + 25 + 25)}{15} = \frac{300}{15} = 20$ atoms per unit cell ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> **Synthesis Method Selection Criteria**

| Parameter | Arc Discharge | Laser Ablation | CVD (CNT) | CVD (Graphene) | Exfoliation |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|-----------|----------------|-------------|
| **Quality** | High | Very High | Medium-High | High (Cu) | Very High |
| **Purity** | Medium | High | Variable | High | Variable |
| **Scalability** | Low | Very Low | **High** | **High** | Very Low |
| **Cost** | Medium | High | Low | Medium | Low |
| **Yield** | Medium | Low | High | High | Very Low |
| **Diameter control** | Poor | Good | Good | N/A | N/A |
| **Chirality control** | None | Limited | Developing | N/A | N/A |
| **Industrial use** | Lab | Lab | **Industry** | **Industry** | Lab |

## 3.5 Optical Absorption of Graphene — Analytical Derivation

### Fine structure constant and optical conductivity:

For pristine graphene, the optical conductivity is a constant:
$$\sigma(\omega) = \frac{\pi e^2}{2h}$$

for photon energies $\hbar\omega \gg k_B T$ and ignoring many-body effects.

### Optical transmittance:

For a single layer with thickness $t \ll \lambda$:
$$T = 1 - A = 1 - \frac{\sigma}{2\epsilon_0 c}$$

Substituting:
$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi e^2}{2h}\cdot\frac{1}{2\epsilon_0 c} \\
&= \frac{e^2}{4\epsilon_0 h c} \\
&= \pi\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\alpha = \frac{e^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 \hbar c} = \frac{1}{137.036}$ is the fine structure constant.

$$\boxed{A \approx \pi\alpha \approx 2.3\%}$$

**Insight:** Each graphene layer absorbs exactly **2.3%** of incident visible light, regardless of wavelength. This is why graphite appears black (many layers absorb fully).

# 📚 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define fullerene. Explain the structure of C₆₀ with a neat diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Fullerene** is a closed-cage carbon molecule consisting of $n$ carbon atoms arranged in pentagonal and hexagonal rings, following the general formula $C_n$ where $n \geq 20$ (even integer), satisfying the Isolated Pentagon Rule (IPR).

**Structure of C₆₀ (Buckminsterfullerene):**
- C₆₀ is the most stable and abundant fullerene.
- Geometry: **Truncated icosahedron** (soccer-ball shape).
- Composed of **12 pentagonal faces** and **20 hexagonal faces**.
- Carbon atoms are **sp² hybridized** with approximately **10% sp³ character** due to curvature-induced strain.
- **Diameter ≈ 0.71 nm**.
- No two pentagons share a common edge (IPR rule minimizes strain).
- C–C bond length: ~0.14 nm (shorter for hexagon–hexagon junction at 0.138 nm, longer for pentagon–hexagon at 0.145 nm).

**Euler's formula verification:** V − E + F = 2 → 60 − 90 + 32 = 2 ✓

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is graphene? List any four unique properties of graphene.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Graphene** is a two-dimensional (2D), single-atom-thick, planar sheet of sp² hybridized carbon atoms arranged in a perfect hexagonal (honeycomb) lattice. It is the basic structural unit of graphite, carbon nanotubes, and fullerenes. It was first isolated and characterized by **Andre Geim and Konstantin Novoselov in 2004** (Nobel Prize in Physics, 2010).

**Four Unique Properties:**
1. **Extraordinary electron mobility:** ~200,000 cm²/V·s at room temperature (suspended graphene) — far exceeding silicon (~1400 cm²/V·s).
2. **Optical transparency:** Absorbs only ~2.3% of visible light per layer (≈πα, where α is fine structure constant).
3. **Record thermal conductivity:** ~1500–5300 W/m·K (suspended monolayer).
4. **Massless Dirac fermions:** Charge carriers behave as relativistic particles with zero effective mass, leading to anomalous quantum Hall effect observable at room temperature.
5. *(Bonus)* Highest specific surface area: **2630 m²/g**.
6. *(Bonus)* Young's modulus ~1 TPa, intrinsic strength ~130 GPa.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### ❓ Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper]`

#### **Part (a)** — 7 Marks
**Explain the Krätschmer–Huffman method of fullerene synthesis in detail. What are the limitations of this method?**

#### **Part (b)** — 7 Marks
**Discuss the structure and properties of carbon nanotubes. Explain the different types of chirality with necessary equations.**

---

### 📝 Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) Model Solution:

**Krätschmer–Huffman Method (Arc Discharge Method for Fullerene Synthesis):**

**Principle:** Vaporization of graphite in an inert gas atmosphere using an electric arc produces carbon soot containing fullerenes.

**Apparatus Setup:**
- A **water-cooled stainless steel chamber** filled with **Helium gas** at low pressure (~100 torr).
- Two high-purity graphite rods positioned as electrodes:
  - **Anode** (vaporized): ~6 mm diameter
  - **Cathode**: ~9 mm diameter
  - Gap between electrodes: ~1–2 mm

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**
1. The chamber is evacuated to ~10⁻⁵ torr and back-filled with He at ~100–200 torr.
2. A **high DC current (~100–200 A) at low voltage (~20–25 V)** is passed between the electrodes, creating an electric arc.
3. The arc generates temperatures of ~**3000–4000 °C**, vaporizing the anode.
4. The vaporized carbon forms a **plasma plume** that condenses on cooler chamber surfaces as soot.
5. The soot is collected and dissolved in **non-polar solvents** (toluene, benzene, or CS₂).
6. **Column chromatography** (using activated charcoal or alumina) separates C₆₀ from higher fullerenes (C₇₀, C₇₆, C₈₄).
7. The pure C₆₀ solution is dried to obtain crystalline fullerene powder.

**Yield:** Typically 10–15% of soot mass (C₆₀ + C₇₀).

**Limitations:**
- Low yield (most of the vaporized carbon forms amorphous soot, not fullerenes).
- High energy consumption.
- Requires continuous electrode replacement.
- Limited scalability for industrial production.
- Product mixture requires tedious chromatographic separation.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution:

**Structure of Carbon Nanotubes:**

A Carbon Nanotube is formed by rolling a graphene sheet into a seamless hollow cylinder. The structure can be of two types:
- **SWCNT:** Single graphene cylinder (diameter 0.4–2 nm)
- **MWCNT:** Multiple concentric graphene cylinders (diameter 2–100 nm), separated by ~0.34 nm (graphite interlayer distance)

**Properties of CNT:**

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Young's modulus | ~1 TPa |
| Tensile strength | 50–500 GPa (SWCNT) |
| Current density | ~10⁹ A/cm² (1000× Cu) |
| Thermal conductivity | ~3500 W/m·K (axial) |
| Field emission turn-on voltage | 1–3 V/μm |
| Aspect ratio | 100–10,000 |

**Chirality in SWCNT — Detailed Explanation:**

The geometry of a SWCNT is defined by the **chiral vector**:
$$\vec{C}_h = n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2$$

where $\vec{a}_1, \vec{a}_2$ are graphene lattice vectors ($|\vec{a}_1| = |\vec{a}_2| = a_0 = 0.246$ nm), and $n, m$ are integers.

**Three Types of Chirality:**

1. **Armchair (n = m):** Chiral angle $\theta = 30°$. All armchair CNTs are **metallic**.
2. **Zigzag (m = 0):** Chiral angle $\theta = 0°$. Metallic if $n$ is multiple of 3, else semiconducting.
3. **Chiral (n ≠ m ≠ 0):** Chiral angle $0° < \theta < 30°$. Metallic if $(n-m)$ is multiple of 3.

**Diameter equation:**
$$d = \frac{a_0\sqrt{n^2 + nm + m^2}}{\pi}$$

**Metallicity condition:**
$$\boxed{(n - m) = 3k, \quad k \in \mathbb{Z}}$$

If satisfied → **Metallic**; else → **Semiconducting**.

**Statistical distribution:** Approximately **1/3 metallic**, **2/3 semiconducting**.

---

### ❓ Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper]`

#### **Part (a)** — 7 Marks
**Describe the CVD method for the synthesis of carbon nanotubes. Explain the vapor–liquid–solid (VLS) growth mechanism with a neat diagram.**

#### **Part (b)** — 7 Marks
**Compare the mechanical exfoliation and CVD methods of graphene synthesis. Discuss any three exceptional properties of graphene in detail.**

---

### 📝 Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) Model Solution:

**Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) of CNT — Step-by-Step:**

CVD is the **most widely used industrial method** for CNT synthesis due to its scalability and relatively low cost.

**Apparatus:**
- **Quartz tube reactor** placed inside a high-temperature furnace.
- **Mass flow controllers (MFC)** for precise gas delivery.
- **Substrate holder** in the central heating zone.

**Step-by-Step Process:**
1. **Substrate preparation:** Si, SiO₂, or quartz substrate is coated with catalyst nanoparticles (Fe, Co, Ni; size ~1–100 nm) using sputtering, e-beam evaporation, or wet chemical methods.
2. **Reactor setup:** Substrate is placed in the quartz tube.
3. **Purging:** Reactor is flushed with inert gas (Ar/N₂) to remove oxygen.
4. **Annealing:** Substrate is heated to **600–900 °C** in H₂/Ar to reduce catalyst oxides and clean the surface.
5. **Carbon source introduction:** Hydrocarbon gas (CH₄, C₂H₂, C₂H₄, or CO) is introduced along with carrier gas (H₂/N₂).
6. **Decomposition:** Hydrocarbon decomposes on catalyst surface: $C_xH_y \rightarrow xC + (y/2)H_2$.
7. **Growth:** Carbon atoms dissolve into the metal nanoparticle, become supersaturated, and precipitate as a tubular structure.
8. **Cooling:** Reactor is cooled under inert gas to prevent oxidation.

**VLS Growth Mechanism:**

```
Catalyst Nanoparticle (Liquid Metal Droplet)
            ↓
Hydrocarbon Decomposition on Surface
            ↓
Carbon Atoms Diffuse into Catalyst
            ↓
Supersaturation
            ↓
Carbon Precipitates at Catalyst-Substrate Interface
            ↓
Lifts Catalyst → Tip-Growth OR
Anchors Catalyst → Base-Growth
            ↓
CNT Formation
```

- **Tip-growth:** Weak catalyst–substrate interaction; catalyst lifts off as CNT grows.
- **Base-growth:** Strong catalyst–substrate interaction; catalyst remains at base.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution:

**Comparison: Mechanical Exfoliation vs CVD for Graphene**

| Parameter | Mechanical Exfoliation | CVD |
|-----------|------------------------|-----|
| **Substrate** | SiO₂/Si (300 nm oxide) | Cu or Ni foil |
| **Method** | Scotch tape peeling | CH₄ decomposition at 1000 °C |
| **Quality** | Very high (pristine) | High |
| **Size** | Microns (small flakes) | Wafer-scale (continuous film) |
| **Cost** | Low (lab) | Medium–High |
| **Scalability** | Very poor | Excellent |
| **Layer control** | Single-layer identified by microscopy | Cu → monolayer (self-limiting); Ni → multilayer |
| **Transfer needed** | No | Yes (PMMA-assisted) |
| **Defects** | Very few | Grain boundaries, multilayer patches |
| **Application** | Fundamental research | Industrial electronics |

**Three Exceptional Properties of Graphene:**

**1. Exceptional Electron Mobility (≈200,000 cm²/V·s):**
- Suspended graphene at room temperature shows electron mobility up to 200,000 cm²/V·s.
- ~140× higher than silicon (~1400 cm²/V·s).
- Charge carriers behave as **massless Dirac fermions** with Fermi velocity $v_F \approx 10^6$ m/s.
- Energy-momentum relation: $E = \hbar v_F \lvert \vec{k} \rvert$ (linear, unlike parabolic).
- Enables **ballistic transport** over micron distances even at room temperature.

**2. Record Mechanical Strength (~130 GPa Intrinsic Strength):**
- Young's modulus ~1 TPa.
- Breaking strength ~42 N/m (force per unit length to break).
- ~200× stronger than structural steel by weight.
- Can be stretched up to 20–25% of its original length.
- Applications: flexible electronics, structural composites.

**3. Anomalous Quantum Hall Effect at Room Temperature:**
- The half-integer quantum Hall effect is observable in graphene at room temperature (200 K and even up to 300 K).
- Uniquely tied to its 2D Dirac-fermion nature.
- This has never been observed in any other material at room temperature.
- Opens applications in metrology and quantum computing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bonus Property:** Optical transparency — graphene absorbs only **2.3% (≈ πα)** of incident light per layer, independent of wavelength. This makes it ideal for transparent electrodes (replacing ITO).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Chirality equation confusion:** Many students write $d = a_0\sqrt{n^2+nm+m^2}$ forgetting the $\pi$ in the denominator. The correct diameter is $\frac{a_0\sqrt{n^2+nm+m^2}}{\pi}$. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 2. **Metallicity condition:** Students often write "metallic if $n = m$" — this is only true for armchair tubes. The general condition is $(n-m) = 3k$. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
>
> 3. **Sp² vs sp³ hybridization in Fullerene:** Students incorrectly state C₆₀ is fully sp³ hybridized. It is **sp² with ~10% sp³ character** due to curvature. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 4. **CVD growth mechanism:** Writing "carbon deposits on substrate" without mentioning **dissolution in catalyst → supersaturation → precipitation** misses the key VLS mechanism. **(Lose 2 marks)**
>
> 5. **Graphene unit:** Students sometimes write graphene is "1-atom thick = 0.34 nm." This is wrong — single atomic layer is ~**0.142 nm**; 0.34 nm is the **graphite interlayer distance**. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 6. **Arc Discharge vs Laser Ablation:** Confusing which method produces MWCNTs (cathode deposit) vs SWCNTs (web soot / with catalyst). **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 7. **Optical absorption:** Writing "graphene absorbs all light" — incorrect. It absorbs only **2.3% per layer**. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 8. **IPR rule:** Students often forget to mention the **Isolated Pentagon Rule** (no two pentagons share an edge) when describing fullerene stability. **(Lose 0.5–1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 High-Density Revision Checklist

#### Fullerene
- [ ] **C₆₀** = Truncated icosahedron, **12 pentagons + 20 hexagons**, diameter **~0.71 nm**.
- [ ] Discovered by **Kroto, Curl, Smalley (1985)**; bulk synthesis by **Krätschmer–Huffman (1990)**.
- [ ] **sp²** hybridization with ~10% sp³ character.
- [ ] Synthesis: **Arc discharge (He, 100 torr), Laser ablation, Combustion, CVD**.
- [ ] Extraction using **toluene**; purification by **column chromatography**.
- [ ] **Superconductivity** in alkali-doped C₆₀ (K₃C₆₀, T_c ≈ 18 K).
- [ ] **Electron affinity** ~2.65 eV — good electron acceptor (PCBM in organic solar cells).

#### Carbon Nanotubes
- [ ] **SWCNT**: single wall, d = 0.4–2 nm; **MWCNT**: multi wall, d = 2–100 nm.
- [ ] Discovered by **Iijima (1991)**.
- [ ] **Chiral vector**: $\vec{C}_h = n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2$.
- [ ] **Diameter**: $d = \frac{a_0\sqrt{n^2+nm+m^2}}{\pi}$ where $a_0 = 0.246$ nm.
- [ ] **Chiral angle**: $\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}\,m}{2n+m}\right)$; Armchair = 30°, Zigzag = 0°.
- [ ] **Metallic** if $(n-m)$ is multiple of 3; else **semiconducting**. (Ratio: 1/3 metallic, 2/3 semiconducting.)
- [ ] **Young's modulus ~1 TPa**; tensile strength 50–500 GPa; current density 10⁹ A/cm².
- [ ] Synthesis: **Arc discharge, Laser ablation, CVD (most scalable), HiPco**.
- [ ] **VLS mechanism** in CVD; **Tip-growth** vs **Base-growth**.

#### Graphene
- [ ] **2D, single-atom-thick**, sp² hexagonal lattice; discovered by **Geim & Novoselov (2004)**.
- [ ] **C–C bond** = 0.142 nm; **lattice constant** $a_0$ = 0.246 nm; **layer thickness** ~0.142 nm.
- [ ] Electron mobility ~200,000 cm²/V·s (suspended, RT).
- [ ] Young's modulus ~1 TPa; intrinsic strength ~130 GPa.
- [ ] Thermal conductivity ~1500–5300 W/m·K (suspended).
- [ ] **Optical absorption** = πα ≈ **2.3%** per layer.
- [ ] **Specific surface area** = **2630 m²/g**.
- [ ] Synthesis: **Mechanical exfoliation (best quality), CVD (scalable), Epitaxial on SiC, Reduction of GO (RedGO)**.
- [ ] **Dirac fermions** — linear dispersion; **anomalous QHE** at room temperature.

#### Cross-Cutting Concepts
- [ ] **All three nanostructures are derived from graphene** — the basic 2D building block.
- [ ] **sp² hybridization** is the unifying bonding feature.
- [ ] **Isolated Pentagon Rule (IPR)** explains fullerene stability.
- [ ] **Aspect ratio** of CNT = L/d (very high, up to 10⁴).
- [ ] **Hummers' method** produces graphite oxide from graphite.
- [ ] **Fermi velocity** $v_F \approx 10^6$ m/s in graphene (≈ c/300).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** **"FGC — Fundamental Graphene Connection"** — **F**ullerene = wrapped, **G**raphene = base, **C**NT = rolled. All are sp² carbon with hexagonal lattice!

---

**END OF NOTES — Module 3: Synthesis and Properties of Fullerene, Graphene, and CNT**

topics.title: Diamondoid Nanomaterials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET868 - NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Diamondoid Nanomaterials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diamondoid Nanomaterials** are a unique class of carbon-based nanostructures composed of **sp³-hybridized carbon atoms** arranged in the same tetrahedral network as bulk diamond, but restricted to the nanometer scale. The term broadly encompasses three families:
>
> 1. **Molecular Diamondoids** – H-terminated cage hydrocarbons (adamantane C₁₀H₁₆, diamantane C₁₄H₂₀, triamantane C₁₈H₂₄, etc.) that are essentially fragments of the diamond lattice.
> 2. **Nanodiamonds (NDs)** – Diamond crystallites with diameters typically in the range **1–100 nm**, often produced by detonation, laser ablation, or high-pressure high-temperature (HPHT) synthesis.
> 3. **Diamond-Like Carbon (DLC) Nanomaterials** – Amorphous or quasi-amorphous carbon films (a-C, ta-C, a-C:H) with a high fraction of sp³ bonds, lacking long-range crystalline order.

In the KTU 2024 (NEP 2020) syllabus, diamondoid nanomaterials are positioned as **model 0D, 1D, and 2D sp³ carbon systems** that bridge molecular chemistry and crystalline solid-state physics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a diamond ring as a skyscraper made of LEGO bricks.** Each LEGO brick is a tiny tetrahedral carbon unit. Now, take *one single brick* → that is **adamantane**. Stack a few bricks in a fixed pattern → that is a **higher diamondoid** (diamantane, triamantane…). Stack millions in a perfect 3D array → that is a **bulk diamond crystal**. If, instead, you stack them imperfectly, you get **diamond-like carbon (DLC)**. Nanodiamonds are tiny, naturally occurring "LEGO skyscrapers" of just a few nanometers in height.

This analogy captures the essence of diamondoid nanomaterials: **they are nature's hardest material scaled down to the molecular limit**, retaining extraordinary mechanical, thermal, and optical properties.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Key benchmark values (memorize for KTU exams):
> - **C–C bond length in diamond:** **1.54 Å (0.154 nm)**
> - **Tetrahedral bond angle:** **109.47°**
> - **Diamond lattice constant (a):** **3.567 Å (0.3567 nm)**
> - **sp³ hybridization fraction in nanodiamond:** **> 90 %**
> - **Hardness of bulk diamond:** **~ 90–100 GPa (Vickers)**
> - **Band gap of diamond:** **5.47 eV (indirect)**
> - **Density of bulk diamond:** **3.515 g/cm³**

### 1.4 Classification Snapshot

| Family | Dimensionality | Typical Size | sp³ Content | Synthesis |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Molecular Diamondoids | 0D (molecule) | 0.5 – 1 nm | 100 % | Petroleum refining, chemical synthesis |
| Nanodiamonds (DND / ND) | 0D (particle) | 1 – 100 nm | 80 – 95 % | Detonation, HPHT, laser ablation |
| Diamond-Like Carbon (DLC) | 2D (film) / 3D (coating) | Film thickness 1 nm – µm | 30 – 85 % | PVD, CVD, PLD, ion beam deposition |
| Single-Wall Diamondoid Rods | 1D | Tens of nm | ~ 100 % | Surface-assisted synthesis |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Hierarchy of Diamondoid Systems

The diamondoid family is best understood as a *hierarchy of sp³ carbon networks*:

- **Step 1 – The Unit:** A single carbon atom is sp³ hybridized → four equivalent sp³ orbitals → bonds with four neighbours at the tetrahedral angle of **109.47°**.
- **Step 2 – The Cage:** Ten carbon atoms form the smallest possible diamond cage → **adamantane (C₁₀H₁₆)**, the structural "unit cell" of the diamondoid family.
- **Step 3 – The Cluster:** Fusing two adamantane units face-to-face creates **diamantane (C₁₄H₂₀)**. Fusing three yields **triamantane (C₁₈H₂₄)**, and so on.
- **Step 4 – The Crystal:** Continuing the fusion indefinitely in 3D gives the bulk **diamond lattice** (Fd3̄m space group, 8 atoms per conventional cubic unit cell).
- **Why this matters:** Diamondoids are *finite fragments* of an infinite diamond lattice. Their electronic, optical, and chemical properties therefore approach those of bulk diamond as their size grows, but quantum confinement effects become significant for the smallest members.

### 2.2 Why sp³ Bonding Produces Extraordinary Properties

- **Hardness:** The 3D tetrahedral network of strong σ bonds (C–C bond energy ≈ **347 kJ/mol**) resists plastic deformation.
- **High thermal conductivity:** Phonons travel efficiently through the rigid sp³ network; bulk diamond has **k ≈ 2200 W/m·K**, the highest of any natural solid.
- **Wide band gap:** Pure sp³ carbon has no π electrons → **5.47 eV** indirect band gap → electrically insulating but UV-active.
- **Chemical inertness:** No dangling bonds when fully H-terminated, but surface functional groups (–COOH, –NH₂, –OH) can be engineered for biomedical targeting.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **most frequently tested** in KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examinations for diamondoid nanomaterials. Master these and you cover ~80 % of the numerical marks.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Diamond lattice constant | $a = \dfrac{4}{\sqrt{3}}\, r$ | $r$ = C–C bond length (nm), $a$ in nm | Crystallography, XRD analysis |
| 2 | Atomic packing factor (APF) of diamond | $\text{APF} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,\pi}{16} \approx 0.34$ | Dimensionless | Comparing diamond with FCC, BCC |
| 3 | Number of atoms per unit cell (diamond) | $n = 8$ | – | Density calculation |
| 4 | Theoretical density of diamond | $\rho = \dfrac{n M}{N_A a^{3}}$ | $M$ = 12.011 g/mol, $N_A$ = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹, $a$ in cm | Mass–volume scaling for NDs |
| 5 | Quantum confinement energy (particle in a box) | $E_n = \dfrac{n^{2} h^{2}}{8 m^{*} L^{2}}$ | $L$ = particle diameter, $m^{*}$ = effective mass | Band gap opening in nanodiamonds |
| 6 | sp³ fraction estimate (Raman ID/IG) | $f_{sp^{3}} = A - B \cdot (I_{D}/I_{G})$ | Empirical A, B constants | DLC film quality |
| 7 | Debye temperature (diamond) | $\theta_{D} = \dfrac{h \nu_{D}}{k_{B}}$ | $\nu_{D}$ = Debye frequency | Thermal property prediction |
| 8 | Surface-to-volume ratio (sphere) | $\text{S/V} = \dfrac{6}{d}$ | $d$ = diameter in nm | Catalysis, drug loading |
| 9 | Phonon mean free path scaling | $\Lambda \propto d^{\alpha}$, $\alpha \approx 1$ | $d$ in nm | Thermal conductivity of NDs |
| 10 | Young's modulus estimate (rule of mixtures, DLC on substrate) | $E_{c} = E_{s} V_{s} + E_{DLC} V_{DLC}$ | $V$ = volume fraction | Tribological coating design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU answer sheets, **always state units** in the final line of any numerical problem. Examiners routinely deduct **0.5 mark** for missing units.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of Diamondoid Nanomaterials

- **Tribology & Wear-Resistant Coatings:** DLC films on cutting tools, engine components, and magnetic hard disks reduce friction coefficient to **µ ≈ 0.05–0.1** (compared to 0.5 for steel-steel).
- **Biomedical Imaging & Drug Delivery:** Fluorescent nanodiamonds (FNDs) containing **nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers** are non-bleaching, photostable, and biocompatible, used in single-particle tracking and intracellular thermometry.
- **Quantum Sensing:** NV centers in nanodiamond act as single-spin sensors for magnetic field, temperature, and pH at the nanoscale (sensitivity ≈ **nT/√Hz**).
- **Electronics:** Diamondoid molecules (adamantane) serve as molecular building blocks in single-molecule transistors and self-assembled monolayers (SAMs).
- **Energy Storage:** Nanodiamond–graphene composites and DLC-coated anodes improve cycle life in Li-ion batteries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Lattice Constant of Diamond from C–C Bond Length

> The diamond cubic lattice can be described as two interpenetrating FCC sublattices offset by (¼, ¼, ¼). The C–C bond connects an atom on one sublattice to its nearest neighbour on the other.

**Step 1:** Identify the bond vector.
The nearest-neighbour vector between the two FCC sublattices is

$$
\vec{r} = \left(\tfrac{a}{4},\; \tfrac{a}{4},\; \tfrac{a}{4}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** The bond length $r$ is the magnitude of this vector.

$$
r = \sqrt{\left(\tfrac{a}{4}\right)^{2} + \left(\tfrac{a}{4}\right)^{2} + \left(\tfrac{a}{4}\right)^{2}}
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the square root.

$$
r = \sqrt{3 \cdot \tfrac{a^{2}}{16}} = \tfrac{a}{4} \sqrt{3}
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $a$.

$$
a = \dfrac{4 r}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $r = 0.154$ nm.

$$
a = \dfrac{4 \times 0.154}{1.732} = \dfrac{0.616}{1.732} \approx 0.3567 \text{ nm}
$$

This matches the experimentally measured **3.567 Å** for natural diamond.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This derivation is the foundation of XRD peak indexing. If you know the (hkl) reflection and the C–C bond length, you can predict every diffraction angle. KTU examiners frequently use this to test understanding of cubic crystal geometry.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Density of Bulk Diamond

**Step 1:** Use the mass-per-unit-cell relation.

$$
\rho = \dfrac{n M}{N_{A} a^{3}}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify the parameters.

- Number of atoms per conventional unit cell: $n = 8$
- Molar mass of carbon: $M = 12.011$ g/mol
- Avogadro's number: $N_{A} = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹
- Lattice constant: $a = 3.567 \times 10^{-8}$ cm

**Step 3:** Compute $a^{3}$.

$$
a^{3} = (3.567 \times 10^{-8})^{3} = 4.536 \times 10^{-23} \text{ cm}^{3}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the numerator.

$$
n M = 8 \times 12.011 = 96.088 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**Step 5:** Plug in.

$$
\rho = \dfrac{96.088}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 4.536 \times 10^{-23}}
$$

$$
\rho = \dfrac{96.088}{27.32} \approx 3.517 \text{ g/cm}^{3}
$$

This is in excellent agreement with the experimentally measured **3.515 g/cm³**, validating the diamond cubic structure model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** For nanodiamond particles, replace bulk $a$ with the lattice constant of the ND crystallite. If the ND has a **lattice contraction** of ~1 % due to surface stress, the calculated density will be ~3 % higher. Mention this in your answer to fetch full marks.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Quantum Confinement Shift in Nanodiamond Band Gap

> Nanodiamonds below ~5 nm exhibit a measurable **band-gap widening** because the electron-hole pair is spatially confined.

**Step 1:** Use the effective mass approximation (particle in a sphere).

$$
\Delta E \approx \dfrac{h^{2}}{8 m^{*} L^{2}}
$$

where $h$ is Planck's constant, $m^{*}$ is the effective mass, and $L$ is the particle diameter.

**Step 2:** Insert values for diamond.

- $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- $m^{*} = 0.2\, m_{0} = 0.2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg (electron effective mass in diamond)
- $m_{h}^{*} \approx 0.3\, m_{0}$ for hole (take reduced mass $\mu \approx 0.12\, m_{0}$ for exciton)

Let $L = 3$ nm $= 3 \times 10^{-9}$ m, $\mu = 0.12 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} = 1.093 \times 10^{-31}$ kg.

**Step 3:** Numerator.

$$
h^{2} = (6.626 \times 10^{-34})^{2} = 4.390 \times 10^{-67} \text{ J}^{2}\text{s}^{2}
$$

**Step 4:** Denominator.

$$
8 \mu L^{2} = 8 \times 1.093 \times 10^{-31} \times (3 \times 10^{-9})^{2}
$$

$$
= 8 \times 1.093 \times 10^{-31} \times 9 \times 10^{-18} = 7.870 \times 10^{-48}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute $\Delta E$.

$$
\Delta E = \dfrac{4.390 \times 10^{-67}}{7.870 \times 10^{-48}} = 5.578 \times 10^{-20} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 6:** Convert to eV (1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J).

$$
\Delta E = \dfrac{5.578 \times 10^{-20}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 0.348 \text{ eV}
$$

So a 3 nm nanodiamond should have a band gap ≈ **5.47 + 0.35 ≈ 5.82 eV**, a measurable blue shift compared to bulk diamond.

> [!NOTE]
> This quantum confinement is one of the most popular 7-mark questions in KTU Module 3. The derivation above is sufficient to score full marks.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Diamondoid Families

| Property | Molecular Diamondoid (Adamantane) | Nanodiamond (DND, 5 nm) | DLC Film (ta-C) | Bulk Diamond |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| sp³ content | 100 % | 80 – 95 % | 60 – 85 % | 100 % |
| H content | High (C₁₀H₁₆) | Surface H | Often H-free | 0 |
| Band gap (eV) | ~ 6–7 (HOMO-LUMO) | 5.5 – 5.9 | 1.0 – 2.5 | 5.47 |
| Hardness (GPa) | N/A (molecular) | 30 – 90 | 20 – 80 | 90 – 100 |
| Density (g/cm³) | ~ 1.07 (solid) | 3.0 – 3.5 | 1.8 – 3.0 | 3.515 |
| Thermal conductivity (W/m·K) | ~ 0.2 | 5 – 100 | 1 – 10 | 2200 |
| Synthesis cost | Low (petroleum) | Moderate (detonation) | Low (PVD/CVD) | Very high (HPHT) |
| Typical KTU exam context | "molecular building block" | "biomedical quantum probe" | "tribological coating" | "reference standard" |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Schematic — Hierarchical Architecture of Diamondoid Nanomaterials

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Carbon Atom sp3 Hybridized] --> B[Adamantane C10H16 Molecule]
```
