> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams in your KTU answer book:** Always start with a brief one-line caption above the figure, label all axes/blocks, and end with a concluding sentence that ties the diagram back to the question stem. This alone can earn **1–2 extra marks** in the ESE.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
Define diamondoid nanomaterials. List any two members of the molecular diamondoid family with their chemical formulae.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Diamondoid nanomaterials are carbon-based nanostructures composed of sp³-hybridized carbon atoms arranged in a tetrahedral network identical to bulk diamond, restricted to the nanometer scale. The family includes molecular diamondoids (H-terminated diamond cages), nanodiamonds, and diamond-like carbon (DLC) films.
- **Examples (1 mark):**
  1. **Adamantane — C₁₀H₁₆** (smallest diamond cage, 1 nm).
  2. **Diamantane — C₁₄H₂₀** (two fused adamantane cages, ~1.2 nm).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
Differentiate between nanodiamond and diamond-like carbon (DLC) in terms of structure and sp³ content.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Nanodiamond (ND) | Diamond-Like Carbon (DLC) |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Crystalline diamond core (Fd3̄m) | Amorphous or quasi-amorphous |
| sp³ content | 80 – 95 % | 30 – 85 % (varies with H content) |
| Form | Discrete particles (1 – 100 nm) | Continuous film / coating |
| Synthesis | Detonation, HPHT, laser ablation | PVD, CVD, FCVA |
| Typical use | Bio-imaging, drug delivery, quantum sensing | Tribological coatings, hard-disk overcoats |

(1 mark for definition of ND, 1 mark for DLC, 1 mark for the differentiating table — examiners love tabular answers.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Attempt **any ONE** of the two full questions.

#### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With neat sketches, explain the structure of adamantane. Discuss why adamantane is considered the "unit cell" of the molecular diamondoid family. List any four properties of adamantane relevant to nanotechnology.

**(b) [7 marks)** Describe the detonation synthesis of nanodiamonds. Mention the typical particle size, sp³ content, and two methods of surface functionalization used to make them biocompatible.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Adamantane structure and properties (7 marks):**
- **Structure (3 marks):** Adamantane (C₁₀H₁₆) has a cage-like topology in which **four CH groups** are at the corners of a tetrahedron and **six CH₂ groups** bridge them. The 10 carbon atoms sit at the vertices of the smallest possible diamondoid cage, with each carbon sp³ hybridized and bonded to four neighbours. Every C–C bond length is **1.54 Å** and every bond angle is **109.47°**, exactly as in bulk diamond.
- **"Unit cell" argument (2 marks):** The adamantane cage is the smallest sub-fragment of the diamond lattice that, when fused, can generate the entire diamond structure. Diamantane = 2 fused adamantanes; triamantane = 3 fused; hence adamantane is the *molecular building block* of all higher diamondoids and of bulk diamond itself.
- **Four properties (2 marks):** (i) High thermal stability up to ~ 500 °C, (ii) chemically inert due to full H-termination, (iii) rigid cage geometry ideal for SAM formation, (iv) can be functionalized at bridgehead positions (1, 3, 5, 7) for drug delivery.

**(b) Detonation synthesis and functionalization of nanodiamonds (7 marks):**
- **Detonation process (4 marks):** An oxygen-deficient mixture of **TNT (trinitrotoluene)** and **RDX (cyclotrimethylenetrinitramine)** is detonated inside a sealed steel chamber filled with inert gas or water cooling medium. The explosion produces a transient **high-pressure (~ 20–30 GPa), high-temperature (~ 3000–4000 K)** environment in which carbon from the explosives condenses into nanoscale diamond crystallites. After the detonation soot is collected, it is purified with strong oxidizing acids (e.g., HNO₃/HClO₄ or H₂SO₄/HNO₃) to remove metallic impurities and non-diamond carbon. The product is **detonation nanodiamond (DND)** with typical particle size **4–5 nm**, sp³ content **80–95 %**, and a primary aggregate size of ~ 100–200 nm.
- **Surface functionalization (3 marks):**
  1. **Air oxidation / acid treatment** → introduces **–COOH** groups → water-dispersible, suitable for further amide coupling.
  2. **Amination** using NH₃ plasma or silanization with **APTES (3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane)** → introduces **–NH₂** groups → enables conjugation with proteins, antibodies, and drugs.
  3. (Bonus) **Hydrogenation** → produces H-terminated hydrophobic ND for oil-phase applications.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the theoretical density of bulk diamond from first principles. Given C–C bond length = 0.154 nm, show all intermediate steps and state the final value in g/cm³.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the quantum confinement effect in nanodiamonds. Using the particle-in-a-sphere model, calculate the band-gap widening for a 3 nm nanodiamond. Comment on its significance for UV optoelectronic devices.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Density derivation (7 marks):** Refer to **Section 3.2** of these notes for the full 5-step derivation. Key points expected by the examiner:
- Diamond cubic has **8 atoms per conventional unit cell** (4 atoms × 2 FCC sublattices).
- $a = \dfrac{4r}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.154}{1.732} = 0.3567$ nm $= 3.567 \times 10^{-8}$ cm.
- $a^{3} = 4.536 \times 10^{-23}$ cm³.
- $\rho = \dfrac{8 \times 12.011}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 4.536 \times 10^{-23}} \approx 3.515$ g/cm³. **(1 mark for each correctly labelled step, 1 mark for the final numerical answer with units, 1 mark for the conclusion.)**

**(b) Quantum confinement in nanodiamonds (7 marks):**
- **Concept (3 marks):** When the nanodiamond diameter $L$ becomes comparable to or smaller than the exciton Bohr radius of diamond (≈ 1.6 nm), the electron and hole are spatially confined. The energy levels quantize and the band gap $E_{g}$ widens according to $\Delta E \approx \dfrac{h^{2}}{8 \mu L^{2}}$, where $\mu$ is the reduced effective mass. This phenomenon is called **quantum confinement**.
- **Calculation (3 marks):** For $L = 3$ nm, $\mu = 0.12\, m_{0}$ (Section 3.3 of notes):
  - Numerator $h^{2} = 4.39 \times 10^{-67}$ J²s²
  - Denominator $8 \mu L^{2} = 7.87 \times 10^{-48}$ J·s²/m²
  - $\Delta E = 5.58 \times 10^{-20}$ J $\approx 0.35$ eV
  - New band gap $E_{g} = 5.47 + 0.35 \approx 5.82$ eV.
- **Significance for UV optoelectronics (1 mark):** Nanodiamonds with sub-5 nm diameters can be tuned into the deep-UV range (~ 5.8 eV ≈ 213 nm), opening applications in **UV-LEDs, photodetectors, and room-temperature single-photon sources for quantum cryptography**.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in this topic (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Confusing nanodiamond with DLC.** Examiners report 30 % of scripts swap these. Always state: *nanodiamond = crystalline; DLC = amorphous*.
> 2. **Forgetting units.** Numerical answers without units lose **0.5 mark** per sub-part (≈ 1 mark per full question).
> 3. **Skipping the tetrahedral angle 109.47°** in adamantane structure questions. The 109.47° is treated as a "compulsory mention" by KTU examiners.
> 4. **Writing "adamantane is diamond."** It is *not* diamond; it is the **molecular precursor / smallest cage fragment** of the diamond lattice. Word this correctly.
> 5. **Omitting surface functionalization** in nanodiamond biomedical questions. Any 7-mark question on bio-applications of ND that fails to mention –COOH or –NH₂ functionalization will lose **at least 2 marks**.
> 6. **Mixing up detonation soot purification** — many students write "HCl wash only." The correct answer requires **strong oxidizing acid mixtures (HNO₃ + H₂SO₄ or HClO₄)** to remove sp² carbon.
> 7. **Sign errors in quantum confinement derivation** — students often write $h/8$ instead of $h^{2}/8$. Re-derive before the exam.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Diamondoid nanomaterials = sp³ carbon nanostructures (molecular diamondoids, nanodiamonds, DLC).
- **Smallest molecular diamondoid:** Adamantane **C₁₀H₁₆**, ~ 1 nm.
- **Key constants:** C–C bond = **1.54 Å**, tetrahedral angle = **109.47°**, $a$ = **3.567 Å**, $E_{g}$ = **5.47 eV**, $\rho$ = **3.515 g/cm³**.
- **Hierarchy:** Carbon → Adamantane → Diamantane → … → Nanodiamond → Bulk diamond.
- **DND synthesis:** Detonation of TNT/RDX in O-deficient sealed chamber → 4–5 nm crystallites → acid purification.
- **DLC sp³ content:** 30–85 % depending on deposition (a-C, ta-C, a-C:H).
- **Density formula:** $\rho = \dfrac{n M}{N_{A} a^{3}}$ → $n=8$ for diamond.
- **Lattice constant formula:** $a = \dfrac{4r}{\sqrt{3}}$.
- **Quantum confinement shift:** $\Delta E = \dfrac{h^{2}}{8 \mu L^{2}}$ → ~ 0.35 eV for 3 nm ND.
- **NV centers** in nanodiamonds → quantum sensing, bio-imaging.
- **Engineering uses:** Tribological coatings, bio-probes, drug delivery, UV-optoelectronics, energy storage.
- **Don't forget:** units, tetrahedral angle, functional groups, and the crystalline-vs-amorphous distinction.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU exam ritual:** Re-draw the adamantane cage, write down the three formulas ($a$, $\rho$, $\Delta E$) on a single A4 sheet, and recite the seven diamondoid constants above. This 15-minute ritual is sufficient to attempt any Part A or Part B question on Diamondoid Nanomaterials with confidence.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carbon Dots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET868 - NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Carbon Dots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carbon Dots (CDs / CQDs):** Carbon Dots are a novel class of **zero-dimensional (0D) carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials** with sizes typically **below 10 nm**, composed primarily of an *sp²/sp³* hybridised carbon core surrounded by surface functional groups (–OH, –COOH, –NH₂, C=O). They exhibit strong **size- and surface-state-dependent photoluminescence (PL)**, high aqueous solubility, low cytotoxicity, excellent chemical inertness, and facile surface functionalizability — making them a versatile alternative to conventional semiconductor quantum dots (e.g., CdSe, CdTe) which contain toxic heavy metals.

According to the KTU 2024 PEMET868 syllabus framework, CDs are classified as a frontier "next-generation carbon nanomaterial" sitting alongside **fullerenes, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs)** in the broader carbon nano-family.

| Sub-Class | Distinguishing Feature |
|-----------|------------------------|
| **Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs)** | Clearly evident quantum confinement; quasi-spherical |
| **Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs)** | Single/few-layer graphene fragments; anisotropic |
| **Carbon Nanodots (CNDs)** | Predominantly sp³ carbon, amorphous, weak QCE |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Glowing Nano-Confetti"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine taking a tiny piece of burnt toast (carbon), grinding it down to a particle **80,000× smaller than the width of a human hair**, and then chemically treating its surface with –OH and –COOH "decorations." Under UV light, this nano-particle glows in brilliant blue, green, yellow, or red — much like **glow-in-the-dark stickers** used by children, but at a scale a billion times smaller. These luminous nano-confetti can be injected into cells to light them up, mixed into inks for anti-counterfeit labels, or used to detect toxic metal ions in drinking water by switching off their glow.

### 1.3 Historical Origin

| Year | Milestone |
|------|-----------|
| **2004** | Serendipitous discovery by **Xu et al.** during electrophoretic purification of SWCNTs |
| **2006** | Sun et al. coined the term *"Carbon Dots"* after laser ablation of carbon |
| **2010–2015** | Hydrothermal / microwave "green synthesis" routes emerged |
| **2015–Present** | Multi-disciplinary applications: bioimaging, LEDs, photocatalysis, theranostics |

### 1.4 Standard Nanoscale Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Critical Physical Constants for CDs:
> - **Typical diameter:** $\mathbf{1 - 10\ \text{nm}}$
> - **Lattice spacing (graphitic):** $\mathbf{0.18 - 0.24\ \text{nm}}$
> - **Specific Surface Area (BET):** $\mathbf{300 - 1500\ \text{m}^2/\text{g}}$
> - **Quantum Yield (QY):** $\mathbf{2\% - 90\%}$ (varies with synthesis)
> - **Largest emission window:** $\mathbf{400 - 800\ \text{nm}}$
> - **Density:** $\mathbf{\sim 1.5 - 2.0\ \text{g/cm}^3}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Carbon Dot

Carbon dots possess a multi-tiered architecture that dictates their photophysical behaviour:

**Tier 1 — Carbon Core:**
- Crystalline (graphitic *sp²*) **OR** amorphous (*sp³*) carbon framework
- Lattice fringes corresponding to the **(100) in-plane** and **(002) basal-plane** of graphite observed under HR-TEM
- Inter-layer spacing typically $\sim 0.34$ nm (similar to bulk graphite)

**Tier 2 — Surface Functional Groups:**
- Oxygen-containing: –OH, –C=O, –COOH, epoxy
- Nitrogen-containing (after doping): –NH₂, pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, graphitic-N
- These groups act as **surface-state traps** that dominate PL emission

**Tier 3 — Passivation / Polymer Shell (optional):**
- PEG, PEI, PAMAM, silica — improves QY and biocompatibility

### 2.2 Photoluminescence Mechanism — "The Four-State Model"

The PL mechanism of CDs is still actively researched; however, the KTU-recommended model involves four coexisting pathways:

1. **Quantum Confinement Effect (QCE):** As particle size approaches the **exciton Bohr radius** of carbon, the bandgap widens, producing size-dependent emission. Smaller dots → blue-shift.
2. **Surface State Emission:** Functional groups create mid-gap electronic states → excitation-dependent emission (red-shift with increasing excitation wavelength).
3. **Molecular Fluorophore Emission:** Small organic fluorophores (e.g., citrazinic acid derivatives) embedded in/on the carbon matrix emit independently.
4. **Crosslink-Enhanced Emission (CEE):** Polymer-like crosslinked subdomains suppress non-radiative vibration, enhancing emission.

### 2.3 Synthesis Paradigm — Top-Down vs Bottom-Up

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU Mental Model:** "Top-Down" = **breaking** a bulk carbon source into nano-pieces. "Bottom-Up" = **building** nano-dots atom-by-atom from molecular precursors.

#### A. Top-Down (Physical Fragmentation)
- **Laser Ablation:** High-power pulsed laser strikes a carbon target submerged in solvent; CDs form in solution.
- **Arc Discharge:** By-product of CNT synthesis; CDs isolated chromatographically.
- **Electrochemical Oxidation:** Graphite rod anode oxidised in ionic liquid / aqueous electrolyte → CDs peeled off into solution.
- **Chemical Oxidation:** Strong acids (HNO₃, H₂SO₄) oxidise soot, activated carbon, or candle wax.

#### B. Bottom-Up (Molecular Assembly)
- **Hydrothermal / Solvothermal:** Carbon precursors (citric acid, glucose, urea) + water/organic solvent, heated in autoclave at 150–250 °C for 3–12 h. *Most common lab method.*
- **Microwave-Assisted:** Rapid dielectric heating (seconds to minutes); energy-efficient.
- **Pyrolysis / Thermal Decomposition:** Carbonisation of organic molecules at 200–400 °C in open air.
- **Plasma Treatment:** Atmospheric-pressure plasma jets convert precursors into CDs.

#### C. Green Synthesis (Bio-precursor route)
- CDs derived from **tea leaves, orange juice, egg-shell membrane, milk, honey, biomass waste** — eco-friendly, low-cost, highly favoured in KTU project evaluations.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table for KTU ESE — these are the only equations repeatedly tested for the Carbon Dots topic.**

| # | Formula / Equation | LaTeX Expression | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Optical Bandgap from Absorption Onset | $E_g = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{onset}} = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda_{onset}\ (\text{nm})}$ | Converts absorption edge to bandgap energy | eV |
| 2 | Quantum Yield (QY) — Relative Method | $\Phi_{CDs} = \Phi_{ref}\ \cdot\ \dfrac{I_{CDs}}{I_{ref}}\ \cdot\ \dfrac{A_{ref}}{A_{CDs}}\ \cdot\ \dfrac{\eta_{CDs}^2}{\eta_{ref}^2}$ | Photonic emission efficiency vs reference dye (e.g., Quinine Sulphate) | dimensionless (%) |
| 3 | Stokes Shift | $\Delta\tilde{\nu} = \tilde{\nu}_{abs} - \tilde{\nu}_{em} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{abs}} - \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{em}}$ | Energy loss between absorption and emission peak | cm⁻¹ |
| 4 | Quantum Confinement (Effective Mass Approx.) | $E_g^{nano} = E_g^{bulk} + \dfrac{h^2}{8\mu R^2}$ | Bandgap widens as particle radius $R$ shrinks | eV |
| 5 | BET Surface Area | $S_{BET} = \dfrac{N_A\ \cdot\ V_m\ \cdot\ \sigma}{V_{molar}}$ | Quantifies accessible surface for catalysis/sensing | m²/g |
| 6 | Urbach Energy (Disorder) | $\alpha(h\nu) = \alpha_0\ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{h\nu}{E_U}\right)$ | Measures structural disorder in carbon core | meV |
| 7 | Size from XRD Scherrer | $D = \dfrac{K\lambda}{\beta\cos\theta}$ | Crystallite size from (002) peak broadening | nm |
| 8 | Stern-Volmer (Sensing) | $\dfrac{F_0}{F} = 1 + K_{SV}\ [Q]$ | Quenching constant for ion/molecule detection | M⁻¹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the **quantitative backbone** of any CD characterisation report. In research, they bridge synthesis (size) ↔ optical property (bandgap) ↔ application (sensitivity, catalytic rate, imaging contrast).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Quantum Yield by Relative Method

**Scenario (typical KTU numerical):** A carbon dot sample absorbs at 360 nm and emits at 450 nm. Using **Quinine Sulphate in 0.1 M H₂SO₄** as the reference ($\Phi_{ref} = 0.54$ at 360 nm), the following data is collected:

| Parameter | Quinine Sulphate (ref) | Carbon Dot Sample |
|-----------|------------------------|--------------------|
| Integrated emission intensity $I$ | 50,000 | 78,000 |
| Absorbance at 360 nm $A$ | 0.10 | 0.08 |
| Solvent refractive index $\eta$ | 1.33 | 1.33 |

**Step 1 — Write the governing relative QY equation:**
$$\Phi_{CDs} = \Phi_{ref}\ \cdot\ \dfrac{I_{CDs}}{I_{ref}}\ \cdot\ \dfrac{A_{ref}}{A_{CDs}}\ \cdot\ \dfrac{\eta_{CDs}^2}{\eta_{ref}^2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values systematically:**

Because the solvent is identical (water), $\eta_{CDs}^2 / \eta_{ref}^2 = 1$. So the equation simplifies to:
$$\Phi_{CDs} = \Phi_{ref}\ \cdot\ \dfrac{I_{CDs}}{I_{ref}}\ \cdot\ \dfrac{A_{ref}}{A_{CDs}}$$

$$\Phi_{CDs} = 0.54\ \cdot\ \dfrac{78000}{50000}\ \cdot\ \dfrac{0.10}{0.08}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate ratio 1 (Intensity ratio):**
$$\dfrac{78000}{50000} = 1.56$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate ratio 2 (Absorbance ratio):**
$$\dfrac{0.10}{0.08} = 1.25$$

**Step 5 — Multiply all three factors:**
$$\Phi_{CDs} = 0.54 \times 1.56 \times 1.25$$

$$0.54 \times 1.56 = 0.8424$$

$$0.8424 \times 1.25 = 1.053$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result:**
$$\boxed{\Phi_{CDs} \approx 1.053 \rightarrow \text{physically clamped to } 1.00\ (\text{100\%})}$$

The "anomalous" $>1$ value arises because Quinine Sulphate is not a perfect reference; in practice, the student should report $\Phi_{CDs} \approx 100\%$ with a remark about reference calibration error. *(A real KTU answer expects the student to compute 1.05 and comment that it exceeds unity, indicating either experimental error or superior photon emission characteristics.)*

---

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Bandgap from Absorption Edge

**Given:** Absorption onset $\lambda_{onset} = 420$ nm (from UV-Vis Tauc plot).

**Step 1 — Apply the bandgap formula:**
$$E_g = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda_{onset}\ (\text{nm})}\ \text{eV}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$E_g = \dfrac{1240}{420} = 2.952\ \text{eV}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Joules (1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J):**
$$E_g = 2.952 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$$

$$2.952 \times 1.602 = 4.729$$

$$E_g = 4.729 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J} \approx \mathbf{4.73 \times 10^{-19}\ J}$$

---

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Quantum Confinement Radius Estimation

**Given:** Bulk graphite bandgap $E_g^{bulk} \approx 0$ eV (semi-metal), and a CD sample shows bandgap $E_g^{nano} = 2.5$ eV with reduced effective mass $\mu = 0.05\ m_e$ (where $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, Planck constant $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s).

**Step 1 — Write the effective-mass equation:**
$$E_g^{nano} = E_g^{bulk} + \dfrac{h^2}{8\mu R^2}$$

Since $E_g^{bulk} \approx 0$:
$$E_g^{nano} = \dfrac{h^2}{8\mu R^2}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $R^2$:**
$$R^2 = \dfrac{h^2}{8\mu\ E_g^{nano}}$$

**Step 3 — Convert $E_g^{nano}$ to Joules:**
$$E_g^{nano} = 2.5 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 4.005 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\mu$:**
$$\mu = 0.05 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} = 4.555 \times 10^{-32}\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $h^2$:**
$$h^2 = (6.626 \times 10^{-34})^2 = 4.390 \times 10^{-67}\ \text{J}^2\text{s}^2$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into $R^2$ equation:**
$$R^2 = \dfrac{4.390 \times 10^{-67}}{8 \times 4.555 \times 10^{-32} \times 4.005 \times 10^{-19}}$$

Denominator: $8 \times 4.555 \times 10^{-32} \times 4.005 \times 10^{-19} = 1.4595 \times 10^{-49}$

$$R^2 = \dfrac{4.390 \times 10^{-67}}{1.4595 \times 10^{-49}} = 3.008 \times 10^{-18}\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 7 — Take the square root:**
$$R = \sqrt{3.008 \times 10^{-18}} = 1.734 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m} \approx \mathbf{1.73\ nm}$$

**Diameter** $D = 2R \approx \mathbf{3.46\ nm}$ — consistent with typical CD size.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — CDs vs Other Nanomaterials

| Property | Carbon Dots | CdSe Quantum Dots | Organic Dyes | Graphene Oxide |
|----------|-------------|--------------------|---------------|------------------|
| **Toxicity** | Very Low | High (Cd²⁺) | Low–Moderate | Low |
| **Photostability** | High | High | Low (photobleach) | Moderate |
| **QY Range (%)** | 2–90 | 30–90 | 20–90 | 1–20 |
| **Size (nm)** | 1–10 | 2–15 | ~1 (molecular) | 100–1000 |
| **Cost (per g)** | Low | Very High | Moderate | Low |
| **Biocompatibility** | Excellent | Poor–Moderate | Moderate | Good |
| **Tunable Emission** | Yes (size/surface) | Yes (composition) | Limited | Limited |
| **Synthesis Complexity** | Simple | Complex (inert atm) | Simple | Simple |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Take-Away Matrix Insight:** Carbon Dots offer the **best balance of low toxicity, high photostability, low cost, and tunable emission** — this is precisely why KTU examiners emphasise CDs as a sustainable alternative to toxic QDs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`
> **Define Carbon Dots. Mention any two distinguishing properties that make them superior to traditional semiconductor quantum dots.**

**Model Answer:**
Carbon Dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with sizes below 10 nm, composed of an *sp²/sp³* carbon core decorated with polar surface functional groups.

**Two distinguishing properties:**
1. **Low Toxicity & High Biocompatibility:** Unlike CdSe / CdTe quantum dots which release toxic Cd²⁺ ions, CDs are largely non-toxic and FDA-friendly, enabling safe in-vivo bioimaging.
2. **Cheap & Facile Synthesis:** CDs can be synthesised from inexpensive bio-precursors (glucose, citric acid, fruit extracts) via simple hydrothermal methods without inert atmosphere, in contrast to heavy-metal QDs requiring glove-box organometallic synthesis.

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`
> **Differentiate between top-down and bottom-up synthesis of carbon dots. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Top-Down | Bottom-Up |
|--------|----------|------------|
| Principle | Bulk carbon fragmented into nano-pieces | Molecular precursors carbonised atom-by-atom |
| Energy Source | Mechanical / electrical / laser | Thermal / chemical |
| Size Control | Moderate (post-synthesis purification) | Good (precursor ratio tuning) |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Example | Laser ablation of graphite in PEG solvent | Hydrothermal treatment of citric acid + ethylenediamine at 200 °C for 5 h |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Question A.** `[KTU ESE Module 3 - Choice A]`
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss in detail the **photoluminescence mechanisms** of carbon dots. Explain the role of **quantum confinement** and **surface states** with suitable equations.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Describe the **hydrothermal synthesis** of carbon dots from citric acid and ethylenediamine. Discuss how reaction parameters (temperature, time, precursor ratio) affect the optical properties.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

**Photoluminescence (PL) Mechanisms of Carbon Dots:**

**(i) Quantum Confinement Effect (QCE):**
When the CD particle radius approaches the exciton Bohr radius of carbon, electrons and holes are spatially confined, widening the effective bandgap.

$$E_g^{nano} = E_g^{bulk} + \dfrac{h^2}{8\mu R^2}$$

A smaller particle (smaller $R$) → larger bandgap → emission shifts to **shorter (blue) wavelengths**. Larger particles emit **red light**.

**(ii) Surface State Emission:**
Functional groups (–COOH, –C=O, –NH₂) introduce mid-gap electronic energy levels. Electrons excited to the conduction band relax non-radiatively into these surface traps, then emit at a lower energy.

$$\Delta\tilde{\nu}_{Stokes} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{abs}} - \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{em}}$$

The result is **excitation-wavelength-dependent emission** — a unique fingerprint of CDs.

**(iii) Molecular Fluorophore Emission:**
Small organic molecules (e.g., citrazinic acid, IPCA) formed *in situ* during carbonisation emit independently of the carbon core.

**(iv) Crosslink-Enhanced Emission (CEE):**
Sub-vibrational crosslinked polymer-like domains fix fluorophores in rigid environments, suppressing non-radiative decay and boosting QY.

**Conclusion:** The simultaneous operation of these mechanisms makes CDs **multicolour-tunable** without changing chemical composition.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**Hydrothermal Synthesis of CDs from Citric Acid + Ethylenediamine (EDA):**

**Procedure:**
1. Dissolve **1 g citric acid** and **0.5 mL EDA** in 30 mL deionised water.
2. Transfer to **Teflon-lined autoclave** (50 mL capacity).
3. Heat at **200 °C for 5 h** in an oven.
4. Cool naturally to room temperature.
5. Filter through **0.22 μm membrane** to remove large aggregates.
6. **Dialyse** (MWCO 500–1000 Da) against deionised water for 24 h.
7. Freeze-dry to obtain solid CD powder.

**Effect of Reaction Parameters:**

| Parameter | Effect on CD Properties |
|-----------|--------------------------|
| Temperature (150–250 °C) | Higher T → larger carbonisation degree → red-shift in emission |
| Time (3–12 h) | Longer time → more surface groups → higher QY (up to a saturation point) |
| Citric Acid : EDA Ratio | Higher amine ratio → nitrogen-doping → blue-shift, increased QY (>80%) |
| pH | Acidic conditions enhance –COOH passivation → improved water solubility |

**Optical characterisation typically yields:** UV-Vis absorption at $\sim 340$ nm (n–π* of C=O), PL emission at $\sim 440$ nm (blue), QY $\sim 80\%$.

---

> **Question B.** `[KTU ESE Module 3 - Choice B]`
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **structural model** of carbon dots. Describe the role of **surface functional groups** in determining photoluminescence.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss any **four major applications** of carbon dots in bioimaging, sensing, photocatalysis, and LED fabrication, with mechanism for each.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

**Structural Model of Carbon Dots:**

CDs possess a **three-tier architecture:**

**Tier 1 — Carbon Core:**
Composed of *sp²* graphitic nanocrystallites (with lattice spacing $\sim 0.34$ nm corresponding to (002) graphite plane) embedded in an *sp³* amorphous carbon matrix. HR-TEM images show clear lattice fringes in crystalline regions.

**Tier 2 — Surface Functional Groups:**
Pendant groups include:
- Oxygen: –OH, –COOH, C=O, epoxy (from incomplete carbonisation)
- Nitrogen: –NH₂, pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, graphitic-N (after doping)
- Sulphur / Phosphorus (after doping)

**Tier 3 — Passivation Shell (optional):**
Polymer (PEG, PEI) or silica coating that enhances QY and colloidal stability.

**Role of Surface Groups in PL:**

1. They create **mid-gap surface states** that act as radiative recombination centres.
2. They determine the **HOMO–LUMO gap** of the trap states, controlling emission wavelength.
3. **Nitrogen doping** introduces pyrrolic-N which narrows the bandgap → red-shift.
4. **Oxidation degree** correlates positively with QY up to an optimum.
5. Surface groups enable **solvatochromic behaviour** — emission shifts with solvent polarity.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**Four Major Applications of Carbon Dots:**

**(i) Bioimaging:**
CDs are excited with UV/blue light and emit visible fluorescence. Their nano-size allows **cellular internalisation via endocytosis**. Surface functionalisation with folic acid / RGD peptides enables **targeted cancer-cell imaging**. Emission in the red/NIR region enables **deep-tissue in-vivo imaging** with reduced auto-fluorescence background.

**(ii) Chemical Sensing (Metal Ions / Biomolecules):**
CDs serve as fluorescent probes. In presence of analytes (e.g., Fe³⁺, Hg²⁺, glucose), PL is **quenched** via static / dynamic quenching, photo-induced electron transfer (PET), or inner-filter effect. Sensitivity quantified by Stern-Volmer equation:

$$\dfrac{F_0}{F} = 1 + K_{SV}[Q]$$

High $K_{SV}$ values indicate strong sensor-analyte interaction.

**(iii) Photocatalysis:**
CDs act as visible-light photocatalysts due to their broad absorption and tuneable bandgap. They generate **electron-hole pairs** under illumination:
- Electrons reduce O₂ → •O₂⁻ (superoxide)
- Holes oxidise H₂O → •OH (hydroxyl radical)
These radicals degrade organic pollutants (rhodamine B, methylene blue) into CO₂ + H₂O. CDs also catalyse **water splitting** (H₂ evolution) and **CO₂ reduction**.

**(iv) LED Fabrication:**
CDs are dispersed in a polymer matrix (PMMA, PVK) and used as **colour-conversion layers** on blue LED chips. Blue light excites CDs → down-converted white / yellow / red emission. Multi-colour CD-LEDs are fabricated by **size/doping-engineering** the CD emission peak.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Carbon Dots Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing GQDs and CDs:** GQDs have anisotropic, layered graphene-like structure with lateral dimensions; CDs are quasi-spherical. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for interchanging.
> 2. **Forgetting to cite specific examples:** Saying "carbon source" without naming citric acid, glucose, urea loses a mark.
> 3. **Skipping the equation in QCE questions:** Always write the **effective-mass formula** and substitute numerical values.
> 4. **Wrong units in QY calculation:** QY is **dimensionless** — never write "litres" or "mol⁻¹".
> 5. **Missing QY reference dye:** When asked about QY measurement, you MUST name the reference (Quinine Sulphate, Rhodamine 6G).
> 6. **PL mechanism oversimplification:** Avoid saying "CDs glow because they're small." Always invoke the **4-state model**.
> 7. **Synthesis classification error:** Microwave, hydrothermal, and pyrolysis are **bottom-up**; do NOT classify them as top-down.
> 8. **Lack of diagrams:** In 14-mark answers, a labelled CD architecture diagram earns at least 1–2 marks even if text is incomplete.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Carbon Dots:**
> - **Definition:** 0D fluorescent carbon nanoparticles, **< 10 nm**, with *sp²/sp³* core + surface functional groups.
> - **Discovery:** Xu et al., **2004** (electrophoretic purification of SWCNTs).
> - **Types:** CQDs (sp², QCE), GQDs (graphene fragments), CNDs (sp³, amorphous).
> - **Top-Down methods:** Laser Ablation, Arc Discharge, Electrochemical Oxidation, Acid Oxidation.
> - **Bottom-Up methods:** Hydrothermal, Solvothermal, Microwave, Pyrolysis, Plasma, Green Synthesis.
> - **Most common lab route:** Hydrothermal (citric acid + ethylenediamine, 200 °C, 5 h).
> - **PL mechanism:** Quantum Confinement + Surface States + Molecular Fluorophore + CEE.
> - **Key equations:** $E_g = 1240/\lambda_{onset}$ (eV); Effective-mass confinement; Stokes shift; Stern-Volmer quenching; Scherrer formula for size.
> - **QY reference dyes:** Quinine Sulphate (0.1 M H₂SO₄, $\Phi = 0.54$); Rhodamine 6G ($\Phi = 0.95$).
> - **Lattice spacing:** ~0.34 nm (002 graphite plane); size confirmed by HR-TEM & XRD.
> - **Advantages over CdSe QDs:** Non-toxic, cheap, eco-friendly, biocompatible, photostable.
> - **Major applications:** Bioimaging, Chemical Sensing, Photocatalysis, LEDs, Drug Delivery, Solar Cells, Anti-counterfeiting.
> - **Highest reported QY:** ~90% (N-doped, surface-passivated CDs).
> - **Typical UV-Vis peak:** ~340 nm (n–π* of C=O); emission blue ~440 nm (excitation-dependent).
> - **Sensing mechanism:** PL quenching (static / dynamic / PET / IFE) — quantified via Stern-Volmer plot.
> - **Photocatalysis active species:** •O₂⁻ and •OH radicals from photo-generated e⁻/h⁺ pairs.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pyrolyzed nanocarbon materials** are a class of carbon-based nanostructures that are synthesized through **pyrolysis** — the thermal decomposition of carbon-rich organic precursors in an inert (oxygen-free) or vacuum atmosphere, typically at temperatures ranging from **400 °C to 1200 °C**. The process drives off volatile species (H₂O, CO₂, CH₄, H₂, tars) and leaves behind a carbon-rich solid residue that can self-organize into nanocarbon allotropes such as **carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon nanofibers (CNFs), graphene, carbon nanohorns, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), onion-like carbon, and glassy (vitreous) carbon**.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context (PEMET868), pyrolyzed nanocarbons are positioned as the bridge between **organic precursors** and **advanced sp²/sp³ hybrid functional nanomaterials** used in energy storage, catalysis, sensors, and biomedical applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Pyrolysis is a *top-down* thermal conversion route that bypasses the need for exotic high-pressure/high-temperature plasma systems. It is a low-cost, scalable, and precursor-flexible method to engineer nanocarbons.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **roasting coffee beans at home**. The raw green beans (organic precursor) go into a hot pan (inert furnace). As the temperature rises, water vapour, CO₂, and aromatic vapours escape (volatiles) — the kitchen fills with the aroma of pyrolysis. What remains is a dark, brittle, carbon-rich bean (the pyrolyzed residue) whose **flavour (property) depends entirely on the temperature, time, and atmosphere** you used.

Now scale this idea down to the **nanoscale**:
- The "roasted residue" is not amorphous char — under carefully tuned conditions, the carbon atoms **self-assemble** into highly ordered nanostructures (CNTs, graphene).
- The "roasting recipe" (temperature ramp, dwell time, catalyst, carrier gas) determines **which nanocarbon you get**.

> [!NOTE]
> Think of pyrolysis as a "nanocarbon kitchen" — the precursor is your ingredient, the furnace is your oven, and the recipe (T, t, catalyst) decides whether you bake a **nanotube cake**, a **graphene wafer**, or a **quantum-dot cookie**.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Pyrolyzed Nanocarbons

| Class | Typical Pyrolysis T (°C) | Key Precursor Examples | Distinguishing Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Carbon Black (CB)** | 1200 – 1800 | Hydrocarbons, oil | Spherical nanoparticles, 10–500 nm |
| **Carbon Nanofibers (CNF)** | 500 – 1000 | PAN, pitch, CH₄ | Stacked graphene cones, 50–500 nm dia. |
| **Carbon Nanotubes (CNT)** | 600 – 1000 | C₂H₂, CH₄, CO | Cylindrical sp² tubes, 1–100 nm dia. |
| **Graphene / Graphene Oxide** | 400 – 1000 | GO, sucrose, polymers | 2D sp² sheet, monolayer to few-layer |
| **Carbon Quantum Dots (CQD)** | 200 – 400 | Citric acid, urea, glucose | <10 nm, photoluminescent |
| **Glassy / Vitreous Carbon** | 1000 – 3000 | Polymer resins | Amorphous, impermeable, glass-like |
| **Carbon Nanohorns** | 800 – 1000 | CO laser ablation of graphite | Horn-shaped single-wall aggregates |
| **Onion-like Carbon** | 1500 – 2000 | Nanodiamond annealing | Concentric fullerene shells |

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Pyrolysis Atmospheres:** Ar, N₂, He (inert); 5–10% H₂/Ar (reducing)
- **Carbonization Temperature Range:** **400 °C – 1500 °C** (graphitization extends to **>2500 °C**)
- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- **Carbon–Carbon Bond Length (graphite):** **0.142 nm** (aromatic C=C)
- **sp²/sp³ Hybridization Ratio:** A key quality metric; higher sp² → better electrical conductivity
- **ID/IG Ratio (Raman):** Disorder indicator; **lower = more graphitic**
- **Yield of Pyrolysis:** Typically **20–50 %** for biomass, up to **>80 %** for synthetic polymers like PAN

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Pyrolysis Process — Stepwise Mechanism

Pyrolysis is not a single reaction — it is a **sequence of overlapping physicochemical stages**. The student must internalize these stages for both theory and ESE questions.

### Stage 1 — Drying (T < 150 °C)
- Free and bound water is driven off.
- Endothermic; governed by heat-transfer rate, not chemistry.

### Stage 2 — Devolatilization / Carbonization (150 °C – 700 °C)
- Weak C–C, C–H, C–O bonds break via **free-radical chain scission**.
- Volatiles: CH₄, H₂, CO₂, H₂O, tars.
- The remaining solid becomes **carbon-rich** (mass drops sharply).

### Stage 3 — Aromatization / Graphitization (700 °C – 1500 °C)
- Residual free radicals recombine into **aromatic (sp²) clusters**.
- Clusters coalesce and stack into nanocrystalline graphite domains.
- Above ~2200 °C: full graphitization to AB-stacked graphite.

### Stage 4 — Catalyst-Assisted Growth (when metal catalyst present)
- For CNTs/CNFs, transition-metal nanoparticles (Fe, Co, Ni) act as **C-dissolving seeds**.
- Carbon atoms dissolve into the metal, supersaturate, and precipitate as tubular/fibrous carbon.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Every property of the final nanocarbon (graphitic order, defect density, morphology) is **frozen in** at the peak pyrolysis temperature. Cooling must therefore be controlled to avoid thermal-stress defects.

---

## 2.2 Why Pyrolysis Produces Nanostructures (The "How" Behind It)

Three governing phenomena make the nanoscale self-assembly possible:

1. **Nucleation–Growth Kinetics:** At the carbonization front, supersaturation of carbon atoms in the metal catalyst drives heterogeneous nucleation. The **Gibbs free energy barrier** for forming a graphitic nucleus is small (~few eV), favouring the formation of many small nuclei — hence nanometre dimensions.

2. **Surface Energy Minimization:** Curved sp² sheets (CNTs, fullerenes) lower their total energy by satisfying dangling bonds; this favours tubular/spherical morphologies over flat sheets when curvature-inducing defects (pentagons) are present.

3. **Vapor–Liquid–Solid (VLS) Growth:** In CVD-pyrolysis with metal catalysts, the catalyst nanoparticle behaves like a **liquid carbon sink**. Carbon from the pyrolysis gas dissolves, saturates, and extrudes a graphitic tube behind it as the catalyst lifts off the substrate.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the formulas below — they form the quantitative backbone of any ESE numerical/model question on pyrolyzed nanocarbons.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $k = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | **Arrhenius** rate constant for pyrolysis; governs carbonization kinetics | k in s⁻¹, Ea in J·mol⁻¹ |
| 2 | $Y_{\text{pyrolysis}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{residue}}}{m_{\text{initial}}} \times 100\%$ | **Pyrolysis mass yield** | dimensionless (%) |
| 3 | $C_{\text{at\%}} = \dfrac{n_C}{n_C + n_H + n_O + n_N} \times 100\%$ | Atomic carbon content (CHNS analysis) | at.% |
| 4 | $G_{r} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{\Delta t}$ | **CVD/CNT growth rate** (length per unit time) | nm·min⁻¹ or µm·s⁻¹ |
| 5 | $I_{D}/I_{G} = \dfrac{\text{intensity at }\sim 1350\ \text{cm}^{-1}}{\text{intensity at }\sim 1580\ \text{cm}^{-1}}$ | **Raman disorder index** — quality metric | dimensionless |
| 6 | $I_{2D}/I_{G}$ ratio | Layer count estimator for **graphene** (>2 = monolayer; ~0.5 = bulk graphite) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\rho_{\text{theor}} = \dfrac{4 M}{N_A a^2 c \sqrt{3}}$ | Theoretical density of **graphite** (M = 12.01 g/mol) | g·cm⁻³ |
| 8 | $\sigma = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ (with $\rho$ in Ω·m) | Electrical **conductivity** of pyrolyzed carbon (10² – 10⁴ S·m⁻¹) | S·m⁻¹ |
| 9 | $E_{g}(\text{CQD}) \approx \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{\text{em}}}$ | Optical band gap of **carbon quantum dots** from PL emission | eV |
| 10 | $\Delta G = \gamma \cdot dA - \Delta \mu \cdot dn$ | Nucleation free energy (curvature-driven CNT formation) | J |

**Boundary Conditions & Practical Values:**

- $E_a$ for cellulose pyrolysis: **150 – 220 kJ·mol⁻¹**
- $E_a$ for PAN carbonization: **180 – 200 kJ·mol⁻¹**
- Raman D-band: **~1350 cm⁻¹**, G-band: **~1580 cm⁻¹**, 2D-band: **~2700 cm⁻¹**
- Graphite interlayer spacing (c/2): **0.335 nm**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pyrolyzed nanocarbons are **workhorse materials** in the modern technology stack:

- **Lithium-ion batteries:** Si/C pyrolyzed composite anodes (2024 commercial state of art).
- **Supercapacitors:** Activated CNF electrodes achieving **>300 F·g⁻¹** specific capacitance.
- **Electrocatalysis:** N-doped pyrolyzed CNFs for ORR (Oxygen Reduction Reaction) in fuel cells — replacing Pt.
- **EMI shielding:** Pyrolyzed carbon foams with >60 dB shielding effectiveness.
- **Biomedical:** Fluorescent CQDs for bio-imaging and drug delivery.
- **Water purification:** Pyrolyzed carbon nano-adsorbents for heavy-metal removal.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Arrhenius-Based Pyrolysis Rate Law

We start with the **first-order decomposition** assumption — valid for most organic precursors during primary pyrolysis:

$$
\frac{dm}{dt} = -k \, m
$$

where $m$ is the residual mass of the precursor at time $t$, and $k$ is the rate constant. Substituting the Arrhenius form $k = A \exp(-E_a / RT)$:

$$
\frac{dm}{dt} = -A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) \, m
$$

Separating variables:

$$
\frac{dm}{m} = -A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) \, dt
$$

Integrating from $m_0$ to $m(t)$ and from $0$ to $t$:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{m(t)}{m_0}\right) = -A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) \, t
$$

Equivalently, the **fractional mass loss** (volatilization) is:

$$
1 - \frac{m(t)}{m_0} = 1 - \exp\!\left[-A t \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)\right]
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This equation is the foundation of **TGA (thermogravimetric analysis)** interpretation. By plotting $\ln k$ vs $1/T$ at multiple temperatures, the slope yields $-E_a/R$, giving the **activation energy** of pyrolysis.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Carbon Yield from Precursor Stoichiometry

Consider a generic precursor $\text{C}_x \text{H}_y \text{O}_z$ (e.g., glucose: $C_6H_{12}O_6$, $x=6, y=12, z=6$). The pyrolysis half-reaction (ideal carbonization, oxygen leaves as CO/CO₂/H₂O) is:

$$
\text{C}_x \text{H}_y \text{O}_z \xrightarrow{\Delta,\ \text{inert}} \text{C}_{\text{solid}} + \text{volatiles}
$$

The **theoretical maximum carbon yield** is:

$$
Y_{\text{theor}} = \frac{x \cdot M_C}{x \cdot M_C + y \cdot M_H + z \cdot M_O} \times 100\%
$$

Substituting $M_C = 12.01$, $M_H = 1.008$, $M_O = 16.00$:

**For glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{\text{theor}} &= \frac{6 \times 12.01}{6 \times 12.01 + 12 \times 1.008 + 6 \times 16.00} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{72.06}{72.06 + 12.10 + 96.00} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{72.06}{180.16} \times 100\% \\
&= 39.99\% \approx 40.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The numerator counts only the **mass of carbon atoms retained** in the solid; the denominator is the **total precursor molar mass**. This is the upper bound — actual yields are 10–30% lower because some carbon escapes as CO/CO₂/CH₄.

**For PAN (C₃H₃N, acrylonitrile unit):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{\text{theor}} &= \frac{3 \times 12.01}{3 \times 12.01 + 3 \times 1.008 + 1 \times 14.01} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{36.03}{36.03 + 3.02 + 14.01} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{36.03}{53.06} \times 100\% \\
&= 67.91\% \approx 67.9\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Why PAN gives a higher yield:** PAN contains nitrogen instead of oxygen; nitrogen leaves as HCN/NH₃, allowing more carbon to remain in the solid residue. This is why PAN is the **industrial carbon-fiber precursor**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: CNT Growth Rate from CVD Kinetics

In **CVD pyrolysis**, the axial growth rate of a CNT follows the **carbon flux** through the catalyst particle:

$$
G_r = \frac{J_C \cdot M_C}{\rho_{\text{CNT}} \cdot \pi d}
$$

where:

- $J_C$ = carbon flux into the catalyst (mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹)
- $M_C$ = 12.01 g·mol⁻¹
- $\rho_{\text{CNT}}$ = density of graphitic carbon (~2.26 g·cm⁻³)
- $d$ = outer diameter of the growing CNT (m)

The flux $J_C$ is governed by the **Fick's first law** across the catalyst:

$$
J_C = -D_C \frac{\partial C_C}{\partial x}
$$

For a hemispherical catalyst of radius $r$, steady-state diffusion gives:

$$
J_C = \frac{D_C (C_s - C_{eq})}{r}
$$

Combining:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_r &= \frac{D_C (C_s - C_{eq}) \cdot M_C}{\rho_{\text{CNT}} \cdot \pi d \cdot r} \\
&= \frac{D_C \cdot \Delta C \cdot M_C}{\rho_{\text{CNT}} \cdot \pi d \cdot r}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** This relation tells us that **smaller catalyst particles** (smaller $r$) and **larger carbon supersaturation** ($\Delta C$) accelerate CNT growth. It is the kinetic basis of "fast growth" CVD protocols reaching **>100 µm·min⁻¹**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Raman $I_D/I_G$ — Quality Metric

The Tuinstra–Koenig relation gives the **in-plane crystallite size** $L_a$ of pyrolyzed carbon:

$$
L_a \, (\text{nm}) = \frac{C(\lambda)}{I_D/I_G}
$$

where $C(\lambda) \approx 4.4$ nm at 514.5 nm excitation. **Rearranging:**

$$
\frac{I_D}{I_G} = \frac{C(\lambda)}{L_a}
$$

**Numerical Example:** A pyrolyzed CNF sample exhibits $I_D/I_G = 0.85$. Calculate $L_a$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_a &= \frac{4.4\ \text{nm}}{0.85} \\
&= 5.18\ \text{nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

A crystallite of ~5 nm is typical of **partially graphitic turbostratic carbon**, indicating medium-quality pyrolyzed material.

> [!NOTE]
> **A subtle correction:** The Tuinstra–Koenig relation works for $L_a > 2$ nm. For smaller crystallites (e.g., amorphous carbon), the **Ferrari–Robertson** model (with $I_D/I_G \propto L_a^2$) must be used.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Energy Balance in a Pyrolysis Reactor

For a **batch pyrolysis reactor** with mass $m_p$ of precursor, the energy balance reads:

$$
Q_{\text{in}} = m_p c_p (T_f - T_i) + \Delta H_{\text{pyr}} m_p + Q_{\text{loss}}
$$

Rearranging for **furnace power** $P$ over time $t$:

$$
P \cdot t = m_p \, c_p (T_f - T_i) + m_p \, \Delta H_{\text{pyr}} + (U A) \int_0^t (T_f - T_{\text{amb}})\, dt'
$$

For an **adiabatic** wall (perfect insulation, $Q_{\text{loss}} = 0$):

$$
P_{\text{req}} = \frac{m_p c_p (T_f - T_i) + m_p \Delta H_{\text{pyr}}}{t}
$$

**Numerical example:** Pyrolyze 1 kg of PAN, $T_i = 25$ °C = 298 K, $T_f = 1000$ °C = 1273 K, $c_p = 1.2$ kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹, $\Delta H_{\text{pyr}} = 300$ kJ·kg⁻¹, $t = 3600$ s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{req}} &= \frac{1 \times 1.2 \times (1273 - 298) + 1 \times 300}{3600} \\
&= \frac{1.2 \times 975 + 300}{3600} \\
&= \frac{1170 + 300}{3600} \\
&= \frac{1470}{3600} \\
&= 0.408\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

A **408 W** furnace is sufficient for this scale — illustrating the **low-energy footprint** of pyrolysis versus arc-discharge or laser-ablation CNT synthesis (which require kW–MW scale).

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Apr 2024 Model Question]**

### Q1. Define pyrolysis. List any four nanocarbon materials produced by pyrolysis.

**Model Answer (≈ 5 lines):**

Pyrolysis is the **thermal decomposition of carbon-rich organic precursors in an inert atmosphere (Ar, N₂, or vacuum)** at elevated temperatures (typically 400 °C – 1200 °C) in the absence of oxygen, yielding a solid carbonaceous residue and volatile by-products.

Four nanocarbons produced:

1. **Carbon nanotubes (CNTs)**
2. **Carbon nanofibers (CNFs)**
3. **Graphene / graphene oxide (GO)**
4. **Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)**

*(1 + 4 × 0.5 = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. What is the significance of the $I_D/I_G$ ratio in Raman spectroscopy of pyrolyzed nanocarbons?

**Model Answer:**

The $I_D/I_G$ ratio is the **intensity of the disorder-induced D-band (~1350 cm⁻¹) relative to the graphitic G-band (~1580 cm⁻¹)** in the Raman spectrum. It is a **quantitative metric of graphitic order / defect density** in pyrolyzed carbons.

- A **low $I_D/I_G$** (< 0.5) → highly graphitic, well-ordered (high-quality CNT, graphene).
- A **high $I_D/I_G$** (> 1.0) → disordered, amorphous (e.g., glassy carbon, defective CNF).

It directly correlates with the **in-plane crystallite size** $L_a$ via the Tuinstra–Koenig relation $L_a \propto 1/(I_D/I_G)$. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — University Model Question, Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of pyrolysis** with its four characteristic stages. Indicate the temperature range and the dominant physicochemical process at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Arrhenius-based first-order rate law** for pyrolysis, and explain how it is used experimentally to determine the **activation energy** $E_a$ from TGA data. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks

The pyrolysis mechanism proceeds in **four overlapping stages**, each governed by distinct chemistry and physics:

**Stage 1 — Drying (T < 150 °C):**
- Removal of free and adsorbed moisture.
- Endothermic; rate limited by **heat transfer**, not chemical reaction.
- *(1 mark)*

**Stage 2 — Devolatilization / Primary Carbonization (150 °C – 500 °C):**
- Weak **C–C, C–H, C–O** bonds break via free-radical chain scission.
- Major volatile release: **H₂O, CO, CO₂, light hydrocarbons (CH₄, C₂H₄), tars**.
- Solid residue becomes carbon-rich; mass drops ~30–50 %.
- *(1.5 marks)*

**Stage 3 — Aromatization / Secondary Carbonization (500 °C – 900 °C):**
- Residual radicals recombine to form **aromatic 6-membered rings**.
- Aromatic clusters **coalesce and stack** into nanocrystalline graphite domains (turbostratic carbon).
- Heteroatoms (N, S, O) leave as HCN, NH₃, H₂S, etc.
- *(1.5 marks)*

**Stage 4 — Graphitization (> 900 °C, optimally > 2000 °C):**
- Aromatic planes **grow in lateral size** and align into AB-stacked graphite.
- $sp^3$ defects anneal out; $sp^2$ fraction rises.
- The **ID/IG ratio** decreases monotonically.
- *(1.5 marks)*

A clean schematic (the Mermaid flow diagram in Section 4.1) earns an additional **0.5 mark**.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Assume first-order decomposition** (justified empirically for most organic precursors):

$$
\frac{dm}{dt} = -k \, m
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Arrhenius form** $k = A \exp(-E_a / RT)$:

$$
\frac{dm}{dt} = -A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) \, m
$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate:**

$$
\int_{m_0}^{m} \frac{dm'}{m'} = -A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) \int_0^t dt'
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{m}{m_0}\right) = -A t \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Linearize for $E_a$ extraction:**

$$
\ln\!\left(-\frac{\ln(m/m_0)}{t}\right) = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{RT}
$$

**Step 5 — Experimental TGA protocol:** Heat the precursor at several constant temperatures $T_i$ and record mass vs time. For each $T_i$, fit a straight line to $\ln(m/m_0)$ vs $t$ → slope $= -A \exp(-E_a/RT_i)$.

**Step 6 — Arrhenius plot:** Plot $\ln(\text{slope})$ vs $1/T_i$. The slope of this line $= -E_a/R$ → gives $E_a$. The intercept gives $\ln A$. *(2 marks for derivation, 2 marks for TGA application, 1 mark for diagram, 1 mark for numerical example if requested)*

**Numerical illustration:** From TGA, slopes at $T_1 = 600$ °C (873 K) and $T_2 = 800$ °C (1073 K) are $5.2 \times 10^{-4}$ s⁻¹ and $4.1 \times 10^{-3}$ s⁻¹.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_a &= R \cdot \frac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{(1/T_1) - (1/T_2)} \\
&= 8.314 \times \frac{\ln(4.1/0.52)}{(1/873) - (1/1073)} \\
&= 8.314 \times \frac{\ln(7.885)}{0.001145 - 0.000932} \\
&= 8.314 \times \frac{2.065}{0.000213} \\
&= 8.314 \times 9694.8 \\
&= 80{,}595\ \text{J·mol}^{-1} \approx 80.6\ \text{kJ·mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is consistent with $E_a$ values typical of biomass/pyrolyzed polymer carbonization (60 – 220 kJ·mol⁻¹).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Describe the **Vapor–Liquid–Solid (VLS) mechanism** of CNT growth during CVD pyrolysis. Differentiate between **tip-growth** and **base-growth** modes with neat sketches. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PAN-based carbon-fiber precursor is pyrolyzed at 1000 °C. Starting from 1 kg of PAN $(C_3H_3N)$, calculate: (i) the theoretical maximum carbon yield, (ii) the actual yield if $I_D/I_G = 0.9$ and crystallite size $L_a$ is found to be 4.9 nm. Comment on the quality of the pyrolyzed product. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**VLS Mechanism (5 stages):**

1. **Precursor decomposition:** Hydrocarbon gas (C₂H₂, CH₄, CO) decomposes catalytically on the surface of a transition-metal nanoparticle (Fe, Co, Ni) at 600 – 900 °C.
2. **Carbon dissolution:** Carbon atoms dissolve into the metal nanoparticle, forming a **liquid-like C–M alloy** (the "L" of VLS).
3. **Supersaturation:** Continued carbon influx drives the alloy composition above the **carbon solubility limit** at that temperature.
4. **Precipitation:** Excess carbon precipitates as a **graphitic layer** at the catalyst–substrate (or catalyst–vapor) interface.
5. **Extrusion:** The graphitic sheet rolls into a tube and lifts the catalyst — extruding a **CNT** of length proportional to growth time. *(4 marks)*

**Tip-growth vs Base-growth (3 marks):**

| Feature | Tip-Growth | Base-Growth |
|---|---|---|
| Catalyst–substrate adhesion | **Weak** | **Strong** |
| Catalyst location | At the **tip** of the growing CNT | At the **base**, anchored to substrate |
| Growth direction | Catalyst lifts off; tube grows downward into vapor | Tube extrudes upward; catalyst fixed |
| Typical support | Smooth SiO₂, Si | Al₂O₃, porous supports |

A labelled schematic (VLS block diagram in Section 4.3) earns the remaining method mark.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**Part (i) — Theoretical Carbon Yield of PAN:** (already derived in Section 3.2)

$$
Y_{\text{theor}} = \frac{3 \times 12.01}{3 \times 12.01 + 3 \times 1.008 + 14.01} \times 100\% = 67.9\%
$$

Therefore, the maximum mass of solid carbon from 1 kg PAN:

$$
m_{\text{C,theor}} = 1.000 \times 0.679 = 0.679\ \text{kg} = 679\ \text{g}
$$

**Part (ii) — Quality assessment from Raman:**

Given $I_D/I_G = 0.9$ and the Tuinstra–Koenig relation $L_a = 4.4 / (I_D/I_G)$:

$$
L_a = \frac{4.4}{0.9} = 4.89\ \text{nm} \approx 4.9\ \text{nm} \quad \checkmark
$$

The given $L_a$ matches the Raman $I_D/I_G$, confirming **self-consistency** of the measurement.

**Comment on quality:**

- $I_D/I_G = 0.9$ is **moderate-to-high**, indicating a **partially disordered graphitic structure**.
- $L_a = 4.9$ nm corresponds to a **turbostratic nanocrystalline** carbon — typical of **low-to-intermediate temperature (≤ 1000 °C) PAN carbonization**.
- To improve graphitic order ($I_D/I_G \to 0.3$, $L_a \to 30$ nm), the material must undergo **high-temperature graphitization at ≥ 2000 °C** (e.g., in a graphite furnace or by Joule heating).
- The 32 % mass loss from theoretical (100 % − 67.9 %) suggests that **heteroatom (N) loss and tar evolution** are still ongoing at 1000 °C — heat treatment above 1500 °C would further densify the carbon. *(2 marks for numerical computation, 2 marks for quality commentary, 1 mark each for theoretical derivation and conclusion)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Pyrolysis Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating "carbonization" and "graphitization."** Carbonization occurs at 400 – 900 °C (mass loss dominated); graphitization requires **> 2000 °C** for crystalline order. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting units in Arrhenius problems.** $E_a$ must be in **J·mol⁻¹** with $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹; if $E_a$ is in kJ·mol⁻¹, you must multiply by 1000.
> 3. **Wrong Raman band assignment.** D = ~1350 cm⁻¹, G = ~1580 cm⁻¹, 2D = ~2700 cm⁻¹. Students frequently swap D and G.
> 4. **No mention of "inert atmosphere."** Pyrolysis is defined by the **absence of oxygen**. Writing "heating in air" or "combustion" is an automatic 0 in the definition question.
> 5. **Ignoring catalyst role in CNT growth.** VLS mechanism is incomplete without naming Fe/Co/Ni as catalysts and specifying the "carbon dissolution–supersaturation–precipitation" triad.
> 6. **Skipping the Raman derivation steps in part (b) numericals.** Examiners expect a clean linearized Arrhenius plot, not just the final $E_a$ value.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Pyrolysis** = thermal decomposition in **inert atmosphere**; *not* combustion.
- **Four stages:** Drying → Devolatilization → Aromatization → Graphitization.
- **Temperature windows:** Carbonization 400 – 900 °C; Graphitization > 2000 °C.
- **Common precursors:** PAN (67.9 % C theoretical yield), glucose (40 %), sucrose, pitch, lignin, biomass.
- **Key products:** CNTs, CNFs, graphene, CQDs, glassy carbon, carbon black, nanohorns.
- **Catalysts for CNT growth:** Fe, Co, Ni (transition metals).
- **VLS mechanism:** decompose → dissolve → supersaturate → precipitate → extrude.
- **Arrhenius equation:** $k = A \exp(-E_a / RT)$ — first-order kinetics.
- **Activation energy range:** 60 – 220 kJ·mol⁻¹ for biomass/polymer pyrolysis.
- **Raman D-band:** ~1350 cm⁻¹; G-band: ~1580 cm⁻¹; 2D-band: ~2700 cm⁻¹.
- **$I_D/I_G$** ↓ → graphitic order ↑; **$I_D/I_G$** ↑ → disorder ↑.
- **Tuinstra–Koenig:** $L_a (\text{nm}) \approx 4.4 / (I_D/I_G)$.
- **Carbon yield formula:** $Y = (m_{\text{residue}} / m_{\text{initial}}) \times 100\%$.
- **Stoichiometric yield:** $Y_{\text{theor}} = (x M_C) / (x M_C + y M_H + z M_O)$.
- **CNT growth rate:** $G_r \propto D_C \cdot \Delta C / r$ — smaller catalyst → faster growth.
- **Carbon applications:** batteries, supercapacitors, ORR catalysis, EMI shielding, bioimaging.
- **Advantages of pyrolysis:** low-cost, scalable, precursor-flexible, no exotic plasma needed.
- **Disadvantages:** high defect density (unless graphitized), broad size distribution, post-treatment purification often required.
- **Key constant to remember:** $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹, $M_C = 12.01$ g·mol⁻¹, graphite $d_{002} = 0.335$ nm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "D-DAG"** for pyrolysis stages: **D**rying → **D**evolatilization → **A**romatization → **G**raphitization. The first three begin with letters that escalate in temperature; "G" marks the high-quality crystalline regime.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emerging 2D Materials: Transition Metal Dichalcogenides
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET868 - NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Emerging 2D Materials: Transition Metal Dichalcogenides

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Transition Metal Dichalcogenides (TMDs)** are a class of layered 2D materials with the general chemical formula **MX₂**, where **M** denotes a transition metal atom from groups 4–10 of the periodic table (e.g., Mo, W, Nb, Re, Ti, Hf) and **X** denotes a chalcogen atom (S, Se, or Te). In each monolayer, a single plane of metal atoms is covalently sandwiched between two planes of chalcogen atoms in an **X–M–X** configuration, while adjacent monolayers are held together by weak **van der Waals (vdW) forces**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** TMDs are positioned as the **post-graphene** class of 2D materials because they offer a tunable, layer-dependent **direct bandgap** in the monolayer limit, which graphene fundamentally lacks. The reference materials are **MoS₂, WS₂, MoSe₂, WSe₂, and MoTe₂**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of a TMD monolayer as a **club sandwich**:
- The two slices of bread on the outside are the **chalcogen (X) layers** — the sulfur/selenium/tellurium atoms.
- The meat/cheese filling in the middle is the **transition metal (M) layer**.
- The bread-meat-bread "sandwich" is held tightly by **covalent bonds**.
- Many such sandwiches can be stacked in a cafeteria counter, but they are merely leaning against each other, held by weak **van der Waals forces**, so you can peel them off one by one — this is **exfoliation**.

> [!NOTE]
> The standard in-plane lattice constant is **a ≈ 0.31–0.33 nm** and the interlayer spacing is **c ≈ 0.6–0.7 nm**, depending on the chalcogen atomic radius.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **In-plane lattice constant (a):** ~0.315 nm (MoS₂), ~0.315 nm (WS₂)
- **Bandgap range:** ~0.5 eV (bulk, indirect) → ~1.8 eV (monolayer, direct)
- **Spin–orbit coupling (SOC) splitting at K-valley:** ~3 meV (MoS₂) up to **~450 meV (WSe₂)**
- **Exciton binding energy (monolayer):** ~0.5 eV (orders of magnitude higher than 3D semiconductors)
- **Carrier mobility (room temperature, monolayer):** ~10–500 cm² V⁻¹ s⁻¹
- **Effective electron mass (MoS₂ monolayer):** ~0.35 m₀
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Crystal Structure and Polymorphism

TMDs exhibit three principal structural phases arising from the coordination geometry of the metal atom:

- **1T phase (octahedral):** D₃d point group; metallic character; thermodynamically stable for group-4 TMDs (TiS₂, ZrS₂).
- **2H phase (trigonal prismatic):** D₃h point group; semiconducting character; thermodynamically stable for group-6 TMDs (MoS₂, WS₂).
- **3R phase (rhombohedral stacking):** Same local coordination as 2H but with three layers per unit cell.

The structural phase can be **reversibly switched** between 2H (semiconducting) and 1T (metallic) via chemical doping, electron-beam irradiation, or alkali-metal intercalation — a property absent in graphene.

> [!NOTE]
> The notation "2H" means **2 layers per hexagonal unit cell**, and "1T" means **1 layer per tetragonal unit cell**.

### 2.2 Band Structure Evolution: Why the Bandgap Changes with Layers

The most celebrated KTU-style high-yield concept is the **direct-to-indirect bandgap crossover** when a bulk TMD is thinned to a monolayer:

- In **bulk 2H-MoS₂**, the valence band maximum (VBM) lies at the Γ-point while the conduction band minimum (CBM) lies midway between Γ and K. This makes the bandgap **indirect (~1.2 eV)**.
- In **monolayer MoS₂**, quantum confinement along the out-of-plane direction lifts the energy of the Γ-valley, pushing the VBM up to the K-point. The direct gap at K is **~1.8 eV**.
- The **K-valley direct gap is largely insensitive to layer number** because the K-point wavefunctions are localized within each layer and have minimal interlayer coupling.

### 2.3 Valley Physics and Spin-Valley Locking

Because the K and K′ valleys in the hexagonal Brillouin zone are related by **time-reversal symmetry** but carry **opposite Berry curvature and opposite spin**, monolayer TMDs exhibit **spin-valley locking**. This is the foundation of **valleytronics** — using the valley pseudospin as an additional degree of freedom for information processing.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | $E_g^{\text{mono}} \approx 1.8 \text{ eV}$ (MoS₂) | Monolayer direct gap | eV |
| 2 | $E_g^{\text{bulk}} \approx 1.2 \text{ eV}$ (MoS₂) | Bulk indirect gap | eV |
| 3 | $\Delta_{\text{SOC}} = \lambda L_z$ | Spin–orbit splitting at K-valley | $\lambda \approx 0.04 \text{ eV (Mo)}, 0.43 \text{ eV (W)}$ |
| 4 | $E_b^{\text{exc}} = \frac{\mu e^4}{2\hbar^2 \epsilon^2} = \frac{4 \mu}{m_0} \cdot \frac{R_y}{\epsilon_r^2}$ | 2D exciton binding energy | 0.3–0.5 eV typical for ML |
| 5 | $a_{\text{exc}} = \frac{\hbar^2 \epsilon_r}{\mu e^2}$ | 2D exciton Bohr radius | ~1 nm in ML TMD |
| 6 | $m^* = \hbar^2 \left( \frac{\partial^2 E}{\partial k^2} \right)^{-1}$ | Effective mass from E–k curve | kg or m₀ units |
| 7 | $E(k) = E_0 \pm \alpha \vert \mathbf{k} \pm \mathbf{K} \vert$ | Linear dispersion near K/K′ | $\alpha \approx 3 \text{ eV·Å}$ (MoS₂) |
| 8 | $R_y = 13.6 \text{ eV}$ | Rydberg constant | Reference value |
| 9 | $\mu = \frac{m_e^* m_h^*}{m_e^* + m_h^*}$ | Reduced mass of exciton | kg |
| 10 | $\Omega(\mathbf{k}) = i \langle \nabla_{\mathbf{k}} u \vert \times \vert \nabla_{\mathbf{k}} u \rangle$ | Berry curvature | Drives valley Hall effect |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** TMDs bridge the gap between zero-bandgap graphene (logic switches with low on/off ratio) and conventional 3D semiconductors. They are the **only known 2D family with intrinsic direct bandgaps in the visible-to-near-IR range**, enabling ultra-thin photodetectors, LEDs, and field-effect transistors (FETs).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Logic transistors (FETs):** Monolayer MoS₂ FETs demonstrate on/off ratios > 10⁸ with sub-nm gate scaling.
- **Photodetectors / Solar cells:** Direct bandgap allows efficient light absorption in atomically thin layers.
- **Catalysis (HER):** Edge sites of 2H-MoS₂ are highly active for the **Hydrogen Evolution Reaction**.
- **Valleytronic memory & logic:** Exploits spin-valley locking at K/K′.
- **Flexible electronics:** Mechanical flexibility arises from strong in-plane bonds.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Monolayer Band Structure from a Three-Band Tight-Binding Model

We derive the low-energy band dispersion of a monolayer MX₂ around the K-point using the **simplified three-orbital (d_z², d_xy, d_x²−y²) tight-binding model** for the transition-metal d-orbitals, as introduced in the foundational framework for MoS₂.

**Step 1 — Identify the basis orbitals and their on-site energies.**
The three relevant transition-metal d-orbitals at each M site, expressed in the basis $\phi = \{ d_{z^2}, d_{xy}, d_{x^2-y^2} \}$, have on-site energies:
- $E_{z^2} = \epsilon_1$ (with $L_z = 0$ character)
- $E_{xy,x^2-y^2} = \epsilon_2$ (with $L_z = \pm 2$ character, Kramers split by SOC)

**Step 2 — Construct the nearest-neighbour hopping Hamiltonian.**
For a triangular lattice of metal atoms, the effective Hamiltonian at the K-point can be reduced to:

$$
H_{\text{TB}} = \begin{pmatrix} \epsilon_1 & 0 & t_0 \\ 0 & \epsilon_2 & t_1 \\ t_0 & t_1 & \epsilon_2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

where $t_0$ and $t_1$ are the hopping amplitudes between the $\ket{d_{z^2}}$–$\ket{d_{x^2-y^2}}$ and $\ket{d_{xy}}$–$\ket{d_{x^2-y^2}}$ orbitals, respectively. These terms reflect intra-layer d–d hopping.

**Step 3 — Diagonalize the 2×2 conduction/valence block.**
The $d_{z^2}$ orbital weakly couples to the conduction band minimum at K. The valence band maximum is dominated by the $d_{xy}$ and $d_{x^2-y^2}$ orbitals, which mix to form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_v^{\pm}(K) &= \frac{\epsilon_2 + \epsilon_2}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left( \frac{\epsilon_2 - \epsilon_2}{2} \right)^2 + t_1^2} \\
&= \epsilon_2 \pm \vert t_1 \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce spin–orbit coupling (intrinsic, atomic-like).**
At the K-point, time-reversal symmetry combined with broken inversion gives a large SOC that splits the valence band:

$$
\Delta E_v^{\text{SOC}} = 2 \lambda_{\text{atom}}
$$

where $\lambda_{\text{atom}}$ is the atomic SOC constant of the metal d-orbital. For W-based TMDs, $\lambda_{\text{atom}} \approx 0.21$ eV, leading to a splitting of $\sim 0.4$ eV, in agreement with ARPES measurements.

**Step 5 — Add the off-resonant coupling to obtain linear dispersion.**
The hybridization with remote $\ket{d_{z^2}}$ orbitals generates a momentum-dependent correction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_v(k) &= E_v^0 + \alpha \cdot \vert \mathbf{k} - \mathbf{K} \vert \\
E_c(k) &= E_c^0 - \alpha' \cdot \vert \mathbf{k} - \mathbf{K} \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

This linear, "Dirac-like" dispersion in the valence band is what makes monolayer TMDs respond to **circularly polarized light (valley-selective optical excitation)** — a result of the optical selection rule at the K and K′ valleys.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Layer-Dependent Bandgap of 2H-MoS₂

| Number of Layers | Bandgap (eV) | Type | VBM Location | CBM Location |
|------------------|--------------|------|--------------|--------------|
| 1 (monolayer) | 1.80 – 1.90 | Direct | K | K |
| 2 | 1.65 | Indirect | Γ | K (midpoint) |
| 3 | 1.35 | Indirect | Γ | K (midpoint) |
| 4 | 1.25 | Indirect | Γ | K (midpoint) |
| Bulk (∞) | 1.20 | Indirect | Γ | midpoint Γ–K |

> [!NOTE]
> The **K-point gap remains almost constant**, while the **Γ-point energy rapidly rises** as the layer count decreases. This is the textbook origin of the direct-to-indirect crossover in KTU question banks.

### 3.3 Numerical Estimate: Exciton Binding Energy in Monolayer MoS₂

We use the 2D hydrogenic exciton model with effective masses $m_e^* \approx 0.35 \, m_0$, $m_h^* \approx 0.45 \, m_0$, and effective dielectric constant $\epsilon_r \approx 4.0$.

**Step 1 — Compute the reduced mass.**

$$
\mu = \frac{m_e^* \cdot m_h^*}{m_e^* + m_h^*} = \frac{0.35 \times 0.45}{0.35 + 0.45} \, m_0 = \frac{0.1575}{0.80} \, m_0 \approx 0.197 \, m_0
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 2D exciton binding-energy formula.**

$$
E_b^{\text{exc}} = \frac{4 \, \mu}{m_0} \cdot \frac{R_y}{\epsilon_r^2}
$$

$$
E_b^{\text{exc}} = 4 \times 0.197 \times \frac{13.6}{(4.0)^2} \text{ eV} = 0.788 \times \frac{13.6}{16} \text{ eV} \approx 0.67 \text{ eV}
$$

This matches the experimentally measured binding energy of ~0.5–0.7 eV for monolayer MoS₂, confirming the strong Coulomb interaction in the 2D limit.

**Step 3 — Compare to 3D bulk MoS₂.**

$$
E_b^{\text{3D}} = \frac{\mu}{m_0} \cdot \frac{R_y}{\epsilon_r^2} = 0.197 \times \frac{13.6}{16} \text{ eV} \approx 0.17 \text{ eV}
$$

The factor-of-**4 enhancement** in the 2D case is a direct consequence of reduced dielectric screening in atomically thin layers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: TMD Monolayer

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **atomic and electronic architecture of a 2H-MoS₂ monolayer** (top view + cross-section + electronic consequence) without requiring a literal atomic drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transition Metal Atom M = Mo] --> B{Chalcogen Coordination}
    B --> C1[Top S Layer X1]
    B --> C2[Bottom S Layer X2]
    C1 --> D[Trigonal Prismatic X-M-X Sandwich]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[2H Phase D3h Symmetry]
    E --> F[In-plane lattice constant a ~ 0.315 nm]
    E --> G[Direct Bandgap ~ 1.8 eV at K Valley]
    G --> H[Valley Selective Optical Transitions]
    H --> I[Spin Valley Locking]
    I --> J[Engineering Applications: FETs LEDs Photodetectors Valleytronics]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Bandgap Evolution from Bulk to Monolayer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N[Bulk MoS2] -->|Layer 1: 1.20 eV indirect| N
    N1[3 Layers] -->|1.35 eV indirect| N1
    N2[2 Layers] -->|1.65 eV indirect| N2
    N3[Monolayer] -->|1.80 eV direct at K| N3
    N --> N1 --> N2 --> N3
    N3 --> KValley[Gamma VBM pushed up by quantum confinement]
    N3 --> KValley2[K valley direct gap stable due to localized d orbitals]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Synthesis Routes and Downstream Applications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Mechanical Exfoliation Scotch Tape Method] --> APP1[Lab Scale Prototypes]
    S2[Liquid Phase Exfoliation] --> APP2[Printed Electronics Inks]
    S3[Chemical Vapor Deposition CVD] --> APP3[Wafer Scale Device Fabrication]
    S4[Atomic Layer Deposition ALD] --> APP4[Conformal Thin Films on 3D Substrates]
    S5[Molecular Beam Epitaxy MBE] --> APP5[Ultra Clean Heterostructures]
    APP1 --> A1[Field Effect Transistors]
    APP2 --> A2[Flexible Sensors]
    APP3 --> A3[Photodetectors LEDs]
    APP4 --> A4[Energy Catalysis]
    APP5 --> A5[Superconductor Interfaces e.g. NbSe2]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Phase Engineering between 2H and 1T

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[2H Phase Trigonal Prismatic] -->|Thermodynamically Stable| P1
    P1 -->|Electron Beam Irradiation| P2[1T Phase Octahedral]
    P1 -->|Alkali Metal Intercalation Li K Na| P2
    P1 -->|Chemical Doping| P2
    P2 -->|Annealing 300 C| P1
    P1 --> Prop1[Semiconducting for Transistors]
    P2 --> Prop2[Metallic for Ohmic Contacts and Catalysis]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. Writing "TMDs are similar to graphene" — partial credit loss. Always highlight the **direct bandgap** and **spin-valley locking** distinction.
> 2. Confusing 1T (metallic) with 2H (semiconducting) phases — both have the same MX₂ stoichiometry but differ in coordination.
> 3. Stating the bandgap of monolayer MoS₂ as "1.2 eV" — that is the *bulk* value. Use **~1.8 eV** for monolayer.
> 4. Forgetting to mention that the K-valley direct gap is **layer-independent** while the indirect gap is layer-dependent.

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**Define Transition Metal Dichalcogenides (TMDs). Why are monolayer TMDs considered superior to graphene for digital logic applications?**

**Model Answer:**
TMDs are layered compounds with the general formula **MX₂**, where M is a transition metal (Mo, W, etc.) and X is a chalcogen (S, Se, Te). Each monolayer consists of an X–M–X covalently bonded sandwich, with adjacent layers held by van der Waals forces.

Monolayer TMDs (e.g., MoS₂) are superior to graphene for digital logic because:

1. Graphene is a **zero-bandgap semimetal**, so its transistors cannot be fully switched off (low on/off ratio ~10).
2. Monolayer MoS₂ has a **direct bandgap of ~1.8 eV**, giving transistor on/off ratios > 10⁸.
3. TMDs possess **spin-valley locking**, enabling valleytronic logic.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for graphene comparison)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**Distinguish between the 1T and 2H phases of MoS₂ with respect to crystal structure and electronic properties.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | 1T Phase | 2H Phase |
|----------|---------|---------|
| Coordination | Octahedral | Trigonal prismatic |
| Point group | D₃d | D₃h |
| Layers/unit cell | 1 | 2 |
| Electronic character | Metallic | Semiconducting |
| Thermodynamic stability | Metastable | Stable (group-6 TMDs) |
| Typical use | Ohmic contacts, HER catalyst | FET channel, optoelectronics |

The 2H → 1T phase transition can be induced by **Li-intercalation** or **electron-beam irradiation**, and is reversible by annealing.

*(3 marks: 1 for structure, 1 for property, 1 for example/transition trigger)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE: Answer either Question A or Question B.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** With a neat band diagram, explain the **direct-to-indirect bandgap crossover** observed in 2H-MoS₂ as the material is thinned from bulk to monolayer. Discuss the role of the Γ and K points and indicate the layer-dependent numerical values of the bandgap. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the 2D exciton binding energy for monolayer MoS₂ given $m_e^* = 0.35 \, m_0$, $m_h^* = 0.45 \, m_0$, and $\epsilon_r = 4.0$. Compare it with the 3D bulk value and comment on the physical origin of the difference. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Bandgap crossover:**
- In **bulk 2H-MoS₂**, VBM lies at Γ (dominated by $d_{z^2}$ with strong interlayer coupling) and CBM lies at the midpoint between Γ and K. This makes the gap **indirect (1.2 eV)**.
- As layers are progressively removed, **interlayer coupling at Γ weakens** and the Γ-point energy rises due to **quantum confinement** along the out-of-plane direction.
- The K-point states, being localized within each layer (mostly $d_{xy}$ and $d_{x^2-y^2}$), are **largely unaffected**.
- For the **monolayer**, the Γ-point is pushed above the K-point, making the gap **direct (1.8 eV)** at K.
- Tabulated values:

| Layers | Gap (eV) | Type |
|--------|----------|------|
| Bulk | 1.20 | Indirect |
| 4 | 1.25 | Indirect |
| 2 | 1.65 | Indirect |
| 1 | 1.80 | Direct |

*(7 marks: 3 for mechanism, 2 for K vs Γ argument, 2 for table)*

**(b) Exciton binding energy derivation:**

Using $R_y = 13.6$ eV and the formula $E_b^{\text{2D}} = 4 \cdot \dfrac{\mu}{m_0} \cdot \dfrac{R_y}{\epsilon_r^2}$:

$$
\mu = \frac{0.35 \times 0.45}{0.35 + 0.45} \, m_0 = 0.197 \, m_0
$$

$$
E_b^{\text{2D}} = 4 \times 0.197 \times \frac{13.6}{(4.0)^2} = 0.67 \text{ eV}
$$

For 3D:

$$
E_b^{\text{3D}} = \frac{\mu}{m_0} \cdot \frac{R_y}{\epsilon_r^2} = 0.197 \times \frac{13.6}{16} = 0.17 \text{ eV}
$$

The 2D value is **~4× larger** because the electric field lines of the Coulomb potential are confined in 2D, leading to unscreened electron–hole interaction. The reduced dielectric environment in a monolayer enhances binding.

*(7 marks: 3 for derivation, 2 for numerical evaluation, 2 for physical explanation)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** What is **valleytronics**? Explain the concept of **valley polarization** and **spin-valley locking** in monolayer MoS₂ with reference to the K and K′ points in the Brillouin zone. Discuss the optical selection rule for valley-selective excitation using circularly polarized light. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List the major **synthesis techniques** for TMD monolayers and explain the **Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD)** method in detail, including precursor chemistry, growth temperature, and substrate considerations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
