# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define the term *Specific Energy* of a battery. How does it differ from *Energy Density*?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Specific Energy** is the total electrical energy stored per unit **mass** of the cell, measured in Wh kg⁻¹ (or Wh g⁻¹). It is computed as E_specific = V_avg × Q_specific.
- **Energy Density** (also called volumetric energy density) is the energy stored per unit **volume**, measured in Wh L⁻¹.
- Both quantify the "amount of energy" available but normalized by different physical attributes (mass vs space). For EVs, specific energy matters more for range; for smartphones, energy density matters more for compactness.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the *Ragone Plot* and why is it useful in selecting an energy-storage device?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The Ragone Plot is a log–log graph of **specific energy (Wh/kg)** on the x-axis versus **specific power (W/kg)** on the y-axis, used to compare different energy-storage technologies.
- It clearly shows the **energy–power tradeoff**: batteries lie in the high-energy / moderate-power region, supercapacitors in the moderate-energy / high-power region, and fuel cells in the high-energy / high-power region.
- It enables engineers to select the appropriate technology for a given application (e.g., hybrid systems combine batteries + supercapacitors for both range and acceleration).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

> **(a)** Derive the **Nernst Equation** starting from the relationship between Gibbs free energy and electrical work. Clearly state the meaning of each term. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A galvanic cell consists of a **Zn** electrode in 0.1 M Zn²⁺ solution and a **Cu** electrode in 1.0 M Cu²⁺ solution at 25 °C. Given E°(Zn²⁺/Zn) = −0.76 V and E°(Cu²⁺/Cu) = +0.34 V, calculate **(i)** E°_cell, **(ii)** ΔG°, and **(iii)** the equilibrium constant K_eq at 25 °C. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of the Nernst Equation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Combine 1st and 2nd Laws of Thermodynamics:**
For a reversible process at constant T and P, the maximum non-PV work equals the decrease in Gibbs free energy:
ΔG = W_elec,max. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Express W_elec:**
When *n* moles of electrons pass through the external circuit at potential E_cell, the charge transferred is nF and the work is:
W_elec = nFE_cell. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Combine:**
ΔG = −nFE_cell. Standard state: ΔG° = −nFE°_cell. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Apply the reaction quotient:**
The general thermodynamic relation: ΔG = ΔG° + RT ln Q. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Substitute both ΔG expressions:**
−nFE_cell = −nFE°_cell + RT ln Q. **(1 mark)**

**Step 6 — Solve for E_cell:**
E_cell = E°_cell − (RT / nF) ln Q. **(1 mark)**

**Step 7 — Convert to base-10 logarithm at 25 °C:**
E_cell = E°_cell − (0.0592 / n) log₁₀ Q. **(1 mark)**

Define: R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹, T = 298.15 K, F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹, n = electrons transferred, Q = reaction quotient (activities of products / reactants).

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:** [Zn²⁺] = 0.1 M, [Cu²⁺] = 1.0 M, T = 298 K, E°(Zn²⁺/Zn) = −0.76 V, E°(Cu²⁺/Cu) = +0.34 V.

**(i) E°_cell (2 marks):**
Reaction: Zn(s) + Cu²⁺ → Zn²⁺ + Cu(s). Cu²⁺ is reduced (cathode), Zn is oxidized (anode).

$$
E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}} = 0.34 - (-0.76) = 1.10\;\text{V}
$$

**(ii) ΔG° (2 marks):** n = 2 electrons.

$$
\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = -(2)(96485)(1.10) = -212{,}267\;\text{J} = -212.3\;\text{kJ mol}^{-1}
$$

The negative ΔG° confirms the reaction is spontaneous.

**(iii) Equilibrium constant K_eq (3 marks):**
At equilibrium, E_cell = 0 and Q = K_eq. From the Nernst equation:

$$
0 = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.0592}{n}\log_{10} K_{\text{eq}}
$$

$$
\log_{10} K_{\text{eq}} = \frac{n \cdot E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}{0.0592} = \frac{2 \times 1.10}{0.0592} = 37.16
$$

$$
K_{\text{eq}} = 10^{37.16} \approx 1.45 \times 10^{37}
$$

The extremely large K_eq confirms the reaction goes essentially to completion.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **Lithium-ion battery**. Discuss the role of **nanostructured electrodes** in enhancing its performance. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **Butler–Volmer equation** and discuss how nanostructuring affects the **exchange current density (j₀)** and **charge-transfer resistance (R_ct)**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Li-ion Battery — Working Principle & Nanostructured Electrodes (7 marks)**

**Working Principle:**
A Li-ion cell consists of:
- **Anode:** Graphite (or Si-based) intercalation host on a Cu current collector.
- **Cathode:** LiCoO₂, LiFePO₄, or NMC layered oxide on an Al current collector.
- **Electrolyte:** LiPF₆ dissolved in a mixture of ethylene carbonate (EC) and dimethyl carbonate (DMC).
- **Separator:** Microporous polyethylene/polypropylene membrane.

**Discharge:** Li atoms in the anode are oxidized (Li → Li⁺ + e⁻); Li⁺ migrates through the electrolyte; e⁻ flows through the external circuit doing work; Li⁺ intercalates into the cathode (Li⁺ + CoO₂ + e⁻ → LiCoO₂). **(2 marks)**

**Charge:** Reverse process driven by external power source; Li⁺ deintercalates from the cathode and re-inserts into the anode. **(1 mark)**

**Nanostructured Electrodes — Roles: (4 marks)**
1. **Reduced diffusion length:** Diffusion time t ~ L²/D; reducing L from 10 µm → 50 nm shortens t by a factor of 40,000 → faster charging.
2. **Increased surface area:** More active sites per gram → higher specific capacity utilization.
3. **Strain accommodation:** Si-nanowire anodes accommodate the ~400 % volume expansion during lithiation without pulverization.
4. **Improved electron transport:** Carbon-coating on LiFePO₄ nanoparticles (intrinsically poor conductor) overcomes poor electronic conductivity.
5. **Examples:** LiFePO₄/C nanocomposites, Si-nanowires, SnO₂ nanotubes, graphene–sulfur composites for Li–S cells.

**(b) Butler–Volmer Equation & Nano-Impact on Kinetics (7 marks)**

**Equation derivation outline (3 marks):**

$$
j = j_{0}\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{\alpha_{a}F\eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\!\left(-\frac{\alpha_{c}F\eta}{RT}\right)\right]
$$

where j = net current density (A m⁻²), j₀ = exchange current density, α_a, α_c = transfer coefficients (typically α_a + α_c = 1), η = overpotential (V), F = Faraday constant, R = gas constant, T = temperature.

**Small-overpotential (linear) regime (1 mark):**

$$
j \approx \frac{j_{0}(\alpha_{a} + \alpha_{c})F\eta}{RT} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \eta = j \cdot R_{\text{ct}},\;\; R_{\text{ct}} = \frac{RT}{j_{0}F}
$$

**Effect of nanostructuring (3 marks):**
- **Exchange current density j₀** ∝ electrode surface area A (since i₀ = j₀·A = FAk⁰C_Ox^β...). Nanostructuring provides **2–3 orders of magnitude more surface area per unit mass**, so j₀ (A per m² of geometric area) actually *increases* while the **true** exchange current density per true surface area stays similar.
- **Charge-transfer resistance R_ct** is therefore **dramatically reduced** because R_ct ∝ 1/(j₀·A). This means lower polarization losses, higher power density, and reduced ohmic heating at high discharge rates.
- **Mass-transport limit** also improves: shorter solid-state diffusion paths in nanoparticles prevent concentration polarization at high currents.
- Net effect: nanostructured electrodes exhibit **flatter voltage profiles** during high-rate discharge, approaching theoretical capacity even at 5C–10C rates.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks — common pitfalls in KTU valuation:**
>
> 1. **Sign of E°_cell** — Forgetting that E°_cell = E°_cathode − E°_anode, not the reverse. A negative E°_cell means a non-spontaneous reaction (electrolytic, not galvanic).
> 2. **Units mismatch** — Specific energy in Wh kg⁻¹ requires Q in Ah kg⁻¹ and V in volts. A frequent error is multiplying V × C/g without converting C to Ah (divide by 3600).
> 3. **Confusing energy and power** — Energy has units of Joules or Wh (a *quantity*); power has units of Watts or J/s (a *rate*). The Ragone plot axes must not be swapped.
> 4. **Nernst equation base confusion** — Some students write log instead of ln. KTU evaluators deduct 1 mark unless the constant 0.0592/n is correctly used at 25 °C.
> 5. **Forgetting anode vs cathode assignment** — Always identify oxidation (anode) and reduction (cathode) *before* applying E°_cell = E°_cathode − E°_anode. A swapped assignment will give the wrong sign and lose 2–3 marks immediately.
> 6. **Half-cell E° must include state** — E°(Fe³⁺/Fe²⁺) ≠ E°(Fe²⁺/Fe). Always specify the redox couple exactly as given in the standard tables.
> 7. **Ignoring packing efficiency in real batteries** — Theoretical specific energy (Wh kg⁻¹) of active material is **always higher** than measured cell-level specific energy (typically 30–50 % lower) due to inactive components. KTU expects this clarification when comparing theoretical vs practical.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 Scheme:**

- **Three families of EES:** Batteries (bulk Faradaic), Supercapacitors (EDLC + pseudocapacitive), Fuel Cells (open-system continuous Faradaic).
- **Nernst Equation (25 °C):** E = E° − (0.0592 / n) log₁₀ Q — *memorize in both ln and log forms*.
- **Gibbs ↔ EMF:** ΔG = −nFE_cell. Negative ΔG ⇒ spontaneous ⇒ positive E_cell.
- **Theoretical specific capacity:** Q (mAh/g) = (26.8 × n) / M_w — *use M_w in kg/mol if you want Ah/kg directly*.
- **Standard reference:** SHE has E° = 0.000 V at 25 °C, 1 atm H₂, [H⁺] = 1 M.
- **Faraday's constant:** F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹ = 26.8 Ah mol⁻¹.
- **Double-layer capacitance:** C_dl = ε_r ε₀ A / d, with d ≈ 0.3–0.8 nm → high capacitance from enormous A (graphene 2600 m²/g).
- **Nanotechnology's role:** Reduces L → faster t_diff ~ L²/D; increases A → more reaction sites; accommodates strain → longer cycle life; reduces R_ct → higher power.
- **Key nano-electrodes:** Si-nanowires (anode), LiFePO₄/C nanoparticles (cathode), graphene/MWCNT (EDLC), RuO₂·xH₂O (pseudocapacitor), Pt/C (PEMFC catalyst).
- **Ragone plot axes:** x-axis = Specific Energy (Wh/kg), y-axis = Specific Power (W/kg), both logarithmic.
- **Butler–Volmer linear regime:** η ≈ j · R_ct; nanostructures → ↑j₀ → ↓R_ct → ↓polarization.
- **Fuel cell types to know:** PEMFC (60–100 °C, Nafion), SOFC (600–1000 °C, YSZ), DMFC (liquid methanol).
- **Application of ΔG° ↔ K_eq:** ln K_eq = nFE° / RT. At 25 °C: log K_eq = (nE°_cell) / 0.0592.
- **Miller indices / crystal planes:** Not in scope for this module, but layered cathode structures (LiCoO₂, R-3m space group) may appear in descriptive questions.
- **Two questions to never skip defining:** "Red Cat An Ox" (reduction at cathode, oxidation at anode) and "Anode is negative during discharge but positive during charge in a galvanic cell."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 4 : HYDROGEN STORAGE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogen Storage (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> Hydrogen storage refers to the containment of molecular hydrogen (H₂) in a safe, reversible, and energy-dense form using engineered materials and systems. In the context of nanotechnology, it specifically denotes the use of nanoscale materials — such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), nanostructured metal hydrides, and complex hydrides — to overcome the inherent low volumetric and gravimetric densities of gaseous/liquid hydrogen, enabling the practical realization of a hydrogen-based clean energy economy.

Hydrogen is the lightest and most abundant element in the universe, with an atomic mass of **1.008 g/mol** and a molecular weight of **2.016 g/mol**. Its energy density by mass is extraordinarily high (**≈ 142 MJ/kg**, about 3 times that of gasoline on a mass basis), but its energy density by volume under standard conditions is dismal (**0.01005 MJ/L at STP** vs. gasoline's **≈ 32 MJ/L**). This volumetric paradox is the central engineering problem that hydrogen storage technologies attempt to solve.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> The 2024 Scheme module categorizes hydrogen storage under three primary pillars: (i) **Physical Storage** (compressed gas, cryo-compressed, liquid H₂), (ii) **Material-Based Storage** (adsorption on nanostructures, absorption in metal hydrides), and (iii) **Chemical Storage** (chemical hydrides, LOHCs). The expected learning outcome is the ability to *evaluate nanomaterials based on gravimetric capacity, volumetric capacity, thermodynamics (ΔH, ΔS), and operating envelope (T, P)*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Sponge Problem":**
> Imagine you need to carry 1 kg of feathers home, but you only have a small cardboard box. The feathers are *light but bulky* — exactly like hydrogen. Now you have three options:
> - **Squeeze the box really hard** (compressed gas at 700 bar) — works but the box might explode.
> - **Shrink the feathers into a tiny ball** (cryogenic liquid hydrogen at −253 °C) — works but the freezer takes more energy than you save.
> - **Get a chemical sponge that soaks up the feathers inside its pores** (nanomaterial adsorbent) — elegant, lightweight, safe. The trick is making a sponge that holds *enough* feathers reversibly.
>
> Nanotechnology gives us that sponge — materials engineered at the atomic scale so that each gram has the surface area of a football field, allowing hydrogen molecules to dock, sit, and release on demand.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (KTU Mandatory Vocabulary)

| Symbol | Quantity | Definition | Typical Target (DOE 2025) |
|:------:|:--------:|:-----------|:--------------------------|
| $\rho_{g}$ | Gravimetric capacity | Mass of H₂ stored / Total system mass | **5.5 wt %** |
| $\rho_{v}$ | Volumetric capacity | Mass of H₂ stored / Total system volume | **40 g/L** |
| $T_{op}$ | Operating temperature | Delivery temperature | **−40 °C to 85 °C** |
| $P_{op}$ | Operating pressure | Delivery pressure | **< 100 bar** |
| $N_{cyc}$ | Cycle life | Number of adsorption/desorption cycles | **1500 cycles** |
| $\Delta H_{des}$ | Desorption enthalpy | Energy required to release 1 mole H₂ | **20–40 kJ/mol** |
| SSA | Specific surface area | Surface per unit mass of adsorbent | **> 1500 m²/g** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these numbers matter (KTU Examiner's viewpoint):**
> A material with 8 wt% capacity that requires 350 °C to release hydrogen is *useless* for a fuel cell vehicle (FCV) — the exhaust heat is only ~80 °C. Thermodynamics trumps raw capacity. This is the key trade-off the examiner will test.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Hydrogen Storage (Operational Breakdown)

Hydrogen storage technology can be classified along two orthogonal axes: **state of matter** (gas / liquid / solid) and **binding mechanism** (physical / chemical). The following structured taxonomy mirrors KTU's 2024 module layout.

#### Pillar I — Physical Storage (No Chemical Bonding)

- **Step 1: Compressed Gas (Type I–IV cylinders):**
  - Hydrogen is pressurized to **350 bar (Type III) or 700 bar (Type IV)**.
  - Type IV uses a polymer liner wrapped in carbon-fiber composite → gravimetric density of tank ≈ **5.7 wt%** at 700 bar.
  - Limitation: Compression work is **≈ 11–13 % of LHV** of H₂ (energetically wasteful).

- **Step 2: Cryo-Compressed (CcH₂):**
  - Combines cryogenic cooling (~ −253 °C) with moderate pressure (≈ 250 bar).
  - Achieves higher density than compressed gas alone.

- **Step 3: Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂):**
  - Liquefaction requires cooling to **20.28 K** — the boiling point of H₂.
  - Energy penalty: **≈ 30–40 % of LHV** to liquefy (huge).
  - Boil-off losses: ~ 1–3 % per day due to heat ingress.

#### Pillar II — Physisorption on Nanomaterials (Weak van der Waals Binding)

- **Step 1: Hydrogen molecules (H₂) approach the pore surface.**
  - Binding energy $E_{b} \approx$ **4–10 kJ/mol** (physisorption, below DOE's 20–40 kJ/mol window).
  - Pure physisorption is **too weak** for room-temperature operation — needs cryo-T.

- **Step 2: Nanomaterial design maximises uptake:**
  - **High SSA** (BET surface area) provides more docking sites.
  - **Narrow pore size** (< 1.5 nm) enhances overlap of potential wells from opposite walls → stronger effective binding.

- **Step 3: Materials of interest:**
  - **Activated carbons** (SSA ≈ 3000 m²/g).
  - **Single-Walled Carbon Nanotubes (SWCNTs)** (theoretical up to 20 wt% — early claims now disputed; reproducible ≈ 1–2 wt%).
  - **Graphene / Graphene oxide** layers.
  - **MOFs** (Metal-Organic Frameworks) — record SSA > 7000 m²/g (NU-110 series).
  - **COFs** (Covalent Organic Frameworks).
  - **Zeolites** (lower SSA, used for benchmarking).

#### Pillar III — Chemisorption / Metal Hydrides (Strong Chemical Binding)

- **Step 1: Hydrogen dissociates on a catalytic metal surface:**
  $$\mathrm{H_2 \rightarrow 2 H_{ads}}$$

- **Step 2: H atoms diffuse into the bulk metal lattice, forming a metal hydride:**
  $$\mathrm{M + \frac{x}{2} H_2 \rightleftharpoons MH_x}$$

- **Step 3: Reversibility is governed by Pressure-Composition-Temperature (PCT) isotherms.**

- **Key Material Classes:**

  | Class | Example | Capacity (wt %) | $T_{des}$ | Notes |
  |:------|:--------|:---------------:|:---------:|:------|
  | Intermetallic | LaNi₅H₆ | 1.4 | ≈ 25 °C | Commercial (AB₅ type) |
  | Mg-based | MgH₂ | 7.6 | ≈ 300 °C | High capacity, slow kinetics |
  | Complex hydride | NaAlH₄ | 5.6 | ≈ 200 °C | Needs Ti catalyst |
  | Complex hydride | LiBH₄ | 13.9 | ≈ 400 °C | Very stable |
  | BCC alloys | Ti-V-Cr | ≈ 2.5 | ≈ 25 °C | Near RT operation |

#### Pillar IV — Chemical Hydrogen Storage (Off-board Regenerable)

- **Ammonia (NH₃):** 17.8 wt% H₂, cracked at 600–900 °C → toxic handling.
- **Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (LOHCs):** Methylcyclohexane → Toluene cycle (hydrogenation/dehydrogenation).
- **Hydrolysis of chemical hydrides:** NaBH₄ + 2H₂O → NaBO₂ + 4H₂ (one-way; borate must be recycled off-board).

### 2.2 Why Nanotechnology is a Game-Changer — The "Why" and "How"

- **Why:** Bulk materials suffer from long diffusion paths, low SSA, and poor reversibility. Nanoparticles (size < 100 nm) reduce diffusion length $L \propto d_{particle}$, accelerate kinetics, and can destabilise overly-stable hydrides via surface energy effects (nanoscale confinement shifts equilibrium).
- **How:**
  - **Particle size reduction** → shorter H diffusion path, faster (de)hydrogenation.
  - **Nano-confinement in porous scaffolds** (e.g., MgH₂ inside CMK-3 mesoporous carbon) lowers $\Delta H_{des}$ by 5–15 kJ/mol.
  - **Catalyst decoration** at the nanoscale (Ni, Pd, Nb nanoparticles) splits H₂ molecules and enables spillover.
  - **High-curvature surfaces** in CNTs and MOF corners provide enhanced binding sites.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA SHEET — Memorise for KTU ESE**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:--------------|
| 1 | $PV = nRT$ | Ideal gas law | $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\rho_{g} = \dfrac{m_{\mathrm{H_2}}}{m_{\mathrm{H_2}} + m_{\mathrm{system}}} \times 100\%$ | Gravimetric capacity | wt % |
| 3 | $\rho_{v} = \dfrac{m_{\mathrm{H_2}}}{V_{\mathrm{system}}}$ | Volumetric capacity | g/L or kg/m³ |
| 4 | $Z = \dfrac{PV}{nRT}$ | Compressibility factor | $Z=1$ ideal; $Z>1$ for H₂ at high P |
| 5 | $\ln P = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{R T} + \dfrac{\Delta S}{R}$ | **Van't Hoff Equation** | Slope gives $\Delta H$ of (de)hydrogenation |
| 6 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S$ | Gibbs free energy | At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$ |
| 7 | $\ln P = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{\mathrm{ads}}}{R T} + \text{const}$ | Clausius-Clapeyron for adsorption | Isosteric heat of adsorption |
| 8 | $E_{a} = E_{a,0} - \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{d}}$ | Activation energy vs nanoparticle diameter $d$ | $E_a$ drops with smaller $d$ |
| 9 | $\Delta H_{des} = E_{\mathrm{phys}} + E_{\mathrm{Kubas}}$ | Total binding in MOF/Metal hybrids | $E_{\mathrm{Kubas}} \approx 20$–$40$ kJ/mol |
| 10 | $t_{\mathrm{diff}} = \dfrac{L^2}{D}$ | Diffusion time across length $L$ | $D$ = H diffusivity |
| 11 | $\mathrm{LHV_{H_2}} = 119.96$ MJ/kg | Lower heating value | Energy released by combustion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Equations (5) and (6) are the *backbone* of KTU numerical problems. If a problem gives you two pressures at two temperatures, immediately invoke Van't Hoff to extract $\Delta H$. This is a guaranteed 5–7 mark question in ESE.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs):** Toyota Mirai (Type IV 700 bar tank, ≈ 5 wt%, ≈ 122 L, range 650 km).
- **Stationary backup power** for telecom towers (metal hydride storage, safe, low-pressure).
- **Grid-scale energy storage** (excess renewable electricity → electrolyse water → store H₂ → regenerate electricity via fuel cell).
- **Aerospace:** NASA uses LH₂ as rocket propellant (Space Shuttle external tank held ≈ 1.5 million litres).
- **Portable electronics:** Future micro-fuel cells powered by metal hydride cartridges.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Van't Hoff to Operating Pressure of a Metal Hydride

**Problem Context (KTU-style):**
A metal hydride MH₂ has an enthalpy of dehydrogenation $\Delta H_{des} = +40$ kJ/mol and entropy of dehydrogenation $\Delta S_{des} = +120$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹. Calculate (a) the equilibrium temperature at $P = 1$ bar, and (b) the operating pressure at $T = 298$ K. (Relevant to MgH₂-class materials.)

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

The reaction: $\mathrm{MH_2 \rightleftharpoons M + H_2}$.

At thermodynamic equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$.

$$\Delta G = \Delta H_{des} - T \Delta S_{des} = 0$$

For an equilibrium reaction involving one mole of gaseous H₂ at pressure $P_{H_2}$, the relation between $\Delta G$ and equilibrium constant $K_P$ is:

$$\Delta G = -RT \ln K_P = -RT \ln P_{H_2}$$

(Note: standard state reference pressure = 1 bar, and $\Delta G^{\circ}$ corresponds to 1 bar.)

Equating the two expressions for $\Delta G$:

$$-RT \ln P_{H_2} = \Delta H_{des} - T \Delta S_{des}$$

Rearranging:

$$\ln P_{H_2} = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{des}}{RT} + \dfrac{\Delta S_{des}}{R}$$

This is the **Van't Hoff equation** (Equation 5 in the formula sheet). This single equation governs the entire thermodynamic design space of a metal-hydride hydrogen store.

**Part (a):** Equilibrium temperature at $P_{H_2} = 1$ bar.

At $P_{H_2} = 1$ bar: $\ln P_{H_2} = \ln 1 = 0$.

Therefore:

$$0 = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{des}}{R T_{eq}} + \dfrac{\Delta S_{des}}{R}$$

Solving for $T_{eq}$:

$$T_{eq} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{des}}{\Delta S_{des}}$$

Substitute values (note unit conversion: $\Delta H = 40000$ J/mol, $\Delta S = 120$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹):

$$T_{eq} = \dfrac{40000 \, \mathrm{J/mol}}{120 \, \mathrm{J \, mol^{-1} \, K^{-1}}}$$

$$\boxed{T_{eq} \approx 333.3 \, \mathrm{K} \,\, (\approx 60.1 \,^{\circ}\mathrm{C})}$$

**Interpretation (KTU model-answer style):** At $T < 333.3$ K and $P = 1$ bar, the forward reaction is spontaneous — hydrogen is *absorbed* (charging). At $T > 333.3$ K, hydrogen is *desorbed* (discharging). Therefore, this is a near-room-temperature hydride.

**Part (b):** Operating pressure at $T = 298$ K.

Apply the Van't Hoff equation directly:

$$\ln P_{H_2} = -\dfrac{40000}{(8.314)(298)} + \dfrac{120}{8.314}$$

Compute term by term:

$$-\dfrac{40000}{2477.572} = -16.144$$

$$\dfrac{120}{8.314} = 14.434$$

Therefore:

$$\ln P_{H_2} = -16.144 + 14.434 = -1.710$$

$$P_{H_2} = e^{-1.710}$$

$$\boxed{P_{H_2} \approx 0.181 \, \mathrm{bar}}$$

**Interpretation:** To desorb hydrogen at room temperature (298 K), the equilibrium pressure is only 0.181 bar — well below atmospheric. This means at 298 K the material naturally releases H₂; conversely, to charge it one needs to apply pressure *greater* than 0.181 bar. Excellent for low-pressure portable applications.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Gravimetric & Volumetric Capacity of a MOF-5 Analogue

**Problem Context (KTU-style):**
A hypothetical MOF stores hydrogen by physisorption at 77 K and 60 bar. The crystal density of the empty MOF is 0.59 g/cm³, and its pore volume is 1.16 cm³/g. Compute the maximum theoretical gravimetric and volumetric capacities assuming complete pore filling with liquid-like H₂ density 0.0708 g/cm³.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Gravimetric capacity:**

The mass of H₂ stored per gram of empty MOF is:

$$m_{\mathrm{H_2}} = \rho_{\mathrm{H_2,\,liq-like}} \times V_{\mathrm{pore}}$$

$$m_{\mathrm{H_2}} = 0.0708 \, \mathrm{g/cm^3} \times 1.16 \, \mathrm{cm^3/g}$$

$$m_{\mathrm{H_2}} \approx 0.0821 \, \mathrm{g_{H_2}/g_{MOF}}$$

Therefore:

$$\rho_{g} = \dfrac{m_{\mathrm{H_2}}}{1 \, \mathrm{g_{MOF}} + m_{\mathrm{H_2}}} \times 100\%$$

$$\rho_{g} = \dfrac{0.0821}{1.0821} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{g} \approx 7.59 \, \mathrm{wt\%}}$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric capacity:**

Total density of the loaded MOF = density of MOF framework + density contribution from stored H₂.

$$\rho_{\mathrm{H_2,\,bulk}} = \rho_{\mathrm{H_2,\,liq-like}} \times V_{\mathrm{pore}} \times \rho_{\mathrm{frame}}$$

Per 1 cm³ of empty MOF, the mass of framework is 0.59 g, and the pore volume inside is $0.59 \times 1.16 = 0.6844$ cm³. The H₂ mass in 1 cm³ of MOF is:

$$m_{\mathrm{H_2}} = 0.0708 \times 0.6844 = 0.04846 \, \mathrm{g}$$

Therefore:

$$\rho_{v} = \dfrac{0.04846 \, \mathrm{g}}{1 \, \mathrm{cm^3}} \times 1000 \, \mathrm{cm^3/L}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{v} \approx 48.5 \, \mathrm{g_{H_2}/L}}$$

**Interpretation:** Both metrics exceed the DOE 2025 targets (5.5 wt%, 40 g/L) — but this calculation assumes **complete pore filling with liquid-like density at 77 K**, which is *idealised*. Real MOF-5 typically achieves ≈ 4.7 wt% at 77 K, 60 bar. The discrepancy factor (≈ 0.6) is often called the *packing efficiency*.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Evaluation of Storage Methods

This is the kind of summary examiners expect in 14-mark answers.

| Method | $\rho_g$ (wt%) | $\rho_v$ (g/L) | $T_{op}$ (°C) | $P_{op}$ (bar) | Energy Penalty | Reversible? | KTU-typical verdict |
|:-------|:--------------:|:--------------:|:-------------:|:--------------:|:--------------:|:-----------:|:--------------------|
| Compressed gas (700 bar, Type IV) | 5.7 | 40 | 25 | 700 | ≈ 11 % LHV | Yes | Mature, commercial |
| Liquid H₂ (LH₂) | ≈ 100 | 70.8 | −253 | 1 | 30–40 % LHV | Yes (boil-off) | Aerospace only |
| Cryo-compressed | 5.4 | 50 | −253 | 250 | 15 % LHV | Yes | Research |
| Metal hydride (LaNi₅) | 1.4 | 100 | 25 | 5–10 | < 2 % LHV | Yes | Safe, low capacity |
| MgH₂ (nano) | 7.6 | 110 | 300 | 1 | < 3 % LHV | Yes | Kinetics-limited |
| NaAlH₄ | 5.6 | 90 | 200 | 100 | < 5 % LHV | Yes | Promising |
| SWCNT | 1–2 (exp.) | ≈ 30 | −196 | 1–100 | ≈ 0 % LHV | Yes | Disputed claims |
| MOF-5 | 4.7 | 31 | −196 | 60 | ≈ 0 % LHV | Yes | Cryo only |
| NH₃ | 17.8 | 121 | 25 | 10 | 20 % LHV | Cracking needed | Toxicity issue |
| LOHC (MCH) | 6.2 | 47 | 25 (liq) | 1 | ≈ 10 % LHV | Yes (catalytic) | Promising |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Hierarchical Taxonomy of Hydrogen Storage Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[HYDROGEN STORAGE METHODS]
    rootB[PHYSICAL STORAGE]
    rootC[MATERIAL BASED STORAGE]
    rootD[CHEMICAL STORAGE]

    rootA --> rootB
    rootA --> rootC
    rootA --> rootD

    rootB --> nB1[Compressed Gas 350 to 700 bar]
    rootB --> nB2[Cryogenic Liquid H2 at 20 K]
    rootB --> nB3[Cryo Compressed at 250 bar and 20 K]

    rootC --> nC1[Physisorption on Nanomaterials]
    rootC --> nC2[Chemisorption as Metal Hydrides]

    nC1 --> nC1a[Carbon Nanotubes]
    nC1 --> nC1b[Graphene and Graphene Oxide]
    nC1 --> nC1c[Metal Organic Frameworks MOFs]
    nC1 --> nC1d[Zeolites and Activated Carbon]

    nC2 --> nC2a[Intermetallics LaNi5]

    nC2 --> nC2c[Complex Hydrides NaAlH4 LiBH4]

    rootD --> nD1[Ammonia NH3]
    rootD --> nD2[Liquid Organic Carriers LOHC]
    rootD --> nD3[Borohydrides NaBH4]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hmolA[H2 Molecule in Gas Phase]
    surfB[Pore Wall of MOF with Open Metal Sites]
    bindC[Physisorbed H2 on Wall]
    desD[Released H2 during Desorption]

    hmolA -->|approaches pore| surfB
    surfB -->|weak van der Waals| bindC
    bindC -->|heat or pressure drop| desD

    metalSite1[Open Metal Site Zr or Cu]

    surfB --> metalSite1
    surfB --> metalSite2
    metalSite1 --- poreSpace
    metalSite2 --- poreSpace
```

```mermaid
    inputA --> compB --> cryoC --> adsD
    adsD --> storeE
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startK{Application Domain}
    startK -->|Automotive| autoM[High energy density needed: 700 bar Type IV or cryo compressed]
    startK -->|Portable Electronics| portN[Low pressure metal hydride LaNi5]
    startK -->|Stationary Backup| statP[Large volume metal hydride tank Mg based]
    startK -->|Aerospace| aeroQ[Cryogenic LH2 in insulated tanks]
    startK -->|Long duration grid| gridR[Underground cavern or LOHC]

    autoM --> autoR1[Trade off: weight versus cost versus safety]
    portN --> portS1[Trade off: cycle life versus gravimetric capacity]
    statP --> statS1[Trade off: cost versus kinetics versus temperature]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]
**Define gravimetric and volumetric hydrogen storage capacities. Why are both metrics important in evaluating a hydrogen storage material?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Gravimetric capacity ($\rho_g$):** The mass of hydrogen stored divided by the total mass of the storage system (including the storage material and any containment hardware), expressed as a weight percentage (wt%).

$$\rho_{g} = \dfrac{m_{\mathrm{H_2}}}{m_{\mathrm{H_2}} + m_{\mathrm{system}}} \times 100\%$$

**Volumetric capacity ($\rho_v$):** The mass of hydrogen stored per unit volume of the storage system.

$$\rho_{v} = \dfrac{m_{\mathrm{H_2}}}{V_{\mathrm{system}}} \quad (\mathrm{g/L})$$

**Importance of both metrics (1 mark):**
- For **mobile applications** (vehicles, drones, laptops), $\rho_g$ is critical because the entire system must be carried; heavy tanks reduce range.
- For **stationary or space-constrained applications**, $\rho_v$ is critical because the volume footprint determines feasibility (e.g., passenger car packaging).
- A material may excel in one metric and fail in the other (e.g., LH₂ has excellent $\rho_g$ but very poor $\rho_v$ until liquefied). Both must be reported to avoid misleading claims.

---

#### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]
**What is the Van't Hoff equation? State its significance in the design of metal-hydride hydrogen storage systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Van't Hoff equation (2 marks):**

For the reversible reaction $\mathrm{MH_x \rightleftharpoons M + \frac{x}{2} H_2}$ at equilibrium:

$$\ln P_{\mathrm{H_2}} = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{des}}{R T} + \dfrac{\Delta S_{des}}{R}$$

where $P_{\mathrm{H_2}}$ is the equilibrium hydrogen pressure (bar), $T$ is absolute temperature (K), $\Delta H_{des}$ is the enthalpy of desorption (J/mol), $\Delta S_{des}$ is the entropy of desorption (J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹), and $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹.

**Significance in design (1 mark):**
- The Van't Hoff plot (ln P vs. 1/T) is a **straight line** whose slope gives $\Delta H_{des}$ — a thermodynamic fingerprint of the hydride.
- It predicts the **operating temperature for a given delivery pressure**, allowing engineers to choose a hydride whose thermodynamics match the available heat source (e.g., exhaust gas of a fuel cell vehicle at ~80 °C).
- It enables **comparative screening** of candidate materials before expensive experimental work.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Common mistakes students make in hydrogen storage problems:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kJ to J** when substituting into Van't Hoff equation (gives off-by-1000 errors in T).
> 2. **Confusing $\Delta H_{abs}$ and $\Delta H_{des}$** — they are equal in magnitude, opposite in sign. The Van't Hoff equation above uses $\Delta H_{des}$ (positive).
> 3. **Mixing up gravimetric and volumetric** — examiners explicitly test that you know which goes in numerator.
> 4. **Stating "$E_{ads} = 20$ kJ/mol implies physisorption"** — incorrect. 20–40 kJ/mol is the *chemisorption* regime. Physisorption is 4–10 kJ/mol.
> 5. **Ignoring system mass/volume** in real-world calculations (tank, valves, regulators count).
> 6. **Failing to draw PCT isotherms** when discussing metal hydrides — losing 2 marks.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** With the aid of a Pressure-Composition-Temperature (PCT) diagram, explain the absorption and desorption behaviour of a metal hydride such as LaNi₅. Discuss the significance of the plateau pressure and hysteresis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A candidate metal hydride MH₂ has the following Van't Hoff parameters: $\Delta H_{des} = +45$ kJ/mol, $\Delta S_{des} = +130$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹.
  (i) Derive the Van't Hoff equation and determine the temperature at which the equilibrium pressure is 1 bar.
  (ii) Determine the pressure at which this hydride will discharge at 350 K.
  (iii) Comment on whether this material is suitable for a fuel-cell vehicle (FCV) operating between 25 °C and 80 °C with a fuel cell inlet requirement of 1.5 bar. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PCT diagram and LaNi₅ behaviour (7 marks):**

A PCT isotherm plots $P_{H_2}$ vs. hydrogen-to-metal ratio (H/M) at constant T. For LaNi₅H₆:

1. **Low-H/M region (α-phase):** Hydrogen dissolves in the metal lattice as a dilute solid solution. Pressure rises steeply with H/M (Henry's-law-like behaviour).

2. **Plateau region (α + β coexistence):** A flat plateau appears because two phases coexist at constant chemical potential (Gibbs phase rule: $F = C - P + 2 = 1 - 2 + 2 = 1$, so at fixed T, P is invariant). This plateau is the **operational region** for storage — pressure remains nearly constant as H/M increases from ≈ 0.1 to ≈ 5.5.

3. **High-H/M region (β-phase):** Hydride is fully formed; further H addition requires steep pressure increase.

- **Absorption isotherm** (charging): left to right.
- **Desorption isotherm** (discharging): right to left.
- **Hysteresis gap:** The desorption pressure is **lower** than the absorption pressure at the same T due to lattice strain energy, plastic deformation, and interfacial energy barriers during phase transformation. For LaNi₅ the hysteresis is moderate (≈ 0.2 bar at 25 °C).

**Significance of plateau pressure (2 marks):**
- Sets the operating delivery pressure. For LaNi₅ at 25 °C, plateau ≈ 2 bar — too low for direct FCV use, but ideal for low-pressure portable electronics.

**Significance of hysteresis (1 mark):**
- Reduces the effective usable capacity (must charge above plateau pressure to compensate).
- Indicates energy loss per cycle due to dissipated mechanical work.

---

**(b) Numerical problem on Van't Hoff (7 marks):**

**(i) Derivation and 1-bar equilibrium temperature (3 marks):**

Starting from $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S = 0$ at equilibrium, and using $\Delta G = -RT \ln P_{H_2}$:

$$-RT \ln P_{H_2} = \Delta H_{des} - T \Delta S_{des}$$

Rearranging yields the Van't Hoff equation:

$$\ln P_{H_2} = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{des}}{RT} + \dfrac{\Delta S_{des}}{R}$$

At $P_{H_2} = 1$ bar, $\ln P_{H_2} = 0$, hence $T_{eq} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{des}}{\Delta S_{des}}$:

$$T_{eq} = \dfrac{45000 \, \mathrm{J/mol}}{130 \, \mathrm{J \, mol^{-1} \, K^{-1}}} \approx 346.15 \, \mathrm{K} \,\, (\approx 73 \,^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$$

**(ii) Pressure at 350 K (2 marks):**

$$\ln P_{H_2} = -\dfrac{45000}{8.314 \times 350} + \dfrac{130}{8.314}$$

$$= -\dfrac{45000}{2909.9} + 15.636 = -15.464 + 15.636 = 0.172$$

$$P_{H_2} = e^{0.172} \approx 1.188 \, \mathrm{bar}$$

**(iii) FCV suitability comment (2 marks):**

At 350 K (≈ 77 °C, near the upper end of FCV operation), the equilibrium pressure is ≈ 1.19 bar — **less than the required 1.5 bar inlet pressure** of the FCV. This means the hydride will only partially release its stored hydrogen at the FCV's maximum waste-heat temperature. To achieve 1.5 bar, one needs:

$$T_{eq}(1.5\,\mathrm{bar}): \quad \ln(1.5) = -\dfrac{45000}{8.314 \, T} + 15.636$$

$$0.405 = -\dfrac{45000}{8.314 \, T} + 15.636 \Rightarrow \dfrac{45000}{8.314 \, T} = 15.231$$

$$T \approx 355.4 \, \mathrm{K} \,\, (\approx 82.4 \,^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$$

**Conclusion:** This material is **marginally suitable** for FCV use — it just reaches 1.5 bar near the maximum operating temperature of 80 °C, leaving no thermal safety margin. A lower $\Delta H_{des}$ (≈ 35 kJ/mol) would be more practical.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Compare physisorption and chemisorption mechanisms of hydrogen storage on nanomaterials. Discuss the role of surface area, pore size, and binding energy in achieving DOE 2025 targets. Use specific examples (CNTs, MOFs, MgH₂). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A MOF-210 has BET surface area 6240 m²/g and pore volume 3.6 cm³/g. Assuming liquid-like H₂ density (0.0708 g/cm³) at 77 K and a packing efficiency of 65 %, calculate:
  (i) Gravimetric capacity (wt%)
  (ii) Volumetric capacity (g/L) given crystal density 0.25 g/cm³.
  (iii) Compare with DOE 2025 targets (5.5 wt% and 40 g/L). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Physisorption vs Chemisorption comparison (7 marks):**

| Property | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
|:---------|:--------------|:--------------|
| Binding energy | **4–10 kJ/mol** (weak vdW) | **20–200 kJ/mol** (covalent/ionic) |
| Operating T | Cryogenic (77 K) | Near-room-T to 300 °C |
| Reversibility | Fully reversible | Requires careful T/P swing |
| Mechanism | H₂ remains molecular on surface | H₂ dissociates → atoms enter lattice |
| Kinetics | Fast | Often slow; needs catalyst |
| Capacity driver | High SSA + narrow pores | Stoichiometry of hydride |

**Role of surface area (2 marks):** Physisorption capacity scales directly with BET SSA. MOF-210 (6240 m²/g) and NU-110 (> 7000 m²/g) are benchmark materials. CNTs have moderate SSA (≈ 400 m²/g for SWCNT bundles) — lower than MOFs.

**Role of pore size (1 mark):** Optimal pore width ≈ 0.6–1.5 nm. In this range, potential wells from opposing pore walls overlap, raising the effective binding energy from ≈ 4 to ≈ 8 kJ/mol (still weak, but improves near-77 K uptake).

**Role of binding energy (1 mark):** DOE's target window of 20–40 kJ/mol cannot be met by pure physisorption. Strategies to bridge this:
- **Kubas interaction** in MOFs with exposed metal sites (e.g., Ni²⁺, Cu²⁺) — σ-donation from H₂ to empty d-orbitals raises binding to ≈ 20–30 kJ/mol.
- **Spillover** (catalyst dissociates H₂; atomic H migrates onto receptor) — demonstrated in Pt-decorated CNTs/IRMOFs.

**Examples (3 marks):**
- **SWCNTs:** Early claims of 20 wt% (Dillon et al., 1997) were never reliably reproduced. Consensus: ≈ 1–2 wt% at 77 K, much less at RT.
- **MOFs:** MOF-5, MOF-177, MOF-210 — highest reported gravimetric capacity (≈ 7–8 wt% at 77 K, 60 bar, theoretical) but RT capacity is < 1 wt%.
- **MgH₂:** 7.6 wt% theoretical, but $T_{des} \approx 300$ °C. Nanoconfinement in CMK-3 reduces this to ≈ 200 °C by destabilising the hydride.

---

**(b) MOF-210 capacity calculations (7 marks):**

**(i) Gravimetric capacity (3 marks):**

Pore volume per gram = 3.6 cm³/g.

Maximum H₂ mass per gram (100% filling) = $0.0708 \times 3.6 = 0.2549$ g H₂/g MOF.

With 65% packing efficiency: $m_{\mathrm{H_2}} = 0.2549 \times 0.65 = 0.1657$ g H₂ / g MOF.

$$\rho_{g} = \dfrac{0.1657}{1 + 0.1657} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.1657}{1.1657} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{g} \approx 14.22 \, \mathrm{wt\%}}$$

**(ii) Volumetric capacity (2 marks):**

Per 1 cm³ of MOF crystal: mass of framework = 0.25 g. Pore volume = $0.25 \times 3.6 = 0.90$ cm³. But pore volume cannot exceed the geometric limit (we assume the crystal density already accounts for porosity — here we recompute the actual stored H₂ per crystal volume using the bulk density of H₂ in the pore, scaled by packing efficiency).

Mass of H₂ per cm³ of MOF crystal:

$$m_{\mathrm{H_2}}/\mathrm{cm^3} = 0.0708 \, \mathrm{g/cm^3} \times \text{pore fraction} \times 0.65$$

Pore fraction = $V_{\mathrm{pore}} \times \rho_{\mathrm{crystal}} = 3.6 \times 0.25 = 0.90$ — *note this exceeds 1 cm³, meaning the framework itself is highly porous and we should instead use directly:*

$$\rho_v = \rho_{\mathrm{H_2,\,liq-like}} \times \dfrac{V_{\mathrm{pore}}}{1/\rho_{\mathrm{crystal}}} \times 0.65$$

$$\rho_v = 0.0708 \times \dfrac{3.6}{1/0.25} \times 0.65 = 0.0708 \times 0.9 \times 0.65$$

$$\rho_v = 0.0708 \times 0.585 = 0.04142 \, \mathrm{g/cm^3} = 41.42 \, \mathrm{g/L}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{v} \approx 41.4 \, \mathrm{g/L}}$$

**(iii) Comparison with DOE 2025 (2 marks):**

- Gravimetric: **14.22 wt% >> 5.5 wt%** → exceeds DOE target by 2.6×. ✓
- Volumetric: **41.4 g/L > 40 g/L** → just exceeds DOE target. ✓ (marginal)

**Critical caveat (KTU-style discussion, 1 mark):**
This is at 77 K (cryogenic) and 60 bar — *not* the DOE operating envelope of −40 to 85 °C. At room temperature, MOF capacity drops to < 1 wt%. So although the material exceeds DOE targets *under cryo-conditions*, it fails the **operational temperature criterion**. This is the central challenge of physisorption-based storage.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- ⚡ **Hydrogen's energy paradox:** Highest gravimetric energy density of any fuel (~142 MJ/kg), but lowest volumetric density → requires storage engineering.
- ⚡ **DOE 2025 targets:** 5.5 wt%, 40 g/L, T = −40 to 85 °C, P < 100 bar, 1500 cycles.
- ⚡ **Four storage pillars:** Physical (compressed / cryogenic), Material (physisorption / chemisorption), Chemical (NH₃ / LOHC), and Underground (caverns).
- ⚡ **Physisorption binding energy:** 4–10 kJ/mol → cryogenic operation only.
- ⚡ **Chemisorption binding energy:** 20–40 kJ/mol (DOE sweet spot) for near-ambient operation.
- ⚡ **Van't Hoff equation:** $\ln P = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{des}}{RT} + \dfrac{\Delta S_{des}}{R}$ — the *master equation* of metal-hydride design.
- ⚡ **PCT isotherm plateau** indicates usable storage capacity; hysteresis gap = energy loss per cycle.
- ⚡ **Nanotechnology advantages:** High SSA (MOFs > 7000 m²/g), short diffusion paths (nanoparticles), destabilisation of stable hydrides (nanoscale effect).
- ⚡ **Key materials to remember:**
  - **LaNi₅H₆** — 1.4 wt%, RT operation, low pressure.
  - **MgH₂** — 7.6 wt%, but needs ≈ 300 °C; nano-MgH₂ in CMK-3 reduces this.
  - **NaAlH₄** — 5.6 wt%, complex hydride, Ti-catalysed.
  - **MOF-5 / MOF-210** — record cryo capacity ≈ 14 wt% theoretical.
  - **SWCNTs** — controversial early claims; consensus ≈ 1–2 wt%.
- ⚡ **Important formulas:** $\rho_g = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{m_{H_2} + m_{sys}} \times 100\%$, $\rho_v = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{V_{sys}}$, and Van't Hoff (memorise).
- ⚡ **Typical KTU trap:** Don't confuse physisorption (< 10 kJ/mol) with chemisorption (> 20 kJ/mol) — both are sometimes loosely called "adsorption."
- ⚡ **Typical KTU trap:** Remember to subtract tank mass when computing *system* gravimetric capacity, not just material capacity.
- ⚡ **Real-world benchmark:** Toyota Mirai uses 700-bar Type IV tanks with ≈ 5 wt% system capacity — the current state of the art.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET868 - NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Solar Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Solar Cells — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Photovoltaic (PV) Solar Cell:** A solid-state electronic device that converts the energy of incident photons directly into electrical energy via the **photovoltaic effect**, typically fabricated from semiconductor materials (Si, GaAs, CdTe, CIGS, organic semiconductors, or hybrid perovskites) whose bandgap is engineered to match the solar spectrum.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance (PEMET868 Nanotechnology, Module 4), a **solar cell is the canonical application of nanoscience in energy conversion** — where the active layer thickness, grain size, or junction morphology directly determines the device efficiency.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Coin Sorting Machine"

Imagine photons of sunlight arriving at your rooftop as a **mixed flood of coins of different denominations** (different energies, from infrared to UV). A conventional solar cell is like a **coin sorting machine** that:

1. Lets only coins of a *specific denomination* (photon energy $\geq E_g$) trigger the mechanism.
2. Anything smaller (sub-bandgap photons) is rejected — and you *lose* that energy as heat.
3. Coins that are *too large* (high-energy UV photons) only release *one unit of useful work*, wasting the excess as thermalization loss.

**Nanotechnology tweaks this machine:**
- **Quantum dots** = adjustable coin slots (tunable bandgap by particle size).
- **Plasmonic nanoparticles** = funnels that increase the *flow rate* of coins through the slot (light trapping).
- **Nanowires / nano-texturing** = *tilting the conveyor belt* so coins spend more time near the sensor (enhanced absorption).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Solar Metrics (used throughout KTU evaluation):**
> - **Air Mass 1.5 Global (AM1.5G)** solar spectrum, irradiance **1000 W/m²**, cell temperature **25 °C (298.15 K)**.
> - **Solar constant** at AM0 ≈ **1361 W/m²**.
> - **Shockley–Queisser limit** for a single-junction Si cell under AM1.5G ≈ **33.7 %**.

## 1.3 The Photovoltaic Effect — Why It Happens

When a photon with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ strikes a semiconductor:

$$h\nu \longrightarrow e^-_{(\text{CB})} + h^+_{(\text{VB})}$$

The absorbed photon energy excites a **valence-band electron** into the **conduction band**, leaving behind a **hole**. A built-in electric field at the p–n junction then **separates** these photogenerated carriers before they recombine, sweeping electrons to the n-side and holes to the p-side. Connecting an external circuit allows current to flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Loss Channels (must remember for KTU exams):**
> 1. **Transmission loss** — $h\nu < E_g$ photons pass through unabsorbed.
> 2. **Thermalization loss** — $h\nu \gg E_g$ excess energy wasted as heat.
> 3. **Recombination loss** — carriers recombine (radiative, Auger, SRH) before collection.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Architecture of a Generic Solar Cell

A solar cell is fundamentally a **large-area p–n junction diode** with the following layered structure (top to bottom):

| Layer | Function | Typical Nanomaterial Used |
|---|---|---|
| Anti-Reflection Coating (ARC) | Reduces reflection losses ($\approx 4$–$30\%$) | TiO₂, Si₃N₄, MgF₂ |
| Front Contact (Grid) | Collects electrons, allows light in | Ag, Al pastes; transparent ITO/FTO |
| Emitter (n-type) | Photogeneration region | n-Si (P-doped), n-CdS |
| Depletion Region | Built-in field separates carriers | p-n junction interface |
| Base (p-type) | Majority carrier collection | p-Si (B-doped), p-CdTe, p-CIGS |
| Back Contact | Hole collection | Al, Mo, Au |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw the band diagram (E vs x) showing $E_c$, $E_v$, $E_Fn$, $E_Fp$, and the photogeneration arrow. Marks are awarded for band bending and quasi-Fermi level splitting.

## 2.2 The Equivalent Circuit (One-Diode Model)

The standard solar cell equivalent circuit consists of:
- A **photocurrent source** $I_{ph}$ (light-generated current).
- A **diode** representing the p–n junction dark current $I_D$.
- A **series resistance** $R_s$ (contact + bulk resistance).
- A **shunt resistance** $R_{sh}$ (leakage paths).
- Load terminals delivering $I$ and $V$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT revision artifact for any KTU solar-cell problem. Memorize the units and the sign conventions.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Photocurrent density | $J_{ph} = q \int_{E_g}^{\infty} \Phi(E) \, dE$ | $\Phi$ = photon flux (m⁻² s⁻¹ eV⁻¹) |
| 2 | Diode dark current (ideal) | $I_D = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1 \right]$ | $I_0$ = reverse saturation current |
| 3 | Solar cell I–V equation | $I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{q(V+IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V+IR_s}{R_{sh}}$ | $n$ = ideality factor (1–2) |
| 4 | Open-circuit voltage | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | $V_{oc} \propto \ln(I_{ph})$ |
| 5 | Short-circuit current | $I_{sc} \approx I_{ph}$ (when $R_s \to 0$, $R_{sh} \to \infty$) | Linear in light intensity |
| 6 | Maximum power point | $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$ | Found graphically on I–V curve |
| 7 | Fill Factor | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$ | $0 < FF < 1$, ideal Si ≈ 0.83 |
| 8 | Power Conversion Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{oc} I_{sc} \, FF}{P_{in}}$ | $\eta_{Si}^{\max} \approx 26.7\%$ |
| 9 | Quantum Efficiency | $EQE(\lambda) = \dfrac{\text{electrons collected/sec}}{\text{photons incident/sec at }\lambda}$ | $0 \leq EQE \leq 1$ |
| 10 | Spectral response | $SR(\lambda) = \dfrac{q \lambda}{hc} \, EQE(\lambda)$ | Units: A/W |
| 11 | Particle-in-a-box energy (QD) | $E_n = \dfrac{n^2 h^2}{8 m^* L^2}$ | $L$ = quantum dot diameter |
| 12 | Effective bandgap (QD) | $E_g^{\text{eff}} = E_g^{\text{bulk}} + \dfrac{h^2}{8 m^* L^2}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{m_e^*} + \dfrac{1}{m_h^*}\right)$ | Quantum confinement blueshift |
| 13 | Detailed balance / SQ limit | $\eta_{max} \approx 33.7\%$ for $E_g \approx 1.34$ eV | For single-junction under AM1.5G |
| 14 | Carrier collection prob. | $\eta_c = 1 - \exp(- \alpha L)$ | $\alpha$ = absorption coefficient |

## 2.4 Why $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$ Matter for Efficiency

- **High $R_s$** (typical industrial target $R_s < 1\,\Omega\!\cdot\!\text{cm}^2$): reduces fill factor by flattening the I–V curve near $V_{oc}$.
- **Low $R_{sh}$** (target $R_{sh} > 1000\,\Omega\!\cdot\!\text{cm}^2$): provides alternate current path, reducing $I_{sc}$.

An **empirical fill-factor approximation** (Green's formula) widely accepted in KTU board answers:

$$FF_0 = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$$

where $v_{oc} = \dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}$. With parasitic losses:

$$FF \approx FF_0 (1 - r_s)(1 - 1/r_{sh})$$

with $r_s = R_s I_{sc}/V_{oc}$ and $r_{sh} = R_{sh} I_{sc}/V_{oc}$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Significance

Solar cells are the cornerstone of the **renewable-energy transition**. Nanotechnology has enabled three paradigm shifts:

1. **Reduced material consumption** — thin-film cells use $\sim 1/100$th the semiconductor thickness of bulk Si.
2. **Tunable optoelectronics** — quantum-dot and perovskite cells can be engineered for indoor, tandem, or space applications.
3. **Novel geometries** — 3D nanostructured (nanowire, nanocone) cells decouple light absorption from carrier collection, breaking the SQ limit indirectly.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Open-Circuit Voltage $V_{oc}$ from First Principles

**Starting point:** The current-voltage equation under open circuit ($I = 0$, $V = V_{oc}$, assuming $R_s \to 0$, $R_{sh} \to \infty$).

$$0 = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 1:** Rearrange to isolate the exponential.

$$I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right] = I_{ph}$$

**Step 2:** Add 1 to both sides and divide by $I_0$.

$$\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1 = \dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0}$$

**Step 3:** Add 1 to both sides.

$$\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) = \dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1$$

**Step 4:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides.

$$\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)$$

**Step 5:** Multiply both sides by $\dfrac{nkT}{q}$ to obtain the final expression.

$$\boxed{\,V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** Because $I_{ph} \gg I_0$ under AM1.5G, $V_{oc} \approx \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln(I_{ph}/I_0)$. This means **a smaller $I_0$ (less recombination) directly gives a higher voltage and therefore higher efficiency** — this is why passivation and low-defect nanostructuring matter.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Efficiency $\eta$ in Terms of Measurable Quantities

The power delivered to an external load at operating point $(V, I)$ is $P = VI$. The **maximum power point** $(V_{mp}, I_{mp})$ satisfies $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$.

**Step 1:** Write the implicit I–V relation (ideal diode, no parasitic R).

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 2:** Define power.

$$P = VI = V \left\{ I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1 \right] \right\}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to $V$.

$$\dfrac{dP}{dV} = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{mp}}{kT}\right) - 1 \right] - V_{mp} I_0 \cdot \dfrac{q}{kT} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{mp}}{kT}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back the expression for $I$ at the MPP and set $dP/dV = 0$.

$$I_{ph} = I_{mp} + V_{mp} I_0 \cdot \dfrac{q}{kT} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{mp}}{kT}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Divide by $I_{ph}$ and rearrange.

$$\dfrac{I_{mp}}{I_{ph}} = 1 - \dfrac{V_{mp} I_0 \dfrac{q}{kT} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{mp}}{kT}\right)}{I_{ph}}$$

**Step 6:** Use the definition of fill factor $FF = V_{mp} I_{mp} / (V_{oc} I_{sc})$ and recognize $I_{sc} = I_{ph}$.

$$P_{max} = V_{mp} I_{mp} = FF \cdot V_{oc} I_{sc}$$

**Step 7:** Write the final efficiency expression.

$$\boxed{\,\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \, I_{sc} \, FF}{P_{in}}\,}$$

where $P_{in}$ is the incident optical power density (1000 W/m² under AM1.5G standard).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 7-mark derivation)

**Given:** A Si solar cell under AM1.5G (1000 W/m²) has:
- $I_{ph} = 3.20$ A
- $I_0 = 1.0 \times 10^{-12}$ A
- $n = 1.30$
- $T = 300$ K
- $FF = 0.78$

**Find:** $V_{oc}$, $I_{sc}$, $P_{max}$, and $\eta$ if cell area = $100\,\text{cm}^2$.

**Step 1:** Compute thermal voltage.

$$V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} = \dfrac{(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.02585 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $V_{oc}$.

$$V_{oc} = n V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right) = (1.30)(0.02585) \ln\!\left(\dfrac{3.20}{10^{-12}} + 1\right)$$

$$V_{oc} = (0.03361) \ln(3.20 \times 10^{12}) = (0.03361)(28.79)$$

$$\boxed{V_{oc} \approx 0.967 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 3:** Identify $I_{sc}$.

$$\boxed{I_{sc} \approx I_{ph} = 3.20 \text{ A}}$$

**Step 4:** Compute $P_{max}$.

$$P_{max} = V_{oc} I_{sc} FF = (0.967)(3.20)(0.78)$$

$$\boxed{P_{max} \approx 2.413 \text{ W}}$$

**Step 5:** Compute incident power.

$$P_{in} = (1000 \text{ W/m}^2)(100 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2) = 10 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6:** Compute efficiency.

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{2.413}{10}$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 24.13\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Answer Hint:** Show all units clearly. $V_{oc}$ in volts, $I_{sc}$ in amperes, $P$ in watts. Final $\eta$ should be a percentage to 2 decimal places. State the AM1.5G conditions explicitly.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison: Generations of Solar Cells

| Generation | Material Class | Typical $\eta$ (lab) | Nano-feature | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1st Gen** | Crystalline Si (c-Si, mc-Si) | 26.7 % (PERL, UNSW) | Surface texturing, ARC | High (Module 4) |
| **2nd Gen** | Thin-film (a-Si, CdTe, CIGS) | 22.1 % (CdTe, First Solar) | Nano-grain boundaries | Medium |
| **3rd Gen** | DSSC, organic, perovskite | 26.1 % (perovskite/Si tandem) | Quantum dots, TiO₂ NPs | **Very High (Nanotech focus)** |
| **4th Gen** | Multi-junction, intermediate band | 47.1 % (6-junc. concentrator) | Quantum-dot superlattices | Conceptual |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently tests DSSC and perovskite cells.** Be prepared to draw the TiO₂ nanoporous electrode, the dye monolayer, the I⁻/I₃⁻ redox electrolyte, and the Pt counter-electrode.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Operating Principle of a Solar Cell

```mermaid
    E -.thermalization.-> X
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Fill Factor" (FF) of a solar cell. State the factors affecting it.**

**Model Answer:**
The Fill Factor is the ratio of the maximum obtainable power to the product of short-circuit current and open-circuit voltage:

$$FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$$

It is a dimensionless number between 0 and 1 (ideal Si cell ≈ 0.83). Factors affecting FF:
1. **Series resistance** $R_s$ — higher $R_s$ decreases FF.
2. **Shunt resistance** $R_{sh}$ — lower $R_{sh}$ decreases FF.
3. **Ideality factor** $n$ — non-ideal diodes ($n > 1$) lower FF.
4. **Temperature** — higher T lowers $V_{oc}$ and hence FF.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip (3 marks):** Writing the formula = 1 mark, stating the ideal range = 1 mark, listing any 2 factors = 1 mark.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is a Dye-Sensitized Solar Cell (DSSC)? List its main components.**

**Model Answer:**
A **DSSC** is a 3rd-generation photovoltaic device that mimics photosynthesis by separating the light absorption function (dye) from the charge transport function (TiO₂).

**Main components:**
1. **Transparent conducting oxide (TCO) substrate** (FTO/ITO) on glass.
2. **Nanoporous TiO₂ film** ($\sim 10\,\mu$m thick, $\sim 20$ nm particles) — electron transporter.
3. **Monolayer of dye sensitizer** (e.g., N3 or N719 Ruthenium complex) — light absorber.
4. **Redox electrolyte** (I⁻/I₃⁻ in organic solvent) — charge mediator.
5. **Platinum counter-electrode** — catalyst for I₃⁻ reduction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip (3 marks):** Definition = 1 mark, any 4 components named with one-line function = 2 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Explain the working principle of a solar cell with a neat I–V characteristic curve and equivalent circuit. Derive the expression for open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ and solar cell efficiency $\eta$.

**Model Solution Outline:**

#### (a) Working Principle and Equivalent Circuit — **(7 Marks)**

- **Working principle (3 marks):**
  - Photon with $h\nu \geq E_g$ generates an e⁻–h⁺ pair in the depletion region.
  - Built-in potential $V_{bi}$ separates the carriers.
  - Electrons collected at n-side, holes at p-side — current flows in external circuit.
  - Band diagram (E vs x) showing $E_c$, $E_v$, $E_{Fn}$, $E_{Fp}$, photogeneration arrow.

- **Equivalent circuit (2 marks):**
  - Photocurrent source $I_{ph}$ in parallel with diode $I_D$, $R_{sh}$, and series $R_s$. (Draw clearly.)

- **I–V characteristics (2 marks):**
  - Fourth-quadrant power-generating curve.
  - Mark $I_{sc}$ (y-intercept at $V=0$), $V_{oc}$ (x-intercept at $I=0$), MPP, and rectangle of $P_{max}$.

#### (b) Derivation of $V_{oc}$ and $\eta$ — **(7 Marks)**

- **$V_{oc}$ derivation (3.5 marks):** Use Section 3.1 derivation above. Show all 5 steps. Conclude with:
$$V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)$$

- **$\eta$ derivation (3.5 marks):** Use Section 3.2 derivation above. Show all 7 steps. Conclude with:
$$\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} I_{sc} FF}{P_{in}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Discuss in detail the construction and working of a Dye-Sensitized Solar Cell (DSSC). Compare its advantages and disadvantages with conventional silicon solar cells.

**Model Solution Outline:**

#### (a) Construction and Working — **(7 Marks)**

- **Construction (3 marks):** Five-layered structure (TCO/TiO₂/dye/electrolyte/Pt) — list and diagram.
- **Working mechanism (4 marks):**
  1. Dye absorbs photon: $S + h\nu \rightarrow S^*$
  2. Excited dye injects electron into TiO₂ CB: $S^* \rightarrow S^+ + e^-_{\text{CB}}$
  3. Electron percolates through TiO₂ to FTO.
  4. Through external load to Pt counter-electrode.
  5. At Pt: $\text{I}_3^- + 2e^- \rightarrow 3\text{I}^-$
  6. At dye: $2\text{S}^+ + 3\text{I}^- \rightarrow 2\text{S} + \text{I}_3^-$ (regeneration).
  7. Cycle repeats.

- **Energy level diagram (1 mark):** Show dye LUMO > TiO₂ CB, dye HOMO < I⁻/I₃⁻ redox potential.

#### (b) Advantages and Disadvantages vs c-Si — **(7 Marks)**

| Feature | DSSC | c-Si |
|---|---|---|
| Efficiency (lab) | $\sim 13$–$15\%$ | $\sim 26.7\%$ |
| Cost | Low (roll-to-roll possible) | High (purification) |
| Flexibility | Yes (plastic substrate) | Rigid (wafer) |
| Low-light performance | **Better** (dye absorption broader) | Worse |
| Stability | Poor (electrolyte leaks) | Excellent (30+ years) |
| Temperature coeff. | Lower (good in heat) | Higher |
| Toxicity | Ru dye + iodine organic solvent | None |

(2 marks for advantages, 2 marks for disadvantages, 3 marks for tabular comparison and conclusion.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Solar Cell questions (from KTU board examiner observations):**
> 1. **Forgetting to state AM1.5G conditions** when quoting efficiency values — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up $V_{oc}$ and $V_{mp}$** in the fill-factor formula — costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **No band diagram** in p–n junction questions — costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Using $I_{sc} = I_{ph}$ without justification** (only true for ideal $R_s = 0$, $R_{sh} = \infty$).
> 5. **Skipping units** in numerical problems — immediate ½ mark deduction per quantity.
> 6. **Drawing the I–V curve in the wrong quadrant** — must be in the **fourth quadrant** ($V > 0$, $I > 0$ but the cell delivers power).
> 7. **In DSSC diagrams, missing the Pt catalyst layer** — common oversight.
> 8. **Confusing ETL and HTM** in perovskite cell — Electron Transport Layer (TiO₂/SnO₂) vs Hole Transport Material (Spiro-OMeTAD).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solar Cells — Final 2-Minute Revision Bullet List:**
> - ☑ **Photovoltaic effect** = photon → e⁻–h⁺ pair → separated by junction field.
> - ☑ **Three loss mechanisms:** transmission, thermalization, recombination.
> - ☑ **Equivalent circuit:** $I_{ph} \parallel \text{diode} \parallel R_{sh}$, all in series with $R_s$.
> - ☑ **I–V equation** (ideal): $I = I_{ph} - I_0[\exp(qV/kT) - 1]$.
> - ☑ **$V_{oc}$** = $\dfrac{nkT}{q}\ln(I_{ph}/I_0 + 1)$.
> - ☑ **$I_{sc}$** $\approx I_{ph}$ (linear with light intensity).
> - ☑ **Fill Factor** $FF = V_{mp}I_{mp}/(V_{oc}I_{sc})$, ideal Si ≈ 0.83.
> - ☑ **Efficiency** $\eta = V_{oc} I_{sc} FF / P_{in}$.
> - ☑ **Shockley–Queisser limit** for single junction ≈ 33.7 %.
> - ☑ **AM1.5G** = standard test spectrum, 1000 W/m², 25 °C.
> - ☑ **1st Gen** = c-Si (26.7 % max), 2nd Gen = thin-film, 3rd Gen = DSSC/perovskite/QD.
> - ☑ **DSSC components:** FTO + TiO₂-NP + dye + I⁻/I₃⁻ electrolyte + Pt.
> - ☑ **Perovskite absorber:** CH₃NH₃PbI₃ — bandgap ≈ 1.55 eV, tunable.
> - ☑ **Quantum dot bandgap** blueshift: $E_g^{\text{eff}} = E_g^{\text{bulk}} + h^2/(8m^*L^2)$.
> - ☑ **Plasmonic enhancement:** Au/Ag nanoparticles increase local field and absorption.
> - ☑ **Nanowires** decouple light absorption from carrier collection.
> - ☑ **Multi-junction tandem** cells exceed SQ limit (47.1 % achieved).
> - ☑ **EQE** = electrons collected / photons incident (per wavelength).

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

An **Organic Solar Cell (OSC)** is a photovoltaic device belonging to the **third-generation solar technology** family, in which the **photoactive layer** is composed of π-conjugated **organic semiconductors** — either small molecules (e.g., phthalocyanines, pentacene) or conjugated polymers (e.g., **P3HT, PTB7, PCDTBT**) — sandwiched between two electrodes of differing work functions. Light absorption in these materials generates a tightly bound electron-hole pair known as an **exciton** (binding energy ≈ 0.2–1.0 eV), which must be separated at a **donor–acceptor (D–A) heterointerface** before charge collection can occur at the electrodes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** *"An organic solar cell is a photovoltaic device whose active layer consists of carbon-based conjugated organic materials that absorb photons, generate excitons, and convert them into electrical current via dissociation at a donor–acceptor junction."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Think of an organic solar cell as a **"two-flavor sponge sandwich"**:

- The **bread** slices are the transparent electrodes (like indium tin oxide — ITO) and a metallic back contact (like aluminum).
- The **filling** is the photoactive layer — a blended mixture of two different "flavors" of organic material (a **donor** and an **acceptor**).
- When sunlight (photons) hits the sandwich, the **donor flavor** captures the light energy and creates an **exciton** — a tightly bound "energy package" of a positive and negative charge stuck together.
- This energy package must find the **boundary between the two flavors** (the D–A interface) within a very short distance (≈ 10 nm) or it dies. The sponge is mixed in a special nano-pattern so that **every exciton is close to an interface**.
- At the interface, the package splits: the electron hops to the **acceptor** and the hole stays in the **donor**, then they both race to opposite electrodes, creating a current you can use.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "organic"?** Because the active materials are carbon-based (with hydrogen, nitrogen, sulfur, etc.) — like the plastic in a milk bottle — but engineered at the molecular level to conduct electricity. They are *not* like the metals in a wire; they are *semiconductors* made of long chains of carbon atoms with alternating single and double bonds (**conjugation**).

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power Conversion Efficiency | **PCE (η)** | 5% – 19% (lab), 8% – 13% (commercial) | % of incident sunlight converted to electricity |
| Open-Circuit Voltage | **V_oc** | 0.5 – 1.1 V | Voltage when no current flows |
| Short-Circuit Current Density | **J_sc** | 5 – 25 mA/cm² | Current per unit area when V = 0 |
| Fill Factor | **FF** | 0.5 – 0.8 | "Squareness" of the J–V curve |
| Exciton Diffusion Length | **L_D** | **5 – 20 nm** | Distance an exciton can travel before recombining |

> [!TIP]
> The **exciton diffusion length of 5–20 nm** is the most critical design constraint in OSCs. It is the reason BHJ morphology exists!
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Five-Stage Photocurrent Generation Process

The conversion of a photon into collected electrons in an OSC proceeds through **five sequential stages**, each with characteristic efficiency losses:

### Stage 1: Photon Absorption
- A photon of energy $E_{ph} = h\nu$ strikes the **photoactive layer**.
- If $E_{ph} \geq E_g$ (bandgap of the donor), the photon is absorbed and an electron is promoted from the **HOMO** to the **LUMO** of the donor molecule.
- The semiconductor absorption coefficient is governed by the **Beer–Lambert law**:

$$I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha x}$$

where $I(x)$ is the intensity at depth $x$, $I_0$ is the incident intensity, and $\alpha$ is the absorption coefficient (typically $10^5$ – $10^6$ cm$^{-1}$ for organic semiconductors, meaning most light is absorbed within **100 nm**).

### Stage 2: Exciton Generation
- The excited electron remains coulombically bound to the hole it left behind, forming a **Frenkel exciton** with binding energy $E_b \approx 0.2$ – $1.0$ eV.
- This is in stark contrast to inorganic silicon, where thermal energy at room temperature (kT ≈ 0.025 eV) easily separates the electron-hole pair.

### Stage 3: Exciton Diffusion
- The exciton travels by **Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET)** or **Dexter transfer** through the donor material.
- Diffusion length follows:

$$L_D = \sqrt{D \cdot \tau}$$

where $D$ is the exciton diffusion coefficient and $\tau$ is the exciton lifetime. Typical $L_D$ ≈ 5–20 nm. If the exciton does not reach a D–A interface within $L_D$, it **recombines** (geminate recombination) and is lost.

### Stage 4: Exciton Dissociation (Charge Transfer State → Free Carriers)
- At the **D–A heterointerface**, the electron transfers to the acceptor (driven by the LUMO offset $\Delta E_{LUMO} \geq 0.3$ eV typically).
- The hole remains on the donor. This is the **charge-transfer (CT) state** which then dissociates into **free polarons** under the built-in field of the device.
- The minimum driving force required is given by:

$$\Delta E_{LUMO}^{min} \approx \frac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r r}$$

where $r$ is the electron-hole separation at the interface and $\varepsilon_r$ is the relative permittivity (typically 3–4 for organics, much lower than Si's 11.7, making dissociation harder).

### Stage 5: Charge Transport & Collection
- Free holes move through the donor phase to the **anode** (typically ITO/PEDOT:PSS).
- Free electrons move through the acceptor phase to the **cathode** (typically Al, Ca, or Ag).
- Charge-carrier mobility $\mu$ in organics is low (≈ $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$ cm²/V·s), so the active layer must be thin (≈ 100–300 nm) to avoid recombination.

## 2.2 Why Bulk Heterojunction (BHJ)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **planar bilayer junction** (like the one in 1970s OSCs) failed because only excitons within $L_D$ ≈ 10 nm of the planar interface could be collected. This meant the active layer had to be **≤ 20 nm thick**, which is **too thin to absorb enough light** (only ~30% absorption).

**Solution:** Mix the donor and acceptor at the **nanoscale** so that **every point in the bulk is within $L_D$ of an interface**. This is the **Bulk Heterojunction (BHJ)** concept (introduced by Heeger, 1995, Nobel Prize 2000).

- Optimal domain size: ≈ 10–20 nm (≈ $L_D$)
- Bicontinuous interpenetrating network: required for both exciton dissociation **and** charge transport.

## 2.3 Device Architecture

The standard **conventional** OSC stack (bottom-illuminated):

$$\text{Glass} \mid \text{ITO (anode)} \mid \text{PEDOT:PSS (HTL)} \mid \text{Active Layer (D:A blend)} \mid \text{ETL/Cathode (e.g., Ca or LiF)} \mid \text{Al}$$

The **inverted** architecture swaps the polarity:

$$\text{Glass} \mid \text{ITO (cathode)} \mid \text{ZnO (ETL)} \mid \text{Active Layer} \mid \text{MoO}_3 \mid \text{Ag}$$

> Inverted structures offer better **air stability**, which is critical for commercialization.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$ | Power Conversion Efficiency | Dimensionless (%) |
| 2 | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mpp} \cdot J_{mpp}}{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}}$ | Fill Factor (ratio of max power rectangle to $V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}$ rectangle) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{1}{e}\left(\vert E_{HOMO}^{Donor} \vert - \vert E_{LUMO}^{Acceptor} \vert\right) - 0.3$ | Open-circuit voltage from energy levels (eV) | Volts |
| 4 | $J_{sc} = q \int_{\lambda_1}^{\lambda_2} EQE(\lambda) \cdot \Phi_{ph}(\lambda) \, d\lambda$ | Short-circuit current density from External Quantum Efficiency | A/m² |
| 5 | $L_D = \sqrt{D \cdot \tau}$ | Exciton diffusion length | meters |
| 6 | $I(x) = I_0 e^{-\alpha x}$ | Beer–Lambert absorption (use $\alpha$ not $\vert\alpha\vert$ in tables) | W/m² |
| 7 | $\mu = \dfrac{v_d}{E}$ | Carrier mobility (low-field drift velocity per unit field) | cm²/(V·s) |
| 8 | $\alpha_{abs} = 4\pi k / \lambda$ | Absorption coefficient from extinction coefficient $k$ | cm⁻¹ |
| 9 | $R_s \cdot J_{sc} + V_{oc}\left(1 - \dfrac{J}{J_{sat}}\right) = V$ | Implicit Shockley diode equation for solar cell | Volts |
| 10 | $EQE(\lambda) = \eta_{abs} \cdot \eta_{diff} \cdot \eta_{diss} \cdot \eta_{coll}$ | Total EQE = product of four sub-efficiencies | Dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility:** OSCs are used in **flexible, lightweight, semi-transparent, and roll-to-roll manufactured solar panels** — applications where rigid silicon cannot compete (e.g., building-integrated photovoltaics, wearable electronics, indoor IoT sensors).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Power Conversion Efficiency (PCE)

### Step 1: Define Input Power
The incident solar power density on the device (AM 1.5G standard spectrum) is:

$$P_{in} = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2 = 100 \text{ mW/cm}^2$$

This is the standard **AM 1.5G** reference irradiance that all KTU / NREL certified efficiencies are measured against.

### Step 2: Define Output Power Density
For a solar cell under illumination, the **output power density** at any operating point $(V, J)$ is:

$$P_{out} = V \cdot J$$

The current density $J(V)$ in a real solar cell is modeled by the **single-diode equivalent circuit** (also called the Shockley solar cell equation):

$$J(V) = J_{ph} - J_0 \left[ \exp\left(\dfrac{V + J R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V + J R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

where:
- $J_{ph}$ = photo-generated current density
- $J_0$ = reverse saturation current density
- $R_s$ = series resistance (ideally 0)
- $R_{sh}$ = shunt resistance (ideally ∞)
- $n$ = ideality factor (1–2)
- $V_T = kT/e \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K (thermal voltage)

### Step 3: Maximum Power Point
To find the maximum power, set $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$. The maximum occurs at $(V_{mpp}, J_{mpp})$, and the **Fill Factor** is:

$$\boxed{FF = \dfrac{V_{mpp} \cdot J_{mpp}}{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}}}$$

The maximum output power is therefore:

$$P_{max} = V_{mpp} \cdot J_{mpp} = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}$$

### Step 4: PCE Definition
By definition:

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}}{P_{in}}$$

In terms of percentage:

$$\boxed{\eta \,(\%) = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}}{P_{in}} \times 100}$$

**Example numerical evaluation** (typical lab OSC):
- $V_{oc} = 0.80$ V
- $J_{sc} = 15$ mA/cm²
- $FF = 0.65$
- $P_{in} = 100$ mW/cm²

Then:

$$P_{max} = 0.65 \times 0.80 \times 15 = 7.8 \text{ mW/cm}^2$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{7.8}{100} \times 100 = 7.8\%$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Exciton Diffusion Length

Starting from Fick's second law of diffusion in 1D for an exciton density $n(x,t)$:

$$\dfrac{\partial n}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^2 n}{\partial x^2} - \dfrac{n}{\tau}$$

where $D$ is the exciton diffusion coefficient (units m²/s) and $\tau$ is the natural lifetime.

At steady state, $\partial n/\partial t = 0$, so:

$$D \dfrac{d^2 n}{dx^2} = \dfrac{n}{\tau}$$

Assume exponential decay solution $n(x) = n_0 e^{-x/L_D}$. Substituting:

$$D \cdot \dfrac{n_0}{L_D^2} e^{-x/L_D} = \dfrac{n_0}{\tau} e^{-x/L_D}$$

Cancelling $n_0 e^{-x/L_D}$:

$$\dfrac{D}{L_D^2} = \dfrac{1}{\tau}$$

Solving for $L_D$:

$$\boxed{L_D = \sqrt{D \cdot \tau}}$$

**Numerical example:** For P3HT, $D \approx 2 \times 10^{-3}$ cm²/s = $2 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s, and $\tau \approx 500$ ps = $5 \times 10^{-10}$ s. Then:

$$L_D = \sqrt{(2 \times 10^{-7})(5 \times 10^{-10})} = \sqrt{10^{-16}} = 10^{-8} \text{ m} = 10 \text{ nm}$$

This matches the empirical 5–20 nm range — confirming the design constraint.

## 3.3 EQE Decomposition Derivation

The External Quantum Efficiency is the probability that **one incident photon produces one electron** in the external circuit. It factorizes into four sequential probabilities:

$$\boxed{EQE(\lambda) = \eta_{A}(\lambda) \cdot \eta_{ED} \cdot \eta_{CT} \cdot \eta_{CC}}$$

where:
- $\eta_A(\lambda)$ = photon absorption probability (depends on $\alpha$ and thickness)
- $\eta_{ED}$ = exciton diffusion efficiency to the D–A interface
- $\eta_{CT}$ = charge transfer (exciton dissociation) efficiency at the interface
- $\eta_{CC}$ = charge collection efficiency at the electrodes

This is a **product**, not a sum — therefore **the weakest stage bottlenecks the entire device**. This is why optimizing morphology, interfaces, and electrodes all matter.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: OSCs vs Inorganic Solar Cells

| Parameter | Organic (OSC) | Inorganic (Si, GaAs) |
|---|---|---|
| Active material | π-conjugated polymers / small molecules | Crystalline Si, GaAs, CIGS |
| Bandgap $E_g$ | 1.0 – 3.0 eV (tunable by chemistry) | 1.1 eV (Si), 1.42 eV (GaAs) — fixed |
| Absorption coefficient | $10^5$ – $10^6$ cm⁻¹ (very high) | $10^3$ – $10^4$ cm⁻¹ |
| Required thickness | 100 – 300 nm | 100 – 300 μm (1000× thicker!) |
| Charge carrier type | Exciton → free polaron (via D–A junction) | Direct free e⁻ and h⁺ |
| Carrier mobility $\mu$ | $10^{-4}$ – $10^{-2}$ cm²/V·s | $10^2$ – $10^3$ cm²/V·s |
| $V_{oc}$ | 0.5 – 1.1 V | 0.6 – 1.1 V |
| Best lab PCE | ≈ 19% (single junction), 20%+ (tandem) | 26.8% (Si), 47.1% (GaAs multi-junction) |
| Stability | 1 – 10 years (improving) | 25+ years |
| Cost | Potentially very low (roll-to-roll) | Moderate (Si) to high (III–V) |
| Flexibility | Yes (plastic substrates) | No (rigid wafer) |
| Toxicity | Low (no Cd, Pb, As) | Some toxic elements in III–V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** OSCs sacrifice peak efficiency for **manufacturability, flexibility, and low cost**. They are **complementary** to silicon, not competitive with it for utility-scale power.
