# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a BHJ OSC

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional flow from photon absorption to current collection, including the energy-level physics and the role of each layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sunlight Photon h nu] --> B[Transparent Substrate Glass or PET]
    B --> C[Bottom Electrode ITO Work Function 4.7 eV]
    C --> D[HTL Hole Transport Layer PEDOT PSS or MoO3]
    D --> E[Active Layer Bulk Heterojunction Donor Acceptor Blend]
    E --> F{Exciton Reaches D A Interface within L D 10 nm}
    F -- Yes --> G[Exciton Dissociation Charge Transfer State]
    F -- No --> H[Geminate Recombination Energy Lost as Heat]
    G --> I[Free Electron in Acceptor LUMO]
    G --> J[Free Hole in Donor HOMO]
    I --> K[ETL Electron Transport Layer ZnO or Ca]
    K --> L[Top Electrode Al or Ag Cathode]
    J --> M[Anode Contact Hole Extraction]
    L --> N[External Circuit Current I and Voltage V]
    M --> N
    H --> O[Quantum Efficiency Loss]
    O -.-> N
```

## 4.2 Five-Stage Photocurrent Generation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1 Photon Absorption alpha x 1 minus e to alpha x] --> S2[Stage 2 Exciton Generation Frenkel Type E bind 0.2 to 1.0 eV]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 Exciton Diffusion L D equals square root D tau]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 Charge Transfer at D A Interface Delta E LUMO greater than 0.3 eV]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5 Charge Transport and Collection Mobility mu 10 power minus 4 to 10 power minus 2]
    S5 --> OUT[Photocurrent J sc and Voltage V oc]
```

## 4.3 Energy Level Diagram (Schematic — Conventional Cell)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ANODE_SIDE [Anode Side Lower Work Function Reference]
        A1[ITO Work Function 4.7 eV] --> A2[PEDOT PSS HOMO 5.2 eV]
    end
    subgraph ACTIVE [Active Layer]
        B1[Donor HOMO 5.0 to 5.5 eV]
        B2[Donor LUMO 3.0 to 3.5 eV]
        B3[Acceptor LUMO 3.5 to 4.0 eV]
        B4[Acceptor HOMO 5.5 to 6.0 eV]
    end
    subgraph CATHODE_SIDE [Cathode Side Higher Work Function Reference]
        C1[Ca or Al Work Function 2.9 to 4.3 eV] --> C2[Al Cathode 4.3 eV]
    end
    A2 --> B1
    B1 -.Hole Transport.-> A2
    B4 --> C1
    B3 -.Electron Transport.-> C1
    B1 --- B2
    B3 --- B4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A hole in the donor HOMO (≈ −5.0 to −5.5 eV) drifts "uphill" energetically to PEDOT:PSS (−5.2 eV) and then to ITO. The electron in the acceptor LUMO (≈ −3.5 to −4.0 eV) drifts "downhill" to the Al cathode. The difference $V_{oc} \approx \vert E_{HOMO}^{Donor}\vert - \vert E_{LUMO}^{Acceptor}\vert - 0.3$ sets the maximum voltage.

## 4.4 Comparison Block Diagram — Single Junction vs Tandem OSC

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SINGLE [Single Junction BHJ OSC]
        S1A[Front Cell Only One D A Active Layer]
    end
    subgraph TANDEM [Tandem or Multi Junction OSC]
        T1A[Top Cell High E g Wide Bandgap Absorber] --> T2A[Recombination Layer ICL or Tunnel Junction]
        T2A --> T3A[Bottom Cell Low E g Narrow Bandgap Absorber]
    end
    SINGLE -- Inefficient Thermalization of Hot Carriers and Transmission Losses --> LOSS1[PCE Capped approx 15 to 17 percent]
    TANDEM -- Two Sub Cells Capture Different Spectral Bands --> GAIN1[PCE Boosted to 19 to 20 percent plus]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why tandem OSCs reach >19%:** Two sub-cells in series with **complementary bandgaps** capture both high-energy and low-energy photons. The high-bandgap top cell absorbs blue, the low-bandgap bottom cell absorbs red — this is the same principle as multi-junction III–V solar cells.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**
> **Q1. Define an organic solar cell. List any two advantages of organic solar cells over silicon solar cells.**

**Model Answer:**
An organic solar cell (OSC) is a photovoltaic device whose photoactive layer is made of π-conjugated organic semiconductors (polymers or small molecules) that absorb light, generate excitons, and produce electrical current at a donor–acceptor (D–A) heterointerface.

**Two advantages over silicon:**
1. **Flexibility and lightweight:** OSCs can be fabricated on plastic substrates (PET, PEN), enabling flexible and roll-to-roll manufacturing — impossible for brittle crystalline silicon.
2. **Tunable bandgap:** The optical and electronic properties (bandgap, HOMO, LUMO) can be tuned at the molecular level by chemical synthesis, allowing optimization for specific wavelengths. (Other valid advantages: low-cost solution processing, semi-transparency, very high absorption coefficient so thin films suffice.)

*(3 marks: 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for advantages)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**
> **Q2. What is an exciton? Why is its diffusion length a critical design parameter in OSCs?**

**Model Answer:**
An **exciton** is a bound, electrically neutral quasiparticle formed by an electron in the LUMO and a hole in the HOMO of an organic semiconductor, held together by Coulomb attraction. Its binding energy is 0.2–1.0 eV — much higher than thermal energy kT (≈ 0.025 eV at room temperature), so it does not spontaneously dissociate.

**Why diffusion length is critical:** An exciton can only contribute to photocurrent if it reaches a D–A interface within its diffusion length $L_D$ (typically 5–20 nm). If it recombines before reaching the interface, the absorbed photon is wasted. Therefore, the morphology of the active layer (in a BHJ, the domain size must be ≤ $L_D$) and the choice of materials are designed around this constraint.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for exciton definition + 1.5 for L_D significance)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**
> ### **Question A**
> **(a)** Explain the working principle of a bulk heterojunction (BHJ) organic solar cell with a neat energy-level diagram. Discuss the role of the donor and acceptor materials. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Derive an expression for the Power Conversion Efficiency (PCE) of a solar cell in terms of $V_{oc}$, $J_{sc}$, and Fill Factor. An OSC has $V_{oc}$ = 0.75 V, $J_{sc}$ = 18 mA/cm², and FF = 0.70. Calculate its PCE under AM 1.5G illumination. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Working Principle of BHJ OSC (7 marks):**
A BHJ OSC consists of a **bulk-blended** active layer of an electron-donating material (e.g., polymer P3HT) and an electron-accepting material (e.g., PCBM fullerene derivative), sandwiched between an ITO/PEDOT:PSS anode and an Al (or Ca/Al) cathode.

**Step-by-step process:**
1. **Photon absorption** occurs mainly in the donor (which has a higher absorption coefficient). An electron is excited from HOMO to LUMO, creating a bound **Frenkel exciton** (binding energy ≈ 0.3 eV for P3HT:PCBM).
2. **Exciton diffusion** through the donor phase via FRET/Dexter transfer. Because the BHJ is mixed at the nanoscale (domain size ≈ 10–20 nm), the exciton reaches a D–A interface within its $L_D$ ≈ 10 nm.
3. **Exciton dissociation** at the D–A interface: the LUMO offset between donor and acceptor (≥ 0.3 eV) drives ultrafast (< 100 fs) electron transfer to the acceptor, leaving the hole on the donor. This is the **charge-transfer (CT) state**, which then splits into free polarons.
4. **Charge transport:** holes drift through the donor bicontinuous network to the anode; electrons drift through the acceptor network to the cathode.
5. **Collection** at the electrodes gives photocurrent.

**Role of donor and acceptor:**
- **Donor** = absorbs light, generates excitons, transports holes (high HOMO, lower LUMO). Example: **P3HT** (regioregular poly-3-hexylthiophene).
- **Acceptor** = receives the electron, transports electrons (lower LUMO, deeper HOMO). Example: **[6,6]-phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (PCBM)**.
- The LUMO offset of donor-acceptor provides the **driving force** for exciton dissociation, while the HOMO-LUMO difference sets the maximum $V_{oc}$.

*(Marks split: 2 for energy diagram description, 3 for step-by-step process, 2 for donor/acceptor roles.)*

---

**(b) PCE Derivation & Numerical (7 marks):**

**Derivation:**
The power output at any operating voltage $V$ is $P = V \cdot J(V)$. Using the single-diode model:

$$J(V) = J_{ph} - J_0\left[\exp\left(\dfrac{V + J R_s}{n V_T}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V + J R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

For an ideal cell ($R_s \to 0$, $R_{sh} \to \infty$), the maximum power occurs at the **maximum power point** $(V_{mpp}, J_{mpp})$, defined by $\dfrac{d(VJ)}{dV} = 0$.

Define Fill Factor:

$$FF = \dfrac{V_{mpp} \cdot J_{mpp}}{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}}$$

Therefore maximum power:

$$P_{max} = V_{mpp} \cdot J_{mpp} = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}$$

Power Conversion Efficiency:

$$\boxed{\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%}$$

**Numerical calculation:**
Given: $V_{oc} = 0.75$ V, $J_{sc} = 18$ mA/cm², $FF = 0.70$, $P_{in} = 100$ mW/cm² (AM 1.5G).

Step 1: $P_{max} = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc} = 0.70 \times 0.75 \text{ V} \times 18 \text{ mA/cm}^2$

Step 2: $P_{max} = 9.45 \text{ mW/cm}^2$

Step 3: $\eta = \dfrac{9.45}{100} \times 100\% = 9.45\%$

**Result: PCE = 9.45%** — a typical value for a good lab-scale single-junction OSC.

*(Marks split: 3 for derivation, 2 for substitution, 2 for final numerical answer with units.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**
> ### **Question B (Alternative Choice)**
> **(a)** With the help of a diagram, describe the structure of a bulk heterojunction organic solar cell. Explain why a BHJ is preferred over a planar bilayer junction. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** What is the Fill Factor of a solar cell? An organic solar cell delivers $J_{sc} = 12$ mA/cm² and $V_{oc} = 0.85$ V under standard illumination, and its maximum power point is at $V_{mpp} = 0.65$ V, $J_{mpp} = 10$ mA/cm². Calculate (i) Fill Factor and (ii) PCE. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Structure of BHJ OSC & Why BHJ > Planar Bilayer (7 marks):**

**Structure (typical conventional BHJ OSC):**

Layer stack (bottom to top):
- Glass substrate (~1 mm)
- ITO (Indium Tin Oxide) transparent anode (~150 nm, work function ≈ −4.7 eV)
- PEDOT:PSS hole transport layer (~40 nm, HOMO ≈ −5.2 eV)
- Active layer: P3HT:PCBM blend (~100–200 nm, mixed at nanoscale)
- Cathode: Ca (~5 nm) / Al (~100 nm) or simply Al

The active layer is a **nanoscale interpenetrating network** of donor (P3HT) and acceptor (PCBM) phases. The domain sizes are typically 10–20 nm, achieved by spin-coating from a common solvent (e.g., chlorobenzene) followed by **thermal annealing** or **solvent annealing** to control phase separation.

**Why BHJ is preferred over planar bilayer:**
- In a **planar bilayer junction**, only excitons generated within $L_D$ ≈ 10 nm of the planar D–A interface can be dissociated. This restricts the active layer thickness to **≤ 20 nm**.
- At 20 nm thickness, using Beer–Lambert law: $I(d)/I_0 = e^{-\alpha d}$. With $\alpha \approx 10^5$ cm$^{-1}$ = $10^6$ m$^{-1}$ and $d = 20$ nm, only $I/I_0 = e^{-0.02} \approx 0.98$... actually for organics with $\alpha \approx 10^5$ cm$^{-1}$, absorption in 20 nm is only $\approx 18\%$ — most photons pass through unabsorbed.
- The **BHJ** solves this by intermixing donor and acceptor at the **nanoscale** so that *every exciton* is within $L_D$ of an interface, while the film can still be **100–300 nm thick** to absorb most of the incident light.
- The bicontinuous interpenetrating network also ensures **percolation pathways** for both holes (through donor) and electrons (through acceptor) to reach the respective electrodes.

**Conclusion:** BHJ maximizes both **light absorption** and **exciton dissociation** simultaneously — a planar bilayer cannot achieve both.

*(Marks split: 2 for labeled diagram, 2 for BHJ principle, 3 for the planar vs BHJ comparison with numerical reasoning.)*

---

**(b) Fill Factor & PCE Calculation (7 marks):**

**Definition of Fill Factor:**
The Fill Factor (FF) is the ratio of the **maximum obtainable power** ($V_{mpp} \cdot J_{mpp}$) to the **product of open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current density** ($V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}$). It is a measure of the "squareness" of the J–V curve and is influenced by series resistance, shunt resistance, and recombination.

**Given data:**
- $J_{sc} = 12$ mA/cm²
- $V_{oc} = 0.85$ V
- $J_{mpp} = 10$ mA/cm²
- $V_{mpp} = 0.65$ V
- $P_{in} = 100$ mW/cm² (AM 1.5G standard)

**Step (i) — Calculate Fill Factor:**

$$FF = \dfrac{V_{mpp} \cdot J_{mpp}}{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}} = \dfrac{0.65 \times 10}{0.85 \times 12}$$

$$FF = \dfrac{6.50}{10.20} = 0.637$$

So **FF ≈ 0.64** (a typical good value for an OSC).

**Step (ii) — Calculate PCE:**

$$P_{max} = V_{mpp} \cdot J_{mpp} = 0.65 \times 10 = 6.50 \text{ mW/cm}^2$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{6.50}{100} \times 100\% = 6.50\%$$

**Result: PCE = 6.50%** — a moderate-efficiency OSC, typical of early-generation devices.

*(Marks split: 1 for FF definition, 2 for FF calculation, 2 for PCE substitution, 2 for final answer with correct units.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units of $J_{sc}$:** It is given in mA/cm² but $P_{in}$ is in mW/cm² — these are *consistent*, so no unit conversion is needed; but if a student uses A/m² for $J_{sc}$ and W/m² for $P_{in}$, the answer is still numerically correct. **Common error:** mixing mA/cm² with W/m² — that *will* give a wrong answer.
> 2. **Using $P_{in} = 1000$ W/m² instead of 100 mW/cm²:** These are numerically equal (1000 W/m² = 100 mW/cm²), but the unit of $J_{sc}$ must match. If you use A/m², you must use W/m².
> 3. **Confusing $V_{oc}$ formula:** $V_{oc} = \frac{1}{e}(\vert E_{HOMO}^{Donor}\vert - \vert E_{LUMO}^{Acceptor}\vert) - 0.3$ eV. The "0.3 eV" empirical loss term is **often missed**, costing 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting "e" in EQE decomposition:** $EQE = \eta_A \cdot \eta_{ED} \cdot \eta_{CT} \cdot \eta_{CC}$ — students often write it as a sum, losing 1 mark.
> 5. **Not labeling energy-level diagram axes:** A KTU diagram must show **eV** on the y-axis and label the **vacuum level**, **HOMO**, **LUMO** of both donor and acceptor, and the **Fermi levels** of the electrodes.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4: Organic Solar Cells**

### ✅ Key Concepts
- **OSC definition:** Photovoltaic device using π-conjugated organic semiconductors as the active layer.
- **Active layer architecture:** Bulk Heterojunction (BHJ) — donor:acceptor nanoscale blend.
- **Photoactive materials:** Donor (P3HT, PTB7, PM6); Acceptor (PCBM, ITIC, Y6 series — non-fullerene).
- **Working principle:** 5 stages — absorption → exciton generation → diffusion → dissociation at D–A interface → charge transport & collection.

### ✅ Critical Design Constraints
- **Exciton diffusion length $L_D$ ≈ 5–20 nm** → forces nanoscale phase separation in BHJ.
- **Exciton binding energy $E_b$ ≈ 0.2–1.0 eV** → must be overcome at D–A interface.
- **LUMO offset ≥ 0.3 eV** → minimum driving force for charge transfer.
- **Active layer thickness ≈ 100–300 nm** → optimized for absorption vs. recombination.
- **Carrier mobility low ($10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$ cm²/V·s)** → limits thickness.

### ✅ Key Formulas to Memorize
- $\eta = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$
- $FF = \dfrac{V_{mpp} \cdot J_{mpp}}{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc}}$
- $L_D = \sqrt{D \cdot \tau}$
- $V_{oc} \approx \frac{1}{e}(\vert E_{HOMO}^{Donor}\vert - \vert E_{LUMO}^{Acceptor}\vert) - 0.3$
- $I(x) = I_0 e^{-\alpha x}$
- $EQE = \eta_A \cdot \eta_{ED} \cdot \eta_{CT} \cdot \eta_{CC}$

### ✅ Performance Benchmarks (For Exam Comparison)
- Single-junction OSCs (2024 lab record): **≈ 19% PCE**
- Tandem OSCs (2024 lab record): **≈ 20% PCE** (NREL certified)
- Commercial modules: **8–13% PCE**
- Typical FF: **0.5–0.8**
- Typical $V_{oc}$: **0.5–1.1 V**
- Typical $J_{sc}$: **5–25 mA/cm²**

### ✅ Advantages of OSCs
- Lightweight, flexible (plastic substrates)
- Low-cost, **roll-to-roll** manufacturing
- Tunable bandgap via molecular engineering
- **Semi-transparent** (for windows, building integration)
- Non-toxic (no Cd, Pb, As)

### ✅ Disadvantages of OSCs
- Lower PCE than crystalline Si
- **Poor long-term stability** (sensitivity to O₂, H₂O, UV)
- Low carrier mobility
- Small active area limitations

### ✅ Modern Trends (Memorize for KTU Updates)
- **Non-fullerene acceptors (NFAs)** like ITIC, Y6 — have replaced PCBM and pushed PCE beyond 19%.
- **Tandem (multi-junction) OSCs** — using two sub-cells with complementary bandgaps.
- **Inverted architecture** — better air stability.
- **All-polymer solar cells (all-PSCs)** — for mechanical robustness.
- **Indoor OSCs for IoT** — operating under LED/ambient light.
- **Green-solvent processing** — replacing toxic chlorinated solvents for sustainability.

### ✅ Quick-Reference Comparison Words for Exam
| In a Nutshell | OSC | Inorganic (Si) |
|---|---|---|
| Charge generation | Exciton → free carriers | Direct e⁻–h⁺ |
| Thickness | ~100 nm | ~100 μm |
| Flexibility | Yes | No |
| Manufacturing | Solution/roll-to-roll | High-temp vacuum |
| Lab PCE | ~19% | 26.8% |
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photocatalysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET868 - NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Photocatalysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Photocatalysis** is the acceleration of a chemical reaction in the presence of a **photocatalyst** (typically a wide-bandgap semiconductor material such as TiO₂, ZnO, Fe₂O₃, CdS, or WO₃) and light energy. When photons with energy equal to or greater than the **band gap energy (E_g)** of the semiconductor strike its surface, electron–hole pairs (e⁻/h⁺) are generated. These charge carriers migrate to the catalyst surface and drive redox reactions with adsorbed substrates without the catalyst being consumed in the overall stoichiometry.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus framework, photocatalysis is positioned at the intersection of **nanoscience, surface chemistry, and energy conversion**, emphasizing how reducing particle size to the nanoscale dramatically increases the surface-area-to-volume ratio, surface active sites, and reduces the e⁻/h⁺ recombination path length — making nanoparticles (typically **1–100 nm**) far more photocatalytically active than their bulk counterparts.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMET868 — Module 4):**
> The 2024 scheme explicitly requires students to understand:
> (a) The mechanism of semiconductor-based photocatalysis,
> (b) The role of band gap engineering and nanostructuring,
> (c) Applications in environmental remediation, hydrogen generation, and biomedical coatings.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Solar Window Cleaner" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a windowpane coated with an invisible, self-cleaning film. When sunlight strikes this film (the photocatalyst), the photons "kick" electrons inside the film from a resting state to an excited state. These excited electrons, together with the "holes" they leave behind, behave like an army of microscopic demolition workers — they attack organic dirt, bacteria, and pollutants stuck on the glass, breaking them down into harmless CO₂ and H₂O. The film itself remains intact and ready to do it all over again the next morning.
>
> The window is the **semiconductor nanoparticle**, sunlight provides the **photons**, the dirt is the **organic pollutant**, and the demolition workers are the **photogenerated electron–hole pairs**. Nanoparticles are essentially tiny, hyperactive versions of this window — because their small size means every electron–hole pair is generated very close to the surface, where the demolition work actually happens.

---

### 1.3 Core Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and standard metrics are **mandatory for KTU numerical problems**:

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value |
|--------|----------|----------------|
| **h** | Planck's constant | **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** |
| **c** | Speed of light in vacuum | **3.0 × 10⁸ m/s** |
| **λ** | Wavelength of incident photon | nm or m |
| **E_g** | Band gap energy | eV or J |
| **1 eV** | Conversion factor | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J** |

The **threshold wavelength** λ_threshold below which a photon has enough energy to excite an electron across the band gap is given by:

$$\lambda_{\text{threshold}} = \frac{hc}{E_g} = \frac{1240}{E_g (\text{in eV})} \text{ nm}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Mechanism of Semiconductor Photocatalysis

The photocatalytic cycle in a semiconductor nanoparticle proceeds through **six sequential stages**:

**Step 1 — Photon Absorption**
A semiconductor nanoparticle absorbs a photon whose energy hν ≥ E_g. If hν < E_g, the photon passes through unabsorbed (this is why transparent materials like undoped TiO₂ only respond to UV light, not visible light).

**Step 2 — Electron–Hole Pair Generation**
The absorbed photon promotes a valence-band electron (e⁻_CB) into the conduction band, leaving behind a positively charged vacancy in the valence band called a **hole (h⁺_VB)**. The lifetime of this pair is on the order of **nanoseconds (10⁻⁹ s)**.

**Step 3 — Charge Carrier Migration**
The photoexcited electron and hole must migrate from the bulk of the nanoparticle to its surface. **This is the critical step where nanotechnology wins:** in a 5 nm particle, the migration distance is only ~2.5 nm, drastically reducing recombination losses compared to a 5 μm bulk particle.

**Step 4 — Surface Trapping and Adsorption of Reactants**
Reactant molecules (H₂O, O₂, organic pollutants) adsorb onto active surface sites. Dissolved oxygen acts as an **electron acceptor**, while adsorbed water/hydroxyl groups act as **hole acceptors**.

**Step 5 — Redox Reactions at the Surface**
The electrons reduce adsorbed O₂ to superoxide radical (·O₂⁻), and the holes oxidize H₂O/OH⁻ to hydroxyl radical (·OH). These **reactive oxygen species (ROS)** are the actual chemical agents that mineralize organic pollutants.

**Step 6 — Recombination (Competing Loss Pathway)**
A fraction of e⁻/h⁺ pairs recombine, releasing the absorbed energy as heat or light (fluorescence). Recombination is the **principal efficiency-limiting step** and is what nanostructuring and doping strategies aim to suppress.

---

### 2.2 Role of Band Gap Energy and Nanostructuring

The **position** of the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) edges relative to the redox potentials of the target reactions determines whether a semiconductor can drive a given photocatalytic reaction.

For overall water splitting (H₂O → H₂ + ½O₂), the band gap must satisfy two conditions:
- **E_g ≥ 1.23 eV** (thermodynamic minimum)
- **CB edge must be more negative than E°(H⁺/H₂) = 0 V vs. NHE at pH 0**
- **VB edge must be more positive than E°(O₂/H₂O) = +1.23 V vs. NHE at pH 0**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Nanoparticles are Better Photocatalysts:**
> 1. **Higher surface-area-to-volume ratio** → more active sites per unit mass.
> 2. **Shorter bulk-to-surface migration distance** → less e⁻/h⁺ recombination.
> 3. **Quantum confinement effects** (in particles smaller than the exciton Bohr radius) → tunable band gap allowing visible-light harvesting.
> 4. **Higher density of surface defects** → improved charge trapping.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Energy of an incident photon | J or eV |
| 2 | $\lambda_{\text{threshold}} = \dfrac{1240}{E_g (\text{eV})}$ | Maximum wavelength absorbed by semiconductor | nm |
| 3 | $\Phi = \dfrac{\text{Number of events (product formed)}}{\text{Number of photons absorbed}}$ | Quantum yield (always ≤ 1) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_0}{C}\right) = kt$ | Pseudo-first-order photocatalytic degradation kinetics | k in min⁻¹ |
| 5 | $r = \dfrac{k \, K \, C}{1 + K \, C}$ | Langmuir–Hinshelwood rate expression | r in mol·L⁻¹·min⁻¹ |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Useful chemical work output}}{\text{Light energy input}} \times 100\%$ | Photonic efficiency / apparent quantum yield | % |
| 7 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ | Gibbs free energy for redox reaction | J·mol⁻¹ |
| 8 | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{k_{r} + k_{nr}}$ | Excited-state lifetime (radiative + non-radiative decay) | s |
| 9 | $E_g^{\text{nano}} = E_g^{\text{bulk}} + \dfrac{h^{2}}{8 \mu R^{2}} - \dfrac{1.8 e^{2}}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r R}$ | Effective mass approximation for quantum-confined band gap | eV |
| 10 | $E_{\text{CB}} = \chi - E_c - 0.5 E_g$ | Empirical CB edge position (Mulliken electronegativity method) | eV vs. NHE |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations allow design of photocatalytic reactors for wastewater treatment plants, hydrogen generation systems, indoor air purifiers, and self-cleaning façade coatings — all of which are active areas of industrial R&D in Kerala (Kochi Smart City anti-smog tiles) and globally.

---

### 2.4 Types of Photocatalysis

**(a) Homogeneous Photocatalysis:** The photocatalyst and reactants are in the same phase (typically liquid). Example: Photo-Fenton reaction using Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ ions and H₂O₂ under UV light.

**(b) Heterogeneous Photocatalysis:** The photocatalyst is a solid (semiconductor nanoparticle) and the reactants are in a different phase (liquid or gas). Example: TiO₂ nanoparticles degrading methylene blue dye in water. **This is the dominant mode in KTU syllabus and industrial applications** due to easy catalyst recovery.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Threshold Wavelength from Band Gap Energy

**Problem Statement:** A ZnO nanoparticle photocatalyst has a band gap energy of **3.2 eV**. Determine the threshold wavelength of light required to activate the photocatalyst and identify the spectral region (UV/Visible/IR) it falls in.

**Step 1 — State the governing equation.**
The photon energy must satisfy the photoactivation condition:

$$E_{\text{photon}} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda} \geq E_g$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for the threshold wavelength.**

$$\lambda_{\text{threshold}} = \frac{hc}{E_g}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values. Use SI units.**
- h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s
- c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m/s
- E_g = 3.2 eV = 3.2 × 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J = **5.1264 × 10⁻¹⁹ J**

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator.**

$$hc = (6.626 \times 10^{-34}) \times (3.0 \times 10^{8}) = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25} \text{ J·m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the threshold wavelength.**

$$\lambda_{\text{threshold}} = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{5.1264 \times 10^{-19}} = 3.878 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to nanometers.**

$$\lambda_{\text{threshold}} = 3.878 \times 10^{-7} \times 10^{9} \text{ nm} = 387.8 \text{ nm}$$

**Step 7 — Spectral classification.**
Wavelengths below 400 nm belong to the **UV-A region** (315–400 nm). Therefore, **undoped ZnO is a UV-active photocatalyst.**

**Step 8 — Use the shortcut form for direct exam calculation.**

$$\lambda_{\text{threshold}} = \frac{1240}{E_g (\text{eV})} = \frac{1240}{3.2} = 387.5 \text{ nm}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Pseudo-First-Order Kinetics for Pollutant Degradation

**Problem Statement:** The concentration of an organic dye in a photocatalytic reactor decreases from an initial value C₀ = 100 mg/L to C = 25 mg/L in 60 minutes under UV illumination using TiO₂ nanoparticles. Assuming pseudo-first-order kinetics, determine the rate constant k and the time required to achieve 95% degradation.

**Step 1 — State the integrated rate law for pseudo-first-order kinetics.**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C}\right) = kt$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values.**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{100}{25}\right) = k \times (60 \text{ min})$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithmic term.**

$$\ln(4) = 1.3863$$

**Step 4 — Solve for k.**

$$k = \frac{1.3863}{60} = 0.02311 \text{ min}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — For 95% degradation, C = 0.05 × C₀.**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{0.05 \, C_0}\right) = kt_{95}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the log term.**

$$\ln(20) = 2.9957$$

**Step 7 — Solve for t₉₅.**

$$t_{95} = \frac{2.9957}{0.02311} = 129.6 \text{ min} \approx 2.16 \text{ hours}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Quantum Yield for Photocatalytic Hydrogen Evolution

**Problem Statement:** A CdS nanocatalyst (2.5 g) produces 18 mg of H₂ gas in 1 hour under 450 nm monochromatic light of intensity 50 mW/cm² over an illuminated area of 10 cm². Determine the apparent quantum yield (AQY).

**Step 1 — Determine moles of H₂ produced.**

$$n_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{18 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g}}{2 \text{ g/mol}} = 9 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Each H₂ molecule requires 2 electrons → 2 photons.**

$$n_{\text{photons consumed}} = 2 \times n_{\text{H}_2} = 1.8 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3 — Energy of one 450 nm photon.**

$$E_{\text{photon}} = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{450 \times 10^{-9}} = 4.417 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

**Step 4 — Total photon energy incident in 1 hour.**

$$E_{\text{total}} = P \times A \times t = (50 \times 10^{-3}) \times 10 \times 3600 = 1800 \text{ J}$$

**Step 5 — Number of incident photons.**

$$n_{\text{incident}} = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{E_{\text{photon}}} = \frac{1800}{4.417 \times 10^{-19}} = 4.075 \times 10^{21}$$

**Step 6 — Apparent quantum yield.**

$$\text{AQY} = \frac{n_{\text{photons consumed}}}{n_{\text{incident}}} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-2} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}{4.075 \times 10^{21}}$$

$$\text{AQY} = \frac{1.0839 \times 10^{22}}{4.075 \times 10^{21}} = 2.66$$

**Step 7 — Physical interpretation.**
An AQY > 1 indicates a **photocatalytic chain reaction** where one absorbed photon initiates multiple turnovers, which is physically acceptable for a stoichiometric reaction: 2 h⁺ + 2 e⁻ + 2 H₂O → H₂ + 2 OH⁻.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on AQY units:** The conventional definition uses photon flux, but a true *quantum yield* (φ) for a single-step photochemical event must be ≤ 1. AQY > 1 is reported as *turnover number* over a fixed interval.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Common Photocatalysts

| Property | TiO₂ (anatase) | ZnO | Fe₂O₃ | CdS | g-C₃N₄ |
|----------|----------------|-----|--------|-----|---------|
| Band gap (eV) | 3.2 | 3.2 | 2.1 | 2.4 | 2.7 |
| Active region | UV only | UV only | Visible | Visible | Visible |
| VB position (V vs. NHE) | +2.7 | +2.9 | +2.5 | +1.8 | +1.6 |
| CB position (V vs. NHE) | −0.5 | −0.3 | +0.1 | −0.6 | −1.1 |
| Photocorrosion | Stable | Unstable in acid | Stable | Severe | Stable |
| Typical nanoparticle size | 5–50 nm | 10–100 nm | 20–80 nm | 2–20 nm | 5–100 nm |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Photocatalytic Reaction Cycle on a Semiconductor Nanoparticle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["LIGHT SOURCE (h nu greater than Eg)"] --> B["PHOTON ABSORPTION BY SEMICONDUCTOR NP"]
    B --> C["ELECTRON HOLE PAIR GENERATION (e CB minus, h VB plus)"]
    C --> D{"CHARGE CARRIER MIGRATION TO SURFACE"}
    D -- "Success" --> E["SURFACE ADSORPTION OF O2 AND H2O"]
    D -- "Loss" --> F["RECOMBINATION (heat or light emission)"]
    E --> G["REDUCTION: O2 plus e minus yields SUPEROXIDE O2 minus"]
    E --> H["OXIDATION: H2O plus h plus yields HYDROXYL RADICAL OH"]
    G --> I["ORGANIC POLLUTANT DEGRADATION"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["MINERALIZATION PRODUCTS: CO2 plus H2O plus mineral acids"]
    F --> K["EFFICIENCY LOSS"]
    J --> L["CATALYST REGENERATED READY FOR NEXT CYCLE"]
    K --> L
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Architecture of a Photocatalytic Reactor System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_BLOCK["INPUT SUBSYSTEM"]
        A1["Pollutant Feed Tank"]
        A2["Oxygen or Air Supply"]
        A3["Light Source UV or Solar Simulator"]
    end

    subgraph REACTOR_BLOCK["PHOTOCATALYTIC REACTOR CORE"]
        B1["Immobilized TiO2 or ZnO Nanoparticle Coating"]
        B2["Stirring or Recirculation Pump"]
        B3["Temperature and pH Sensor Array"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_BLOCK["OUTPUT SUBSYSTEM"]
        C1["Treated Effluent Collection"]
        C2["Sampling Port for UV-Vis Spectroscopy"]
        C3["Online TOC Analyzer"]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL_BLOCK["CONTROL AND MONITORING"]
        D1["Microcontroller PLC"]
        D2["Data Acquisition and Logging"]
        D3["Safety Interlocks"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    B1 --> C2
    B1 --> C3
    B3 --> D1
    B1 --> D2
    D1 --> B2
    D1 --> D3
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Strategies to Enhance Photocatalytic Efficiency

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["PHOTOCATALYSIS EFFICIENCY ENHANCEMENT ROUTES"]

    ROOT --> P1["BAND GAP ENGINEERING"]
    P1 --> P1A["Metal Doping Fe, Cu, Ag"]
    P1 --> P1B["Non-metal Doping N, C, S"]
    P1 --> P1C["Anion Doping C, N, S"]

    ROOT --> P2["NANOSTRUCTURING"]
    P2 --> P2A["Quantum Dots Zero D"]
    P2 --> P2B["One D Nanorods and Nanotubes"]
    P2 --> P2C["Two D Nanosheets"]
    P2 --> P2D["Three D Mesoporous Networks"]

    ROOT --> P3["HETEROJUNCTION FORMATION"]
    P3 --> P3A["Type II Staggered Junction"]
    P3 --> P3B["Z Scheme Junction"]
    P3 --> P3C["Schottky Junction with Noble Metal"]
    P3 --> P3D["Graphene or MoS2 Composite"]

    ROOT --> P4["SENSITIZATION"]
    P4 --> P4A["Dye Sensitization"]
    P4 --> P4B["Quantum Dot Sensitization"]
    P4 --> P4C["Plasmonic Au or Ag Nanoparticle Coupling"]

    ROOT --> P5["OPERATIONAL CONTROLS"]
    P5 --> P5A["pH Optimization"]
    P5 --> P5B["Temperature Optimization 20 to 60 C"]
    P5 --> P5C["Catalyst Loading Optimization"]
    P5 --> P5D["Electron Scavenger Addition H2O2 or S2O8 2 minus"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Major Application Domains of Photocatalysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CORE["NANOSCALE PHOTOCATALYSIS"]
    CORE --> APP1["Environmental Remediation"]
    APP1 --> APP1A["Water Treatment Dye and Pesticide Removal"]
    APP1 --> APP1B["Air Purification NOx and VOC Oxidation"]
    APP1 --> APP1C["Soil Detoxification"]

    CORE --> APP2["Energy Generation"]
    APP2 --> APP2A["Photocatalytic Water Splitting H2 Production"]
    APP2 --> APP2B["CO2 Photoreduction to Solar Fuels"]
    APP2 --> APP2C["Dye Sensitized Solar Cells DSSCs"]

    CORE --> APP3["Self Cleaning Surfaces"]
    APP3 --> APP3A["Building Facades and Exterior Glass"]
    APP3 --> APP3B["Anti fog Bathroom Mirrors"]
    APP3 --> APP3C["Anti staining Textiles"]

    CORE --> APP4["Biomedical"]
    APP4 --> APP4A["Antibacterial Coatings TiO2 on Implants"]
    APP4 --> APP4B["Photodynamic Therapy Cancer Treatment"]
    APP4 --> APP4C["Antiviral Surfaces SARS CoV 2 Inactivation"]

    CORE --> APP5["Industrial Synthesis"]
    APP5 --> APP5A["Selective Organic Transformations"]
    APP5 --> APP5B["Polymerization Initiation"]
    APP5 --> APP5C["Green Chemistry Pathways"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define photocatalysis. Explain with a neat sketch the mechanism of semiconductor photocatalysis involving electron–hole pair generation, migration, and surface redox reactions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Photocatalysis is the acceleration of a chemical reaction in the presence of a light-activated catalyst (photocatalyst) that is not consumed in the overall reaction. In semiconductor photocatalysis, when a semiconductor nanoparticle (e.g., TiO₂) absorbs a photon of energy hν ≥ E_g, an electron is excited from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), leaving behind a hole in the VB. This electron–hole pair (e⁻/h⁺) migrates to the surface where the electron reduces adsorbed O₂ to ·O₂⁻ and the hole oxidizes H₂O/OH⁻ to ·OH. These reactive oxygen species mineralize organic pollutants to CO₂ and H₂O. **(3 Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for mechanism explanation with labels)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Why are nanoparticles better photocatalysts than bulk semiconductors? List any three reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Higher surface-area-to-volume ratio** in nanoparticles provides a greater number of surface active sites per unit mass for adsorption and redox reactions. **(1 Mark)**
2. **Shorter migration distance** for photogenerated charge carriers from the bulk to the surface, which reduces the probability of electron–hole recombination. **(1 Mark)**
3. **Quantum confinement effects** in nanoparticles smaller than the exciton Bohr radius allow band gap tuning, enabling harvesting of visible light in materials like TiO₂ that normally absorb only UV. **(1 Mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Photocatalytic Mechanism and Kinetics**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the mechanism of heterogeneous semiconductor photocatalysis with a band-structure diagram showing electron–hole pair generation, migration, and recombination. Compare the conduction and valence band edge positions of TiO₂, ZnO, and CdS with respect to water reduction and oxidation potentials.

**Model Solution:**

**Mechanism of Heterogeneous Photocatalysis (5 Marks):**

The process involves five fundamental steps:

**Step 1 — Photon Absorption:** A photon of energy hν ≥ E_g is absorbed by the semiconductor nanoparticle, exciting a valence-band electron to the conduction band.

**Step 2 — Electron–Hole Pair Generation:** This creates a conduction-band electron (e⁻_CB) and a valence-band hole (h⁺_VB).

$$Semiconductor + h\nu \rightarrow e^-_{CB} + h^+_{VB}$$

**Step 3 — Charge Migration:** The e⁻ and h⁺ diffuse to the catalyst surface. Nanoparticles offer shorter diffusion paths, reducing recombination.

**Step 4 — Surface Redox Reactions:** At the surface, electrons reduce electron acceptors (typically O₂) and holes oxidize electron donors (typically H₂O or OH⁻).

$$O_2 + e^-_{CB} \rightarrow \cdot O_2^- \quad \text{(superoxide radical)}$$

$$H_2O + h^+}_{VB} \rightarrow \cdot OH + H^+ \quad \text{(hydroxyl radical)}$$

**Step 5 — Pollutant Degradation:** The ROS (·OH, ·O₂⁻) attack organic pollutants, mineralizing them to CO₂, H₂O, and mineral acids.

**Step 6 — Recombination (Competing):** A fraction of e⁻/h⁺ pairs recombine, releasing energy as heat or light — this is the primary efficiency loss.

**Band-Edge Position Comparison (2 Marks):**

| Semiconductor | E_g (eV) | CB (V vs. NHE) | VB (V vs. NHE) | Activity |
|---------------|----------|----------------|----------------|----------|
| TiO₂ (anatase) | 3.2 | −0.50 | +2.70 | UV-active, very stable |
| ZnO | 3.2 | −0.31 | +2.89 | UV-active, photocorrodes in acid |
| CdS | 2.4 | −0.60 | +1.80 | Visible-active, severe photocorrosion |

For overall water splitting: CB must lie above H⁺/H₂ (0 V) and VB must lie below O₂/H₂O (+1.23 V). **All three materials satisfy thermodynamic requirements**, but only CdS operates under visible light.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a photocatalytic experiment, methylene blue dye (initial concentration 50 mg/L) is degraded using TiO₂ nanoparticles (anatase, E_g = 3.2 eV) under UV irradiation. The concentration decreases to 5 mg/L in 90 minutes. Assuming pseudo-first-order kinetics:
**(i)** Calculate the rate constant k.
**(ii)** Determine the time required for 98% degradation.
**(iii)** Calculate the threshold wavelength of TiO₂ and identify the spectral region.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Rate Constant k (2 Marks):**

The pseudo-first-order integrated rate law is:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C}\right) = kt$$

Substituting C₀ = 50 mg/L, C = 5 mg/L, t = 90 min:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{50}{5}\right) = k \times 90$$

$$\ln(10) = 2.3026 = 90k$$

$$k = \frac{2.3026}{90} = 0.02559 \text{ min}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Time for 98% Degradation (2 Marks):**

For 98% degradation, C = 0.02 × C₀:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{0.02}\right) = kt_{98}$$

$$\ln(50) = 3.9120 = 0.02559 \times t_{98}$$

$$t_{98} = \frac{3.9120}{0.02559} = 152.9 \text{ min} \approx 2 \text{ h } 33 \text{ min}$$

**(iii) Threshold Wavelength and Spectral Region (3 Marks):**

Using the band gap formula:

$$\lambda_{\text{threshold}} = \frac{1240}{E_g} = \frac{1240}{3.2} = 387.5 \text{ nm}$$

Since 387.5 nm < 400 nm, this falls in the **UV-A region (315–400 nm)** of the electromagnetic spectrum. Therefore, undoped anatase TiO₂ can only be activated by UV light, which constitutes only ~4–5% of the solar spectrum — a key limitation driving research into visible-light-active photocatalysts.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Nanostructuring, Applications, and Modifications**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss with examples how nanostructuring enhances the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO₂. Compare 0D, 1D, 2D, and 3D TiO₂ nanostructures in terms of surface area, charge transport, and light-harvesting ability.

**Model Solution:**

**Why Nanostructuring Enhances Photocatalysis (2 Marks):**

Bulk TiO₂ (anatase) suffers from low surface area, rapid e⁻/h⁺ recombination, and can only absorb UV light. Nanostructuring addresses these limitations through:

1. **Increased surface-area-to-volume ratio** → more active sites
2. **Reduced charge-carrier migration distance** → less recombination
3. **Quantum confinement** → tunable band gap
4. **Enhanced light scattering and absorption** → better photon harvesting

**Comparison of TiO₂ Nanostructures (5 Marks):**

| Dimensionality | Example | Surface Area | Charge Transport | Light Harvesting |
|----------------|---------|--------------|------------------|------------------|
| **0D (Quantum Dots)** | TiO₂ QDs (~3–5 nm) | Very high | Poor (many grain boundaries) | Excellent (tunable band gap via QC) |
| **1D (Nanorods/Nanotubes)** | TiO₂ nanotubes (NTs) | High | **Excellent** (direct pathway along axis) | Good (light trapping in tubular geometry) |
| **2D (Nanosheets)** | Exfoliated TiO₂ nanosheets with exposed {001} facets | Very high (two exposed basal planes) | Moderate (in-plane transport) | Excellent (thinness ensures short migration distance) |
| **3D (Mesoporous/Forest)** | TiO₂ nanoflowers, inverse opals | High with interconnected pores | Moderate (network junctions) | Excellent (multiple scattering in hierarchical pores) |

**Best Performer:** 1D TiO₂ nanotubes are generally considered the most balanced architecture for photocatalysis because they provide direct, low-resistance electron transport along the tube axis while maintaining a high surface area and a short radial diffusion distance (~10–15 nm wall thickness) for holes to reach the surface. They are widely used in **dye-sensitized solar cells and water-splitting photoanodes**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe any three major applications of semiconductor photocatalysis in environmental remediation and energy. For each application, name the target reaction and explain the role of the photocatalyst.

**Model Solution:**

**Application 1 — Water Treatment via Dye Degradation (2.5 Marks):**

**Target Reaction:** Mineralization of organic dyes (methylene blue, rhodamine B, methyl orange) released from textile industries.

**Role of Photocatalyst:** TiO₂ (Degussa P25) nanoparticles, when illuminated with UV light, generate ·OH radicals that non-selectively oxidize the chromophoric groups of dye molecules, breaking them into CO₂, H₂O, NO₃⁻, and SO₄²⁻.

$$\text{Dye} + \cdot OH \rightarrow \text{intermediates} \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2O + \text{mineral acids}$$

**Significance:** This is a "green" alternative to chlorine-based water treatment, which generates carcinogenic chlorinated byproducts.

**Application 2 — Photocatalytic Hydrogen Generation (2.5 Marks):**

**Target Reaction:** Overall water splitting into H₂ and O₂ using sunlight.

**Role of Photocatalyst:** Modified TiO₂ (e.g., Pt-loaded, N-doped) or visible-light photocatalysts (CdS, g-C₃N₄) absorb sunlight and use the photogenerated electrons to reduce water at the CB while holes oxidize water at the VB.

$$2H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{photocatalyst}, h\nu} 2H_2 + O_2$$

**Significance:** Produces **green hydrogen** — a zero-carbon fuel. Coupled with solar energy, this offers a sustainable alternative to steam-methane reforming (which emits 10 tons of CO₂ per ton of H₂ produced).

**Application 3 — Self-Cleaning and Antibacterial Surfaces (2 Marks):**

**Target Reaction:** Decomposition of organic dirt, oils, and bacterial cell walls on coated surfaces.

**Role of Photocatalyst:** A thin TiO₂ film (~50–100 nm) is coated on glass, tiles, or medical devices. Under sunlight or indoor UV, the film generates ROS that oxidize organic contaminants and rupture bacterial membranes (E. coli, S. aureus). The surface becomes hydrophilic (water contact angle → 0°) via photo-induced surface restructuring, allowing rainwater to sheet off and wash away loosened dirt.

**Significance:** Reduces maintenance cost of buildings, hospital-acquired infections, and airborne disease transmission — a major post-COVID application area.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Photocatalysis Questions:**
>
> 1. **Band gap unit confusion:** Students frequently mix eV and J. KTU examiners award **zero marks** if units are inconsistent. Always state "E_g = 3.2 eV = 5.13 × 10⁻¹⁹ J" explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing pseudo-first-order with zero-order kinetics:** Photocatalytic dye degradation is **first-order in dye concentration at low concentrations (<10⁻³ M)**. Do not write the zero-order equation [C = C₀ − kt] unless explicitly told otherwise.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in hydrogen quantum yield:** Producing 1 mole of H₂ requires **2 electrons → 2 photons**. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if this is missed.
> 4. **Misidentifying spectral regions:** UV-A: 315–400 nm, UV-B: 280–315 nm, UV-C: 100–280 nm, Visible: 400–700 nm. TiO₂ (E_g ≈ 3.2 eV) is UV-A active, NOT visible.
> 5. **Skipping the band-edge diagram:** Even a rough sketch of VB and CB positions with the H⁺/H₂ and O₂/H₂O redox levels fetches **2 full marks** in 14-mark questions. KTU examiners have a strong bias for labelled diagrams.
> 6. **Writing "TiO₂ is visible-light active":** Pure anatase TiO₂ is a **UV-only** photocatalyst. Only doped or heterojunction TiO₂ extends into the visible region. This single factual error can cost 2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Photocatalysis**
>
> ✅ **Core Definition:** Photocatalysis = light + catalyst (not consumed) accelerates a reaction.
>
> ✅ **Active Materials:** TiO₂ (anatase), ZnO, Fe₂O₃, CdS, g-C₃N₄, BiVO₄, WO₃.
>
> ✅ **Band Gap Threshold Condition:** Photon energy hν ≥ E_g for activation.
>
> ✅ **Master Formula:** λ_threshold (nm) = 1240 / E_g (eV).
>
> ✅ **Six-Step Mechanism:** Absorption → e⁻/h⁺ generation → migration → surface adsorption → redox reactions → recombination (loss).
>
> ✅ **Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS):** ·OH, ·O₂⁻, H₂O₂ — these are the actual chemical agents.
>
> ✅ **Kinetics:** Pseudo-first-order: ln(C₀/C) = kt (most common in KTU problems).
>
> ✅ **Langmuir–Hinshelwood:** r = kKC / (1 + KC); used for high-concentration or surface-saturated reactions.
>
> ✅ **Why Nano:** Higher surface area + shorter charge migration + quantum confinement = superior photocatalysis.
>
> ✅ **Band Edge Requirements:** For water splitting, CB < 0 V and VB > +1.23 V vs. NHE (at pH 0).
>
> ✅ **Enhancement Strategies:** Doping, heterojunctions, dye sensitization, plasmonic coupling, nanostructuring, cocatalyst loading (Pt, Au).
>
> ✅ **Major Applications:** Dye degradation, water splitting (H₂), CO₂ reduction, self-cleaning glass, antibacterial coatings, indoor air purification.
>
> ✅ **Key Limitation:** TiO₂ absorbs only ~4–5% of solar spectrum → drives research into visible-light-active materials.
>
> ✅ **Photo-corrosion:** A serious issue for CdS, ZnO — electrons/holes oxidize the catalyst itself, limiting reusability. TiO₂ is photochemically stable.
>
> ✅ **Remember:** AQY > 1 is possible for chain reactions (like H₂ evolution); quantum yield φ ≤ 1 for single-step events.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Purification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET868 - NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Water Purification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Water Purification** in the nanotechnology context refers to the application of nanoscale materials, structures, and processes to remove physical, chemical, and biological contaminants from water, producing potable (drinkable) or industrial-grade water. Nanotechnology enables purification through mechanisms such as adsorption, membrane filtration, photocatalytic degradation, disinfection, and desalination at efficiencies far superior to conventional macro-scale methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Water purification is a flagship application of nanotechnology. The 2024 Scheme PEMET868 Module 4 emphasizes *nanomaterials* (CNTs, TiO₂, graphene oxide, zeolites, magnetic nanoparticles), *nanofiltration membranes*, *photocatalysis*, and *desalination* as core exam-relevant topics.

The water contaminants targeted by nanotechnology fall into four categories:
1. **Pathogens** — bacteria (*E. coli*, *Vibrio cholerae*), viruses (rotavirus, norovirus), protozoa (*Giardia*, *Cryptosporidium*).
2. **Heavy metals** — arsenic (As), lead (Pb), mercury (Hg), cadmium (Cd), chromium (Cr).
3. **Organic pollutants** — pesticides, pharmaceutical residues, dyes, endocrine disruptors, VOCs.
4. **Inorganic salts** — NaCl, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, sulfate, nitrate (relevant for desalination).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Smart Sieve":**
> Imagine a kitchen sieve catching pasta but letting water drain — that's conventional filtration at the *millimeter* scale. Now shrink every hole in that sieve down to the size of a *virus* or an *ion*. The sieve can now trap things 100,000× smaller than the naked eye can see. But nanotechnology goes one step further: it doesn't just *trap* contaminants, it can **destroy them** with light (photocatalysis), **snatch them** with magnetic grip (magnetic nanoparticles), or **burn them** into harmless gases (advanced oxidation).

**Key Standard Metrics** (highlighted in **bold**):
- **Pore size**: measured in **nanometers (nm)**; ultrafiltration ≈ 1–100 nm, nanofiltration ≈ 0.5–5 nm, reverse osmosis ≈ 0.1–1 nm.
- **WHO permissible limit for arsenic**: **10 µg/L (10 ppb)**.
- **WHO permissible limit for lead**: **10 µg/L**.
- **Clean Water Act turbidity standard**: **< 1 NTU**.
- **Log reduction value (LRV)**: $\text{LRV} = \log_{10}(N_0/N_f)$, where $N_0$ is initial and $N_f$ is final microbial count.
- **Flux (J)**: water throughput per unit area, typically **L/(m²·h)** or **LMH**.

### 1.3 Why Nanotechnology for Water?

Conventional water treatment plants use coagulation, sedimentation, chlorination, and sand filtration. These methods:
- Generate toxic **disinfection by-products (DBPs)** like trihalomethanes.
- Cannot remove **nanoplastics**, **pharmaceutical traces**, or **heavy metals** below regulatory limits.
- Consume **high energy** and produce **large sludge volumes**.

Nanomaterials circumvent these limitations due to their **high surface-area-to-volume ratio**, **tunable surface chemistry**, and **quantum-scale reactivity**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Core Nanomaterials for Water Purification

The module groups purification technologies into **five pillars**. Each is analyzed below.

#### 2.1.1 Carbon-Based Nanomaterials (CNT, Graphene, GO)

- **Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs)** — hollow cylindrical tubes of sp² carbon. Internal diameter 1–100 nm, length up to microns. Used as **adsorbents** for heavy metals (Pb²⁺, Cd²⁺, As³⁺/⁵⁺) and organic dyes. Functionalization with –COOH, –OH, –NH₂ groups enhances hydrophilicity and binding.
- **Graphene Oxide (GO)** — single-atom-thick carbon sheet decorated with epoxide, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups. Forms stable aqueous dispersions and exhibits exceptional dye and metal adsorption.
- **Mechanism**: π–π stacking, electrostatic attraction, hydrogen bonding, and ion-exchange.

#### 2.1.2 TiO₂ Nanoparticles (Photocatalysis)

- Anatase TiO₂ has a bandgap of **≈ 3.2 eV**, making it photoactive under **UV light (λ < 385 nm)**.
- Under UV irradiation, TiO₂ generates electron–hole pairs: $e^-_{CB}$ and $h^+_{VB}$.
- These species react with water and oxygen to form **hydroxyl radicals (·OH)** and **superoxide radicals (O₂·⁻)**, which mineralize organic pollutants into CO₂ + H₂O.

#### 2.1.3 Nano-Membranes (Nanofiltration, Reverse Osmosis)

- **Polymeric NF/RO membranes** with embedded nanofillers (zeolites, TiO₂, GO, MOFs).
- Operate on **size exclusion**, **Donnan exclusion** (charge-based), and **solution-diffusion** mechanisms.
- Pore sizes are precisely in the **0.1–10 nm** range.

#### 2.1.4 Magnetic Nanoparticles (Fe₃O₄, γ-Fe₂O₃)

- Surface-functionalized (e.g., with chitosan, EDTA, humic acid) magnetite nanoparticles.
- After binding contaminants, the particles are recovered using an **external magnetic field**.
- Enables **recycling**, **no filtration needed**, and **rapid separation**.

#### 2.1.5 Nano-Silver (Ag NPs) and Nano-Copper for Disinfection

- Release Ag⁺ ions that disrupt bacterial **cell membranes**, denature **DNA**, and generate **reactive oxygen species (ROS)**.
- Highly effective against antibiotic-resistant bacteria (e.g., *MRSA*).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Constants |
|---|-------------------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $E_g = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Bandgap–wavelength relation for photocatalyst activation | $E_g$ in eV, $\lambda$ in nm; $h = 4.136 \times 10^{-15}$ eV·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 2 | $\text{LRV} = \log_{10}(N_0 / N_f)$ | Log Reduction Value for microbial inactivation | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $J = \dfrac{V}{A \cdot \Delta t}$ | Membrane flux (water throughput) | $\text{LMH} = \text{L}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{h})$ |
| 4 | $R = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}\right) \times 100\%$ | Membrane rejection % | % |
| 5 | $q_e = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e) V}{m}$ | Adsorption capacity at equilibrium | mg/g |
| 6 | $\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{K_L q_{\max}} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_{\max}}$ | Langmuir isotherm linear form | $K_L$ = L/mg, $q_{\max}$ = mg/g |
| 7 | $\ln q_e = \ln K_F + \dfrac{1}{n} \ln C_e$ | Freundlich isotherm linear form | $K_F$, $n$ dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{SSA} = \dfrac{6}{\rho \cdot d}$ | Specific surface area of spherical NPs | $\rho$ = density, $d$ = diameter |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{photocat}} = \dfrac{C_0 - C_t}{C_0} \times 100\%$ | Photocatalytic degradation efficiency | % |
| 10 | $\Delta \Pi = i M R_g T$ | Osmotic pressure (van't Hoff) | Pa; $R_g = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) |
| 11 | $\text{BET:} \;\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{C-1}{V_m C}\dfrac{P}{P_0}$ | BET surface area equation | — |
| 12 | $\zeta$-potential | Zeta potential governs colloidal stability | mV |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are exam staples for KTU PEMET868. The Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms are tested almost every cycle for **adsorption-based water treatment problems**.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **AquaSure / Desalitech**: RO plants incorporating graphene-oxide membranes in Israel and Singapore.
- **Magnetic nanobead water filters**: deployed in Bangladesh for **arsenic remediation** (As concentrations often exceed 500 µg/L).
- **Solar TiO₂ reactors**: used in rural India for dye and pesticide degradation from textile effluent.
- **CNT/polymer composite filters**: commercialized in **Lenntech** and **Nanostone** water filtration cartridges.
- **NASA water recovery systems**: use **Ag-impregnated activated carbon** for spacecraft potable water.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Threshold Wavelength for TiO₂ Photocatalyst Activation

Given the bandgap of anatase TiO₂ is $E_g = 3.2$ eV, derive the **maximum wavelength** of light that can activate the photocatalyst.

$$E_g = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

Solving for $\lambda$:

$$\lambda = \frac{hc}{E_g}$$

Substituting numerical values ($h = 4.136 \times 10^{-15}$ eV·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s):

$$\lambda = \frac{(4.136 \times 10^{-15})(3 \times 10^8)}{3.2}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{1.2408 \times 10^{-6}}{3.2} \;\text{m}$$

$$\lambda \approx 3.878 \times 10^{-7} \;\text{m} = 387.8 \;\text{nm}$$

**Interpretation:** Only UV light with wavelength $\lambda \leq 387.8$ nm can generate electron–hole pairs in pristine TiO₂. This explains why **visible-light-active doped TiO₂** (N-doped, Fe-doped, CdS-coupled) is a major research focus.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Specific Surface Area of Spherical TiO₂ Nanoparticles

For spherical nanoparticles of diameter $d$ and density $\rho$, the specific surface area is the surface area divided by mass.

$$\text{SSA} = \frac{\text{Surface area}}{\text{Mass}}$$

For a single sphere: $A = \pi d^2$ and $V_{\text{sphere}} = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{6}$.

$$\text{SSA} = \frac{\pi d^2}{\rho \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6}} = \frac{6}{\rho \cdot d}$$

**Numerical Example:** For $d = 25$ nm anatase TiO₂ with $\rho \approx 3.89$ g/cm³:

$$\text{SSA} = \frac{6}{(3.89 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{kg/cm}^3)(25 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{cm})}$$

Computing the denominator step-by-step:

$$\rho \cdot d = (3.89 \times 10^{-6})(25 \times 10^{-7}) = 97.25 \times 10^{-13} \;\text{kg/cm}^2$$

$$\text{SSA} = \frac{6}{97.25 \times 10^{-13}} = 6.17 \times 10^{10} \;\text{cm}^2/\text{kg}$$

Converting to m²/g:

$$\text{SSA} \approx 61.7 \;\text{m}^2/\text{g}$$

**Interpretation:** A single gram of 25 nm TiO₂ has the equivalent surface area of roughly **two tennis courts** — this is why nanoparticles are exceptional adsorbents and catalysts.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm — Maximum Capacity $q_{\max}$

The Langmuir isotherm assumes monolayer adsorption on a homogeneous surface. Its linear form is:

$$\frac{C_e}{q_e} = \frac{1}{K_L \, q_{\max}} + \frac{C_e}{q_{\max}}$$

A plot of $\dfrac{C_e}{q_e}$ (y-axis) versus $C_e$ (x-axis) gives:
- **Slope** = $\dfrac{1}{q_{\max}}$
- **Intercept** = $\dfrac{1}{K_L \, q_{\max}}$

**Worked Numerical Example:** GO adsorbs Pb²⁺ from solution. Experimental data:

| $C_e$ (mg/L) | $q_e$ (mg/g) |
|---|---|
| 2 | 4.8 |
| 5 | 11.5 |
| 10 | 20.0 |
| 20 | 28.6 |

Compute $C_e / q_e$:

| $C_e$ | $q_e$ | $C_e / q_e$ |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | 4.8 | 0.4167 |
| 5 | 11.5 | 0.4348 |
| 10 | 20.0 | 0.5000 |
| 20 | 28.6 | 0.6993 |

Linear regression on $(C_e, \;C_e/q_e)$ yields:
- Slope $\approx 0.0156$ → $q_{\max} = 1 / 0.0156 \approx \mathbf{64.1 \;\text{mg/g}}$
- Intercept $\approx 0.40$ → $K_L = \dfrac{1}{q_{\max} \cdot \text{intercept}} = \dfrac{1}{64.1 \times 0.40} \approx \mathbf{0.039 \;\text{L/mg}}$

**Interpretation:** GO can adsorb up to 64.1 mg of lead per gram — orders of magnitude higher than activated carbon (~10–25 mg/g).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Nanomaterial by Contaminant

| Contaminant Class | Best-Fit Nanomaterial | Mechanism | Key Performance Metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heavy metals (Pb, Cd, As, Hg) | GO, functionalized CNTs, Fe₃O₄@chitosan | Chemisorption, electrostatic, ion-exchange | $q_{\max}$ up to 100 mg/g |
| Organic dyes (methylene blue, rhodamine B) | TiO₂ NPs, ZnO NRs, GO | Photocatalytic degradation + adsorption | > 95% in 60 min UV |
| Pathogens (*E. coli*, virus) | Ag NPs, CuO NPs, CNTs with antimicrobial functionalization | ROS + membrane disruption | LRV > 5 |
| Salts / Desalination | GO membranes, MOFs, zeolite-A nanofillers in PA-RO | Size exclusion, Donnan exclusion | Rejection > 99% NaCl |
| Nitrate, fluoride | MgO NPs, layered double hydroxides (LDHs) | Ion-exchange, precipitation | Defluoridation capacity ≈ 1000 mg F⁻/kg |
| Oil/water emulsion | Superhydrophobic CNT sponges, SiO₂ nanofiber mats | Wettability-based separation | Separation efficiency > 99% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** In KTU exam problems, you may be asked to "justify the choice of nanomaterial for a given contaminant." Use this matrix as a reference framework.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Nanotechnology Water Purification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawWater[Raw Water Intake] --> preScreen[Pre-Screening and Coagulation]
    preScreen --> nanoAdsorb[Nanomaterial Adsorption Stage]
    nanoAdsorb --> membStage[Nanofiltration or RO Membrane Stage]
    membStage --> photoStage[Photocatalytic TiO2 Reactor]
    photoStage --> magStage[Magnetic Nanoparticle Polishing]
    magStage --> disStage[Ag NP or UV Disinfection]
    disStage --> sensorStage[Real-time Nanosensor QC]
    sensorStage --> cleanOut[Clean Potable Water Output]
    nanoAdsorb -. Regeneration .-> regenUnit[Acid Wash / Thermal Regeneration]
    regenUnit -. Reuse .-> nanoAdsorb
    sensorStage -. Data .-> cloudLink[IoT Cloud Dashboard]
```

### 4.2 Schematic — TiO₂ Photocatalysis Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    uvLight[UV Photons h nu greater than 3.2 eV] --> tio2[TiO2 Nanoparticle Anatase]
    tio2 --> ecb[Electron in Conduction Band e CB]
    tio2 --> hvb[Hole in Valence Band h VB]
    ecb --> o2red[O2 plus e yields O2 minus superoxide]
    hvb --> ohform[H2O plus h yields OH plus H plus Hydroxyl Radical]
    o2red --> organics[Minearalization of Organic Pollutants]
    ohform --> organics
    organics --> co2h2o[Final Products CO2 plus H2O]
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow — Selecting Nanomaterial for Contaminant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Identify Target Contaminant] --> q1{Is the contaminant a heavy metal?}
    q1 -- Yes --> pathA[Use GO or functionalized CNT or Fe3O4 NP]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Is it an organic dye or VOC?}
    q2 -- Yes --> pathB[Use TiO2 or ZnO photocatalyst]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Is it salt water desalination?}
    q3 -- Yes --> pathC[Use GO membrane or zeolite RO]
    q3 -- No --> q4{Is it a pathogen or microbe?}
    q4 -- Yes --> pathD[Use Ag NPs or CuO NPs]
    q4 -- No --> pathE[Hybrid nano composite system]
    pathA --> ready[Process Optimization]
    pathB --> ready
    pathC --> ready
    pathD --> ready
    pathE --> ready
```

### 4.4 Process Block — Magnetic Nanoparticle Recovery

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    contWater[Contaminated Water] --> mix[Mix with Functionalized Fe3O4 NPs]
    mix --> bind[NPs bind heavy metals and organics]
    bind --> magField[Apply External Magnetic Field]
    magField --> sep[NP Contaminant Complex Separates]
    sep --> cleanWater[Clean Water Decanted]
    sep --> wash[Acid Wash Regenerates NPs]
    wash --> reuse[Reused for Next Batch]
    reuse --> mix
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The four diagrams above collectively represent the **block-level functional architecture** of a typical nanotechnology-enabled water treatment plant. They are designed to comply with Mermaid rendering safeguards: pure alphanumeric node IDs and clean unformatted labels.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**Q1. Define photocatalysis and explain how TiO₂ nanoparticles act as photocatalysts for water purification.**

**Model Answer:**
Photocatalysis is the acceleration of a chemical reaction by a catalyst activated by light. TiO₂ (anatase, $E_g \approx 3.2$ eV) absorbs UV photons ($\lambda \leq 387$ nm), generating electron-hole pairs. The holes ($h^+_{VB}$) react with water to form **hydroxyl radicals (·OH)**, while electrons ($e^-_{CB}$) reduce O₂ to **superoxide (O₂·⁻)**. Both radicals are powerful oxidizers that mineralize organic pollutants into CO₂ + H₂O. TiO₂'s high chemical stability, non-toxicity, and reusability make it ideal for water treatment. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. What is the Langmuir adsorption isotherm? Mention its significance in nano-adsorption studies.**

**Model Answer:**
The Langmuir isotherm describes monolayer adsorption on a homogeneous surface with a finite number of identical sites. Its linear form is:

$$\frac{C_e}{q_e} = \frac{1}{K_L \, q_{\max}} + \frac{C_e}{q_{\max}}$$

Significance: it provides the **maximum adsorption capacity** $q_{\max}$ (mg/g) and the **Langmuir constant** $K_L$, which characterizes binding affinity. For nanomaterial characterization, $q_{\max}$ is the single most cited metric for benchmarking GO, CNT, and Fe₃O₄ nano-adsorbents against activated carbon. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Describe with neat schematics the mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants using TiO₂ nanoparticles. Derive the threshold wavelength for anatase TiO₂ ($E_g = 3.2$ eV).

**Model Solution:**

*Mechanism (4 marks):*
1. **Photon absorption**: UV photon ($\lambda \leq 387.8$ nm) excites valence electron to conduction band.
2. **Charge separation**: electron in CB ($e^-_{CB}$), hole in VB ($h^+_{VB}$).
3. **Reactive species formation**:
$$\text{TiO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow e^-_{CB} + h^+_{VB}$$
$$h^+_{VB} + H_2O \rightarrow \cdot OH + H^+$$
$$e^-_{CB} + O_2 \rightarrow O_2^{-\bullet}$$
4. **Mineralization**: $\cdot OH$ and $O_2^{-\bullet}$ oxidize organic dyes, pesticides, and pharmaceuticals into CO₂ + H₂O + mineral acids.

*Derivation (3 marks):*

$$\lambda = \frac{hc}{E_g} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{3.2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{5.126 \times 10^{-19}} = 3.877 \times 10^{-7} \;\text{m} \approx 388 \;\text{nm}$$

Hence TiO₂ requires UV light; visible-light activity needs doping.

---

**(b) (7 Marks)** A graphene oxide (GO) adsorbent is tested for lead removal. Data: $C_0 = 50$ mg/L, $V = 200$ mL, $m = 0.05$ g, equilibrium $C_e = 18$ mg/L. Compute the equilibrium adsorption capacity $q_e$. Comment on whether this is favorable using Langmuir analysis given $q_{\max} = 76.9$ mg/g and $K_L = 0.04$ L/mg.

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Compute $q_e$:*

$$q_e = \frac{(C_0 - C_e) V}{m} = \frac{(50 - 18)(0.200)}{0.05} = \frac{32 \times 0.200}{0.05} = \frac{6.4}{0.05} = 128 \;\text{mg/g}$$

*Step 2 — Compute the dimensionless separation factor $R_L$*:

$$R_L = \frac{1}{1 + K_L \, C_0} = \frac{1}{1 + (0.04)(50)} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

*Step 3 — Interpret $R_L$ (Langmuir favorability):*
- $0 < R_L < 1$ → **favorable adsorption**.
- $R_L = 0$ → irreversible.
- $R_L = 1$ → linear.
- $R_L > 1$ → unfavorable.

Since $R_L = 0.333$, the adsorption is favorable.

*Step 4 — Compare with $q_{\max}$:*

The measured $q_e = 128$ mg/g **exceeds** the given $q_{\max} = 76.9$ mg/g, which is **physically inconsistent** with monolayer Langmuir assumption. This indicates possible multilayer adsorption, surface heterogeneity, or experimental error. *(Examiner expects students to flag this inconsistency.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Part B (a):** Many students write only "TiO₂ absorbs light" without deriving the threshold wavelength numerically. KTU examiners allocate 2–3 marks strictly to the **$\lambda = hc/E_g$ calculation**. Skip the math, lose marks.
>
> **Pitfall Warning — Part B (b):** Students often compute $q_e$ correctly but skip the **Langmuir $R_L$** interpretation. Always comment on **favorability** to earn the final 2 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the working of a graphene oxide (GO) membrane for desalination. Discuss size-exclusion and Donnan exclusion mechanisms. List two advantages and two disadvantages.

**Model Solution:**

*Working Principle (3 marks):*
A GO membrane is a stacked lamellar structure of graphene oxide sheets with **interlayer nanochannels** of 0.3–0.9 nm. Water molecules (kinetic diameter ≈ 0.26 nm) pass through these channels via a low-friction "frictionless" flow aided by the **hydrogen-bond network** with GO's oxygen functional groups. Hydrated ions (Na⁺ ≈ 0.72 nm, Cl⁻ ≈ 0.66 nm) are physically blocked.

*Mechanisms (3 marks):*
1. **Size exclusion** — Physical sieve effect: water molecules fit, hydrated ions do not.
2. **Donnan exclusion** — GO carries negative charge (–COO⁻, –O⁻); it repels anions (Cl⁻) and rejects co-ions; this enhances salt rejection beyond pure size effects.

*Advantages (0.5 × 2 = 1 mark):*
- Ultrahigh water flux (orders of magnitude higher than polymeric RO).
- Excellent antifouling when functionalized.

*Disadvantages (0.5 × 2 = 1 mark):*
- Swelling in water degrades selectivity over time.
- Difficult to scale up at low cost.

---

**(b) (7 Marks)** A nanofiltration membrane has a flux $J = 25$ LMH and rejects 98% of a 1000 mg/L NaCl feed. If membrane area is 5 m², how many liters of permeate are produced per day? If the rejection drops to 95% due to fouling, calculate the new permeate concentration $C_p$.

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Daily permeate volume:*

$$V_{\text{permeate}} = J \times A \times t = 25 \;\frac{L}{m^2 h} \times 5 \;m^2 \times 24 \;h = 3000 \;\text{L/day}$$

*Step 2 — Initial permeate concentration at 98% rejection:*

$$R = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f} \Rightarrow C_p = C_f (1 - R) = 1000 \times (1 - 0.98) = 20 \;\text{mg/L}$$

*Step 3 — New permeate concentration at 95% rejection:*

$$C_p^{\text{new}} = 1000 \times (1 - 0.95) = 50 \;\text{mg/L}$$

*Step 4 — Interpretation:*
Fouling causes permeate salinity to rise from **20 mg/L to 50 mg/L** — a 2.5× deterioration. WHO recommends < 250 mg/L TDS for palatability, so even fouled permeate remains acceptable, but **membrane cleaning is essential** to maintain rejection.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Question B (b):** A common error is forgetting to multiply by the **24-hour** time factor. Students often write $J \times A$ and stop, missing 1 mark. Also, **always** express the final $C_p$ in the same units as $C_f$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Water Purification (Module 4)**

- ✅ **Five pillars of nano-water purification**: (1) Adsorption, (2) Membrane filtration, (3) Photocatalysis, (4) Magnetic separation, (5) Antimicrobial NPs.
- ✅ **Bandgap of anatase TiO₂** = **3.2 eV** → activation wavelength ≈ **387.8 nm** (UV-A).
- ✅ **Langmuir equation** gives $q_{\max}$; **Freundlich** is for heterogeneous multilayer adsorption.
- ✅ **GO membranes** rely on **size exclusion + Donnan exclusion**; water (0.26 nm) passes; NaCl (hydrated 0.7 nm) rejected.
- ✅ **CNTs** functionalized with –COOH, –OH, –NH₂ are highly effective for **heavy metal** capture.
- ✅ **Fe₃O₄ magnetic NPs** are recovered using an **external magnet** → no filtration needed, recyclable.
- ✅ **Ag NPs** release Ag⁺ → disrupt microbial membranes + DNA → LRV up to 5–7 against *E. coli*.
- ✅ **Specific surface area of a 25 nm TiO₂ NP** ≈ **62 m²/g** — explains exceptional reactivity.
- ✅ **Flux** $J = V / (A \cdot t)$; **Rejection** $R = 1 - C_p/C_f$.
- ✅ **WHO arsenic limit** = **10 µg/L**; **WHO lead limit** = **10 µg/L**.
- ✅ **Key equations to memorize**: $E_g = hc/\lambda$, $\text{LRV} = \log_{10}(N_0/N_f)$, $\text{SSA} = 6/(\rho d)$.
- ✅ **Practical exam tip**: When asked to "select a nanomaterial," always tie your answer to the **contaminant class** and justify via **mechanism** (adsorption / photocatalysis / filtration).

---

> **End of Module 4 — Water Purification Notes**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme PEMET868 — Nanotechnology.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Desalination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET868 - NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Desalination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Desalination** is the engineered process of removing dissolved salts, minerals, and other ionic contaminants from saline water sources (seawater, brackish water, or industrial brine) to produce freshwater suitable for human consumption, agriculture, or industrial use.

In the context of **Nanotechnology (PEMET868, Module 4)**, desalination refers specifically to the deployment of **nanomaterials, nanostructures, and nanoscale phenomena** to enhance the efficiency, selectivity, energy economy, and antifouling performance of traditional desalination processes such as **Reverse Osmosis (RO)**, **Nanofiltration (NF)**, **Forward Osmosis (FO)**, **Membrane Distillation (MD)**, **Capacitive Deionization (CDI)**, and **Solar Thermal Distillation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMET868 Module 4):** Students must understand how nanoscale engineering transforms conventional water purification — particularly through nanofabricated membranes, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene oxide (GO) sheets, aquaporin biomimetic channels, and plasmonic nanoparticles for photothermal evaporation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Smart Sieve" Model

Imagine a **sandbox** filled with a mixture of:
- **Large marbles** (representing salt ions like Na⁺, Cl⁻)
- **Tiny grains of sand** (representing water molecules, H₂O)
- **Dust particles** (representing organic contaminants)

Now imagine a **magical sieve** whose pores are so precisely engineered — at the scale of **billionths of a meter** — that:
- Water molecules slip through **effortlessly** (like sand through fingers)
- Salt ions **cannot pass** (they are too large or are electrostatically repelled)
- The sieve **never gets clogged** (because the surface repels dirt and bacteria)

This **"smart sieve"** is exactly what a **nanomaterial-based desalination membrane** does. Unlike a traditional polymer membrane whose pores are randomly distributed, a **nanoengineered membrane** (made from aligned CNTs, graphene laminates, or biological aquaporins) has:
- **Uniform pore geometry**
- **Molecular-level precision**
- **Tunable surface chemistry** (hydrophilic, antifouling, antibacterial)

---

## 1.3 Why Nanotechnology Matters in Desalination

| Conventional Limitation | Nanotechnology-Enabled Solution |
|--------------------------|----------------------------------|
| High energy consumption (3–6 kWh/m³) | Nanochannels reduce friction → ultra-low energy RO |
| Membrane fouling by bacteria & organics | TiO₂, Ag, CuO nanocoatings provide **photocatalytic self-cleaning** |
| Trade-off between permeability & selectivity | CNT, GO, MOF membranes break the **upper bound limit** |
| Brine disposal concerns | Nanophotonic solar evaporators enable **zero-liquid-discharge** |
| Slow evaporation in solar stills | Plasmonic nanoparticles achieve **>90% solar-to-vapor efficiency** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Nanoscale Metric:** The dominant desalination transport channel operates in the **0.3 nm – 2 nm** range. This is the diameter window where water molecules (kinetic diameter ≈ **0.275 nm**) can permeate while hydrated ions (Na⁺ ≈ **0.716 nm**, Cl⁻ ≈ **0.664 nm**) are rejected.

---

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Kinetic diameter of H₂O molecule:** **0.275 nm**
- **Hydrated radius of Na⁺:** **0.358 nm**
- **Hydrated radius of Cl⁻:** **0.332 nm**
- **Standard seawater salinity:** **35,000 ppm (3.5 wt%)**
- **WHO drinking water salinity limit:** **500 ppm (TDS)**
- **Salt rejection threshold:** **>99%** (for potable water)
- **World seawater volume:** **≈ 1.332 billion km³**
- **Theoretical minimum energy for seawater desalination:** **≈ 1.06 kWh/m³**
- **Typical commercial RO energy use:** **3–6 kWh/m³**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Nanomaterial Desalination

The theoretical foundation rests on three pillars, each addressing a distinct mechanism:

### Pillar 1: Size Exclusion (Steric Hindrance)
- Pores of **molecular dimensions (< 2 nm)** physically block hydrated ions.
- The pore diameter **d_p** is engineered to satisfy: $d_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} < d_p < d_{\text{ion}}$
- Engineered materials: **CNT arrays, GO laminates, MOFs (e.g., ZIF-8), COFs**

### Pillar 2: Frictionless Water Transport
- Water inside smooth, hydrophobic CNTs experiences a **slip length** $\lambda$ of tens of microns.
- Conventional Hagen-Poiseuille flow is replaced by a high-slip regime where:

$$v_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{8\mu L} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{4\lambda}{R}\right)$$

- For CNTs where $\lambda \gg R$, the flow rate increases **3–4 orders of magnitude** over no-slip predictions.

### Pillar 3: Surface Charge & Donnan Exclusion
- Nanomembrane surfaces are functionalized with carboxyl (–COO⁻), sulfonate (–SO₃⁻), or amine (–NH₃⁺) groups.
- Cations (Na⁺, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺) and anions (Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻) are electrostatically rejected.
- Effective rejection follows **Donnan equilibrium**:

$$C_{\text{permeate}} = C_{\text{feed}} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{zF\psi}{RT}\right)$$

where $\psi$ is the membrane surface potential.

---

## 2.2 The Nanophotonic (Solar) Desalination Mechanism

In plasmonic solar desalination, metal nanoparticles (Au, Ag, Al) absorb sunlight and convert it into **localized heat** via **localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR)**:

1. **Photon absorption** → nanoparticle electron cloud oscillation
2. **Non-radiative Landau damping** → hot electrons
3. **Electron-phonon coupling** → lattice heating (within picoseconds)
4. **Heat transfer to surrounding water** → interfacial evaporation
5. **Vapor collected/condensed** → pure freshwater

The **Solar-to-Vapor Efficiency** $\eta$ is defined as:

$$\eta = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot h_{\text{lv}}}{C_{\text{opt}} \cdot P_{\text{in}}}$$

where $\dot{m}$ is the mass evaporation rate, $h_{\text{lv}}$ is the latent heat of vaporization (≈ **2260 kJ/kg**), $C_{\text{opt}}$ is the optical concentration, and $P_{\text{in}}$ is the incident solar power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modern nanostructured evaporators achieve $\eta$ exceeding **90%** under one-sun illumination (1 kW/m²), compared to ~40% for bulk water heating — because heat is confined to the air-water interface, eliminating bulk thermal losses.

---

## 2.3 Capacitive Deionization (CDI) with Nanostructured Electrodes

CDI removes ions by **electrosorption** onto porous, high-surface-area electrodes:

- Conventional activated carbon electrodes: specific surface area ≈ **500–1500 m²/g**
- **Nanostructured electrodes** (CNT forests, graphene aerogels, MXene films): SSA ≈ **1500–3500 m²/g**

The **Salt Adsorption Capacity (SAC)** in mg/g is given by:

$$\text{SAC} = \frac{(C_0 - C_{\text{eff}}) \cdot V}{m_{\text{electrode}}}$$
